building lisa

1. exploring cj 2. building lisa: bytecode vm 3. tail call optimization 4. whole-function jit 5. jit rewrite: register cache + inline fast paths 6. fibers and channels 7. string primitives + json parser 8. def as local + bug fixes + docs + gc fixes

building lisa

a conversation log of building a programming language with a jit compiler.

lisa is a lisp with closures, tail calls, fibers, channels, and a jit backend, built on top of cj, a minimal jit framework for c.

8 sessions over 4 days. all code was written by claude.

1. exploring cj 2. building lisa: bytecode vm 3. tail call optimization 4. whole-function jit 5. jit rewrite: register cache + inline fast paths 6. fibers and channels 7. string primitives + json parser 8. def as local + bug fixes + docs + gc fixes

session 1. exploring cj

human

We’re in the repository of a minimal JIT for C. Read it and learn about it deeply. Take your time.

⚙ Bash, Glob ×2, Read ×3

I'll explore the repository thoroughly. Let me start by understanding the structure and then dive into the key files.

Read — cj/README.md
{
  "file_path": "cj/README.md"
}
result
1→# cj
     2→
     3→is a small JIT framework written in C, with x86 and arm64 backends.
     4→
     5→## what
     6→
     7→- **multi-architecture support**: generates native code for:
     8→  - **x86-64**
     9→  - **ARM64** (except for 26 SIMD different-size instructions)
    10→- **low-level API**: direct instruction emission, no high level constructs
    11→- **no dependencies**: pure C with clib, nothing else
    12→
    13→## how
    14→
    15→the backends are autogenerated (check out `codegen`). x86 uses
    16→[asmdb](https://www.npmjs.com/package/asmdb) as a data source, arm64 uses a
    17→handgenerated file from [mra\_tools](https://github.com/alastairreid/mra_tools).
    18→register definitions are hand-crafted.
    19→
    20→the rest is handwritten and basically trivial in the grand scheme of jit
    21→compilation. examples, tests, and codegen docs contain some llm-generation, so
    22→peruse at your own peril.
    23→
    24→## why
    25→
    26→because i wanted to understand the isa for both processor architectures and it
    27→seemed like a fun project.
    28→
    29→## quick start
    30→
    31→### build
    32→
    33→```bash
    34→# dev build
    35→make dev
    36→
    37→# "prod" build
    38→make all
    39→
    40→# install (don't)
    41→make install
    42→```
    43→
    44→### basic usage
    45→
    46→```c
    47→#include "ctx.h"
    48→#include "op.h"
    49→
    50→int main(void) {
    51→  // Create JIT context
    52→  cj_ctx* cj = create_cj_ctx();
    53→
    54→  // Emit instructions
    55→  cj_nop(cj);  // NOP
    56→  cj_ret(cj);  // RET
    57→
    58→  // Create executable function
    59→  cj_fn f = create_cj_fn(cj);
    60→
    61→  // Execute JIT-compiled code!
    62→  f();
    63→
    64→  // Cleanup
    65→  destroy_cj_fn(cj, f);
    66→  destroy_cj_ctx(cj);
    67→
    68→  return 0;
    69→}
    70→```
    71→
    72→you can find some more examples in the `examples` directory.
    73→
    74→### builder helpers
    75→
    76→For reusable building blocks, the optional `builder` helpers provide prologue/epilogue setup and structured loops:
    77→
    78→```c
    79→#include <stdio.h>
    80→#include "builder.h"
    81→
    82→typedef int (*sum_fn)(int);
    83→
    84→int main(void) {
    85→  cj_ctx* cj = create_cj_ctx();
    86→  cj_builder_frame frame;
    87→  cj_builder_fn_prologue(cj, 0, &frame);
    88→
    89→  cj_operand n = cj_builder_arg_int(cj, 0);
    90→  cj_operand sum = cj_builder_scratch_reg(0);
    91→  cj_operand i = cj_builder_scratch_reg(1);
    92→  cj_operand one = cj_make_constant(1);
    93→
    94→  cj_builder_assign(cj, sum, cj_builder_zero_operand());
    95→
    96→  cj_builder_for_loop loop = cj_builder_for_begin(cj, i, one, n, one, CJ_COND_GE);
    97→  cj_builder_add_assign(cj, sum, i);
    98→  cj_builder_for_end(cj, &loop);
    99→
   100→  cj_builder_return_value(cj, &frame, sum);
   101→
   102→  sum_fn fn = (sum_fn)create_cj_fn(cj);
   103→  printf
... (truncated)
Read — cj/Makefile
{
  "file_path": "cj/Makefile"
}
result
1→TARGET=libcj.so
     2→BUILDDIR=bin/
     3→PREFIX=/usr/local/lib/
     4→SOURCES=$(wildcard src/*.c src/arch/*.c src/arch/*/*.c)
     5→override CFLAGS+=-std=c11 -O2 -Wno-gnu
     6→LDFLAGS+=-shared
     7→DEVFLAGS=-Werror -Wall -g -fPIC -DNDEBUG -Wfloat-equal -Wundef -Wwrite-strings -Wuninitialized -pedantic -O0
     8→
     9→.PHONY: all codegen dev example demo clean install uninstall
    10→
    11→all:
    12→	mkdir -p $(BUILDDIR)
    13→	$(CC) $(SOURCES) -o $(BUILDDIR)$(TARGET) $(CFLAGS) $(LDFLAGS)
    14→
    15→codegen: codegen_x86 codegen_arm64
    16→
    17→codegen_x86:
    18→	@echo "Generating x86-64 backend..."
    19→	node codegen/x86_encoder.js
    20→
    21→codegen_arm64:
    22→	@echo "Generating ARM64 backend..."
    23→	node codegen/arm64_encoder.js
    24→
    25→dev:
    26→	mkdir -p $(BUILDDIR)
    27→	$(CC) $(SOURCES) -o $(BUILDDIR)$(TARGET) $(CFLAGS) $(DEVFLAGS) $(LDFLAGS)
    28→
    29→install: all
    30→	install $(BUILDDIR)$(TARGET) $(PREFIX)$(TARGET)
    31→
    32→uninstall:
    33→	rm -rf $(PREFIX)$(TARGET)
    34→
    35→clean:
    36→	rm -rf $(BUILDDIR)
    37→

1→{
     2→  "name": "cj-codegen",
     3→  "version": "1.0.0",
     4→  "description": "Code generator for cj JIT framework",
     5→  "private": true,
     6→  "scripts": {
     7→    "generate:x86": "node codegen/x86_encoder.js",
     8→    "generate:arm64": "node codegen/arm64_encoder.js",
     9→    "generate": "npm run generate:x86 && npm run generate:arm64"
    10→  },
    11→  "dependencies": {
    12→    "asmdb": "^0.0.3"
    13→  }
    14→}
    15→

result
1→#pragma once
     2→
     3→#include <stddef.h>
     4→#include <stdint.h>
     5→
     6→typedef void (*cj_fn)(void);
     7→
     8→typedef struct {
     9→  int id;
    10→} cj_label;
    11→
    12→typedef enum { CJ_FIXUP_KIND_ARM_BRANCH, CJ_FIXUP_KIND_X86_RELATIVE } cj_fixup_kind;
    13→
    14→typedef struct {
    15→  int label_id;
    16→  uint64_t patch_offset;
    17→  cj_fixup_kind kind;
    18→  union {
    19→    struct {
    20→      uint8_t offset_bits;
    21→      uint8_t offset_shift;
    22→    } arm;
    23→    struct {
    24→      uint8_t width;
    25→    } x86;
    26→  } info;
    27→} cj_fixup;
    28→
    29→typedef struct {
    30→  uint8_t *mem;
    31→  uint64_t len;
    32→  uint64_t size;
    33→
    34→  uint64_t *label_positions;
    35→  int num_labels;
    36→  int label_capacity;
    37→
    38→  cj_fixup *fixups;
    39→  int num_fixups;
    40→  int fixup_capacity;
    41→
    42→  uint8_t *executable_base;
    43→  uint8_t *executable_raw;
    44→  size_t executable_size;
    45→  uint64_t executable_code_size;
    46→} cj_ctx;
    47→
    48→cj_ctx *create_cj_ctx(void);
    49→void cj_add_u8(cj_ctx *, uint8_t);
    50→void cj_add_u16(cj_ctx *, uint16_t);
    51→void cj_add_u32(cj_ctx *, uint32_t);
    52→void cj_add_u64(cj_ctx *, uint64_t);
    53→void cj_add_bytes(cj_ctx *, uint8_t *, uint64_t);
    54→void destroy_cj_ctx(cj_ctx *);
    55→cj_fn create_cj_fn(cj_ctx *);
    56→void destroy_cj_fn(cj_ctx *, cj_fn);
    57→
    58→cj_label cj_create_label(cj_ctx *ctx);
    59→void cj_mark_label(cj_ctx *ctx, cj_label label);
    60→
    61→void cj_emit_branch(cj_ctx *ctx, uint32_t base_instr, cj_label label, uint8_t offset_bits,
    62→                    uint8_t offset_shift);
    63→void cj_emit_x86_rel(cj_ctx *ctx, const uint8_t *opcode, size_t opcode_len, uint8_t disp_width,
    64→                     cj_label label);
    65→void *cj_resolve_label(const cj_ctx *ctx, cj_fn module, cj_label label);
    66→
Read — cj/src/ctx.c
{
  "file_path": "cj/src/ctx.c"
}
result
1→#define _DEFAULT_SOURCE
     2→#include <assert.h>
     3→#include <stdint.h>
     4→#include <stdlib.h>
     5→#include <string.h>
     6→#include <sys/mman.h>
     7→
     8→#include "ctx.h"
     9→
    10→cj_ctx *create_cj_ctx(void) {
    11→  cj_ctx *res = malloc(sizeof(cj_ctx));
    12→  res->mem = calloc(1, 32);
    13→  res->len = 0;
    14→  res->size = 32;
    15→
    16→  res->label_capacity = 16;
    17→  res->num_labels = 0;
    18→  res->label_positions = malloc(sizeof(uint64_t) * res->label_capacity);
    19→
    20→  res->fixup_capacity = 32;
    21→  res->num_fixups = 0;
    22→  res->fixups = malloc(sizeof(cj_fixup) * res->fixup_capacity);
    23→
    24→  res->executable_base = NULL;
    25→  res->executable_raw = NULL;
    26→  res->executable_size = 0;
    27→  res->executable_code_size = 0;
    28→
    29→  return res;
    30→}
    31→
    32→void grow_cj_ctx(cj_ctx *ctx) {
    33→  if (!ctx) return;
    34→
    35→  uint64_t old_size = ctx->size;
    36→  uint64_t new_size = old_size * 2;
    37→  if (new_size < old_size) return;
    38→
    39→  uint8_t *new_mem = realloc(ctx->mem, new_size);
    40→  if (!new_mem) return;
    41→
    42→  ctx->mem = new_mem;
    43→  memset(ctx->mem + old_size, 0, old_size);
    44→  ctx->size = new_size;
    45→}
    46→
    47→void destroy_cj_ctx(cj_ctx *ctx) {
    48→  free(ctx->mem);
    49→  free(ctx->label_positions);
    50→  free(ctx->fixups);
    51→  free(ctx);
    52→}
    53→
    54→cj_fn create_cj_fn(cj_ctx *ctx) {
    55→  if (!ctx->len) return NULL;
    56→
    57→  uint64_t code_size = ctx->len;
    58→  size_t total_size = sizeof(uint64_t) + (size_t)code_size;
    59→
    60→  uint8_t *raw = mmap(NULL, total_size, PROT_READ | PROT_WRITE, MAP_PRIVATE | MAP_ANONYMOUS, -1, 0);
    61→  if (raw == MAP_FAILED) return NULL;
    62→
    63→  assert(ctx->mem);
    64→  uint8_t *dest = raw + sizeof(uint64_t);
    65→  memcpy(dest, ctx->mem, code_size);
    66→  *((uint64_t *)raw) = code_size;
    67→
    68→  if (mprotect(raw, total_size, PROT_READ | PROT_EXEC) != 0) {
    69→    munmap(raw, total_size);
    70→    return NULL;
    71→  }
    72→
    73→  ctx->executable_raw = raw;
    74→  ctx->executable_base = dest;
    75→  ctx->executable_size = total_size;
    76→  ctx->executable_code_size = code_size;
    77→
    78→  // clear the I cache for ARM64
    79→  __builtin___clear_cache((char *)raw, (char *)raw + total_size);
    80→
    81→// we know this is unsafe, and we do it anywyy
    82→#pragma GCC diagnostic push
    83→#pragma GCC diagnostic ignored "-Wpedantic"
    84→  return (cj_fn)(raw + sizeof(uint64_t));
    85→#pragma GCC diagnostic pop
    86→}
    87→
    88→void destroy_cj_fn(cj_ctx *ctx, cj_fn mem) {
    89→  (void)ctx;
    90→  if (!mem) return;
    91→
    92→// yes, yes. unsafe. boo-hoo.
    93→#pragma GCC diagnostic push
    94→#pragma GCC diagnostic ignored "-Wpedantic"
    95→  uint8_t *code = (uint8_t *)(void *)mem;
    96→#pragma GCC diagnostic pop
 
... (truncated)

{
  "file_path": "cj/src/op.h"

result
1→#pragma once
     2→
     3→#include "ctx.h"
     4→
     5→typedef enum { CJ_REGISTER, CJ_CONSTANT, CJ_MEMORY, CJ_REGISTER_LIST } cj_operand_type;
     6→
     7→typedef enum {
     8→  CJ_SHIFT_KIND_NONE = 0,
     9→  CJ_SHIFT_KIND_LSL,
    10→  CJ_SHIFT_KIND_LSR,
    11→  CJ_SHIFT_KIND_ASR,
    12→  CJ_SHIFT_KIND_ROR
    13→} cj_shift_kind;
    14→
    15→typedef enum {
    16→  CJ_EXTEND_KIND_NONE = 0,
    17→  CJ_EXTEND_KIND_UXTB,
    18→  CJ_EXTEND_KIND_UXTH,
    19→  CJ_EXTEND_KIND_UXTW,
    20→  CJ_EXTEND_KIND_UXTX,
    21→  CJ_EXTEND_KIND_SXTB,
    22→  CJ_EXTEND_KIND_SXTH,
    23→  CJ_EXTEND_KIND_SXTW,
    24→  CJ_EXTEND_KIND_SXTX
    25→} cj_extend_kind;
    26→
    27→typedef enum { CJ_MEM_MODE_OFFSET = 0, CJ_MEM_MODE_PRE, CJ_MEM_MODE_POST } cj_mem_mode;
    28→
    29→typedef struct {
    30→  uint8_t kind;       // cj_shift_kind
    31→  uint8_t amount;     // Shift amount (bits depend on instruction)
    32→  uint8_t has_amount; // Explicit amount provided
    33→} cj_shift_info;
    34→
    35→typedef struct {
    36→  uint8_t kind;       // cj_extend_kind
    37→  uint8_t amount;     // Optional left shift applied after extend
    38→  uint8_t has_amount; // Explicit shift amount provided
    39→} cj_extend_info;
    40→
    41→typedef enum {
    42→  CJ_ROUND_DEFAULT = 0,
    43→  CJ_ROUND_NEAREST,
    44→  CJ_ROUND_DOWN,
    45→  CJ_ROUND_UP,
    46→  CJ_ROUND_ZERO
    47→} cj_rounding_mode;
    48→
    49→typedef struct {
    50→  cj_operand_type type;
    51→  const char *mask;
    52→  uint8_t zero_mask;
    53→  uint8_t sae;
    54→  uint8_t rounding;
    55→  union {

    70→  cj_shift_info shift;
    71→  cj_extend_info extend;
    72→} cj_operand;
    73→
    74→static inline cj_operand cj_make_register(const char *name) {
    75→  cj_operand op = {
    76→      .type = CJ_REGISTER,
    77→      .mask = NULL,
    78→      .zero_mask = 0,
    79→      .sae = 0,
    80→      .rounding = CJ_ROUND_DEFAULT,
    81→      .reg = name,
    82→      .shift = {.kind = CJ_SHIFT_KIND_NONE, .amount = 0, .has_amount = 0},
    83→      .extend = {.kind = CJ_EXTEND_KIND_NONE, .amount = 0, .has_amount = 0},
    84→  };
    85→  return op;
    86→}
    87→

    91→      .mask = NULL,
    92→      .zero_mask = 0,
    93→      .sae = 0,
    94→      .rounding = CJ_ROUND_DEFAULT,
    95→      .constant = value,
    96→      .shift = {.kind = CJ_SHIFT_KIND_NONE, .amount = 0, .has_amount = 0},
    97→      .extend = {.kind = CJ_EXTEND_KIND_NONE, .amount = 0,

result
1→
     2→#include <string.h>
     3→#include <stdlib.h>
     4→#include "../../ctx.h"
     5→
     6→#pragma GCC diagnostic push
     7→#pragma GCC diagnostic ignored "-Wunused-label"
     8→
     9→static const uint8_t REG_RAX = 0, REG_RCX = 1, REG_RDX = 2, REG_RBX = 3;
    10→static const uint8_t REG_RSP = 4, REG_RBP = 5, REG_RSI = 6, REG_RDI = 7;
    11→static const uint8_t REG_R8 = 8, REG_R9 = 9, REG_R10 = 10, REG_R11 = 11;
    12→static const uint8_t REG_R12 = 12, REG_R13 = 13, REG_R14 = 14, REG_R15 = 15;
    13→
    14→static int parse_reg_index(const char* name, const char* prefix, int max) {
    15→  if (!name || !prefix) return -1;
    16→  size_t len = strlen(prefix);
    17→  if (strncmp(name, prefix, len) != 0) return -1;
    18→  const char* p = name + len;
    19→  if (*p == '\0') return -1;
    20→  char* end = NULL;
    21→  long value = strtol(p, &end, 10);
    22→  if (!end || *end != '\0') return -1;
    23→  if (value < 0 || value >= max) return -1;
    24→  return (int)value;
    25→}
    26→
    27→static int is_mmx_reg(const char* name) {
    28→  return parse_reg_index(name, "mm", 8) >= 0;
    29→}
    30→
    31→static int is_xmm_reg(const char* name) {
    32→  return parse_reg_index(name, "xmm", 32) >= 0;
    33→}
    34→
    35→static int is_ymm_reg(const char* name) {
    36→  return parse_reg_index(name, "ymm", 32) >= 0;
    37→}
    38→
    39→static int is_zmm_reg(const char* name) {
    40→  return parse_reg_index(name, "zmm", 32) >= 0;
    41→}
    42→
    43→static int is_mask_reg(const char* name) {
    44→  return parse_reg_index(name, "k", 8) >= 0;
    45→}
    46→
    47→static int8_t parse_reg(const char* name) {
    48→  if (!name) return -1;
    49→
    50→  if (strcmp(name, "rax") == 0) return REG_RAX;
    51→  if (strcmp(name, "eax") == 0) return REG_RAX;
    52→  if (strcmp(name, "ax") == 0) return REG_RAX;
    53→  if (strcmp(name, "al") == 0) return REG_RAX;
    54→  if (strcmp(name, "x0") == 0) return REG_RAX;
    55→  if (strcmp(name, "rcx") == 0) return REG_RCX;
    56→  if (strcmp(name, "ecx") == 0) return REG_RCX;
    57→  if (strcmp(name, "cx") == 0) return REG_RCX;
    58→  if (strcmp(name, "cl") == 0) return REG_RCX;
    59→  if (strcmp(name, "x1") == 0) return REG_RCX;
    60→  if (strcmp(name, "rdx") == 0) return REG_RDX;
    61→  if (strcmp(name, "edx") == 0) return REG_RDX;
    62→  if (strcmp(name, "dx") == 0) return REG_RDX;
    63→  if (strcmp(name, "dl") == 0) return REG_RDX;
    64→  if (strcmp(name, "x2") == 0) return REG_RDX;
    65→  if (strcmp(name, "rbx") == 0) return REG_RBX;
    66→  if (strcmp(name, "ebx") == 0) return REG_RBX;
    67→  if (strcmp(name, "bx") == 0) return REG_RBX;
    68→  if (strcmp(name, "bl") == 0) return REG_RBX;
    69→  if (strcmp(name, "x3") == 0) return REG_RBX;
    70→  if (strcmp(name, "rsp") == 0) return REG_RSP;
    71→  if (strcmp(name, "esp") == 0) return REG_RSP;
    72→  if (strcmp(name, "sp") == 0) return REG_RSP;
    73→  if (strcmp(name, "

Read — cj/src/arch/arm64/backend.h

result
1→#pragma once
     2→
     3→#include <stdint.h>
     4→#include <stdio.h>
     5→#include <string.h>
     6→#include "../../ctx.h"
     7→#include "../../op.h"
     8→
     9→static inline int arm64_parse_reg(const char* name) {
    10→  if (!name) return -1;
    11→
    12→  for (int i = 0; i <= 30; i++) {
    13→    char buf[8];
    14→    snprintf(buf, sizeof(buf), "x%d", i);
    15→    if (strcmp(name, buf) == 0) return i;
    16→  }
    17→
    18→  for (int i = 0; i <= 30; i++) {
    19→    char buf[8];
    20→    snprintf(buf, sizeof(buf), "w%d", i);
    21→    if (strcmp(name, buf) == 0) return i;
    22→  }
    23→
    24→  if (strcmp(name, "xzr") == 0) return 31;
    25→  if (strcmp(name, "wzr") == 0) return 31;
    26→  if (strcmp(name, "sp") == 0) return 31;
    27→
    28→  return -1;
    29→}
    30→
    31→static inline int arm64_is_64bit(const char* name) {
    32→  if (!name) return 0;
    33→  return name[0] == 'x' || strcmp(name, "sp") == 0;
    34→}
    35→
    36→static inline int arm64_parse_fp_reg(const char* name) {
    37→  if (!name) return -1;
    38→
    39→  for (int i = 0; i <= 31; i++) {
    40→    char buf[8];
    41→    snprintf(buf, sizeof(buf), "s%d", i);
    42→    if (strcmp(name, buf) == 0) return i;
    43→  }
    44→
    45→  for (int i = 0; i <= 31; i++) {
    46→    char buf[8];
    47→    snprintf(buf, sizeof(buf), "d%d", i);
    48→    if (strcmp(name, buf) == 0) return i;
    49→  }
    50→
    51→  return -1;
    52→}
    53→
    54→static inline int arm64_is_fp_64bit(const char* name) {
    55→  if (!name) return 0;
    56→  return name[0] == 'd';
    57→}
    58→
    59→typedef struct {
    60→  int reg;
    61→  uint32_t q;
    62→  uint32_t size;
    63→} arm64_vec_reg_info;
    64→
    65→static inline arm64_vec_reg_info arm64_parse_vec_reg(const char* name) {
    66→  arm64_vec_reg_info info = { .reg = -1, .q = 0, .size = 0 };
    67→  if (!name || name[0] != 'v') return info;
    68→
    69→  const char* ptr = name + 1;
    70→  int reg = 0;
    71→  while (*ptr >= '0' && *ptr <= '9') {
    72→    reg = reg * 10 + (*ptr - '0');
    73→    ptr++;
    74→  }
    75→  if (ptr == name + 1 || reg < 0 || reg > 31) {
    76→    info.reg = -1;
    77→    return info;
    78→  }
    79→  info.reg = reg;
    80→
    81→  uint32_t q = 1;
    82→  uint32_t size = 0;
    83→
    84→  if (*ptr == '.') {
    85→    ptr++;
    86→    if (ptr[0] == '8' && ptr[1] == 'b') {
    87→      size = 0;
    88→      q = 0;
    89→      ptr += 2;
    90→    } else if (ptr[0] == '1' && ptr[1] == '6' && ptr[2] == 'b') {
    91→      size = 0;
    92→      q = 1;
    93→      ptr += 3;
    94→    } else if (ptr[0] == '4' && ptr[1] == 'h') {
    95→      size = 1;
    96→      q = 0;
    97→      ptr += 2;
    98→    } else if (ptr[0] == '8' && ptr[1] == 'h') {
    99→      size = 1;
   100→      q = 1;
   101→      ptr += 2;
   102→    } else if (ptr[0] == '2' && ptr[1] == 's') {
   103→      size = 2;
   104→      q = 0;
... (truncated)

result
1→const asmdb = require("asmdb");
     2→const x86 = new asmdb.x86util.X86DataBase().addDefault();
     3→
     4→const hexByte = (value) => `0x${value.toString(16).padStart(2, '0')}`;
     5→
     6→function toCamel(str) {
     7→  return str.replace(/[^a-z0-9]+/gi, '_');
     8→}
     9→
    10→const registers = [
    11→  { r64: 'rax', r32: 'eax', r16: 'ax', r8: 'al', aliases: ['x0'], encoding: 0 },
    12→  { r64: 'rcx', r32: 'ecx', r16: 'cx', r8: 'cl', aliases: ['x1'], encoding: 1 },
    13→  { r64: 'rdx', r32: 'edx', r16: 'dx', r8: 'dl', aliases: ['x2'], encoding: 2 },
    14→  { r64: 'rbx', r32: 'ebx', r16: 'bx', r8: 'bl', aliases: ['x3'], encoding: 3 },
    15→  { r64: 'rsp', r32: 'esp', r16: 'sp', r8: 'spl', aliases: [], encoding: 4 },
    16→  { r64: 'rbp', r32: 'ebp', r16: 'bp', r8: 'bpl', aliases: [], encoding: 5 },
    17→  { r64: 'rsi', r32: 'esi', r16: 'si', r8: 'sil', aliases: [], encoding: 6 },
    18→  { r64: 'rdi', r32: 'edi', r16: 'di', r8: 'dil', aliases: [], encoding: 7 },
    19→  { r64: 'r8',  r32: 'r8d', r16: 'r8w', r8: 'r8b', aliases: [], encoding: 8 },
    20→  { r64: 'r9',  r32: 'r9d', r16: 'r9w', r8: 'r9b', aliases: [], encoding: 9 },
    21→  { r64: 'r10', r32: 'r10d', r16: 'r10w', r8: 'r10b', aliases: [], encoding: 10 },

1→/**
     2→ * WATCH OUT: I LET AN LLM GENERATE THESE COMMENTS. TAKE WITH A GRAIN OF SALT.
     3→ * ARM64 Instruction Encoder Generator
     4→ *
     5→ * This code generator creates C functions for encoding ARM64 instructions.
     6→ * It reads instruction definitions from arm64_instructions_full.jsonl and
     7→ * generates efficient encoder functions in src/arch/arm64/backend.h
     8→ *
     9→ * Key features:
    10→ * - Supports 2,516+ ARM64 A64 instructions (99% coverage)
    11→ * - Handles multiple instruction variants (32/64-bit, FP/integer, etc.)
    12→ * - Generates type-safe operand checking
    13→ * - Optimizes for common addressing modes
    14→ *
    15→ * Architecture:
    16→ * 1. Load and deduplicate instruction definitions
    17→ * 2. Filter to supported instructions (A64, valid C identifiers)
    18→ * 3. Group by mnemonic and sort by priority
    19→ * 4. Generate C encoder functions with proper register field mapping
    20→ */
    21→
    22→const fs = require('fs');
    23→
    24→// Read ARM64 instruction data with operands
    25→const lines = fs.readFileSync('./codegen/arm64_instructions_full.jsonl', 'utf8').split('\n').filter(l => l.trim());
    26→const allInstructions = lines.map(line => JSON.parse(line));
    27→
    28→// Deduplicate by ID (some entries appear multiple times)
    29→const seenIds = new Set();
    30→const instructions = allInstructions.filter(inst => {
    31→  if (seenIds.has(inst.id)) return false;
    32→  seenIds.add(inst.id);
    33→  return true;
    34→});
    35→
    36→console.error(`Loaded ${instructions.length} ARM64 instruction encodings (${allInstructions.length - instructions.length} duplicates removed)`);
    37→
    38→// Exclude complex instruction sets that need special handling
    39→// Accept everything else - we have complete encodings!
    40→const EXCLUDED_PATTERNS = [
    41→  // SIMD/NEON - complex operations that need special handling
    42→  '.*_asimddiff.*',     // SIMD long/narrow operations with invalid mnemonics (ADDHN{2}, etc)
    43→
    44→  // ALL INSTRUCTION SETS NOW SUPPORTED:
    45→  // - SIMD/NEON: All operations except asimddiff
    46→  // - Cryptography: AES, SHA, etc.
    47→  // - System instructions: MSR, MRS, SYS, SYSL
    48→  // - Pointer authentication
    49→  // - SVE (Scalable Vector Extension): Z and P registers - NOW SUPPORTED!
    50→];
    51→
    52→function matchesPattern(id, patterns) {
    53→  if (!id) return false;
    54→  for (const pat of patterns) {
    55→    const regex = new RegExp('^' + pat + '$');
    56→    if (regex.test(id)) return true;
    57→  }
    58→  return false;
    59→}
    60→
    61→// Check if mnemonic is a valid C identifier or has {2} suffix, .<cond>, or <bt>
    62→function isValidCIdentifier(mnemonic) {
    63→  if (!mnemonic) return false;
    64→  // Allow {2} suffix for narrow/widen operations
    65→  if (mnemonic.endsWith('{2}')) {
    66→    const base = mnemonic.slice(0, -3);
    67→    ret
... (truncated)

result
150→  arm64_z_reg_info info = { .reg = -1, .size = 0 };
   151→  if (!name || name[0] != 'z') return info;
   152→
   153→  const char* ptr = name + 1;
   154→  int reg = 0;
   155→  while (*ptr >= '0' && *ptr <= '9') {
   156→    reg = reg * 10 + (*ptr - '0');
   157→    ptr++;
   158→  }
   159→  if (ptr == name + 1 || reg < 0 || reg > 31) {
   160→    info.reg = -1;
   161→    return info;
   162→  }
   163→  info.reg = reg;
   164→
   165→  uint32_t size = 0;
   166→  if (*ptr == '.') {
   167→    ptr++;
   168→    if (*ptr == 'b' || *ptr == 'B') {
   169→      size = 0;
   170→      ptr++;
   171→    } else if (*ptr == 'h' || *ptr == 'H') {
   172→      size = 1;
   173→      ptr++;
   174→    } else if (*ptr == 's' || *ptr == 'S') {
   175→      size = 2;
   176→      ptr++;
   177→    } else if (*ptr == 'd' || *ptr == 'D') {
   178→      size = 3;
   179→      ptr++;
   180→    } else {
   181→      info.reg = -1;
   182→      return info;
   183→    }
   184→  }
   185→
   186→  if (*ptr != '\0') {
   187→    info.reg = -1;
   188→    return info;
   189→  }
   190→
   191→  info.size = size;
   192→  return info;
   193→}
   194→
   195→static inline int arm64_parse_p_reg(const char* name) {
   196→  if (!name || name[0] != 'p') return -1;
   197→
   198→  const char* ptr = name + 1;
   199→  int reg = 0;
   200→  while (*ptr >= '0' && *ptr <= '9') {
   201→    reg = reg * 10 + (*ptr - '0');
   202→    ptr++;
   203→  }
   204→  if (ptr == name + 1 || reg < 0 || reg > 15) {
   205→    return -1;
   206→  }
   207→
   208→  if (*ptr == '/' && (ptr[1] == 'm' || ptr[1] == 'z')) {
   209→    ptr += 2;
   210→  }
   211→
   212→  if (*ptr != '\0') {
   213→    return -1;
   214→  }
   215→
   216→  return reg;
   217→}
   218→
   219→typedef struct {
   220→  arm64_vec_reg_info regs[4];
   221→  uint8_t count;
   222→} arm64_vec_list_info;
   223→
   224→static inline int arm64_parse_vec_list_operand(cj_operand operand, uint8_t expected_count, arm64_vec_list_info* out) {
   225→  if (!out || expected_count == 0 || expected_count > 4) return 0;
   226→  out->count = 0;
   227→
   228→  if (operand.type == CJ_REGISTER) {
   229→    if (expected_count != 1) return 0;
   230→    arm64_vec_reg_info info = arm64_parse_vec_reg(operand.reg);
   231→    if (info.reg < 0) return 0;
   232→    out->regs[0] = info;
   233→    out->count = 1;
   234→    return 1;
   235→  }
   236→
   237→  if (operand.type != CJ_REGISTER_LIST || !operand.reg_list.regs || operand.reg_list.count != expected_count) {
   238→    return 0;
   239→  }
   240→
   241→  for (uint8_t i = 0; i < expected_count; ++i) {
   242→    arm64_vec_reg_info info = arm64_parse_vec_reg(operand.reg_list.regs[i]);
   243→    if (info.reg < 0) return 0;
   244→    if (i > 0) {
   245→      const arm64_vec_reg_info prev = out->regs[i - 1];
   246→      if (info.q != prev.q || info.size != prev.size) return 0;
   247→      if (((prev.reg + 1) & 0x1f) != info.reg) return 0;
   248→    }
   249→

1→#include "ctx.h"
     2→#include "op.h"
     3→#include <stdio.h>
     4→
     5→typedef int (*add_fn)(int);
     6→
     7→int main(void) {
     8→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
     9→
    10→#ifdef __aarch64__
    11→  cj_operand reg = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "x0"};
    12→#else
    13→  cj_operand reg = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rax"};
    14→  cj_operand arg = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rdi"};
    15→  cj_mov(cj, reg, arg);
    16→#endif
    17→
    18→  cj_operand val = {.type = CJ_CONSTANT, .constant = 32};
    19→
    20→  cj_add(cj, reg, val);
    21→  cj_ret(cj);
    22→
    23→  add_fn f = (add_fn)create_cj_fn(cj);
    24→
    25→  int res = f(10);
    26→
    27→  destroy_cj_fn(cj, (cj_fn)f);
    28→  destroy_cj_ctx(cj);
    29→
    30→  return res;
    31→}

result
1→/*
     2→ * Fibonacci - JIT Compilation Demo
     3→ *
     4→ * This example demonstrates JIT compilation of an iterative Fibonacci function.
     5→ * We'll generate optimized machine code at runtime that computes Fibonacci numbers.
     6→ *
     7→ * Function: int fib(int n)
     8→ *   Returns the nth Fibonacci number
     9→ *   fib(n) = iterative calculation
    10→ *
    11→ * This shows:
    12→ *   - Conditional branches with labels
    13→ *   - Loops in JIT-compiled code
    14→ *   - Register allocation patterns
    15→ *   - ARM64 function calling conventions
    16→ */
    17→
    18→#include "ctx.h"
    19→#include "op.h"
    20→#include <stdio.h>
    21→#include <time.h>
    22→
    23→// Type for our JIT-compiled function
    24→// int fib(int n)
    25→typedef int (*fib_fn)(int);
    26→
    27→// Reference implementation for comparison
    28→int fib_c(int n) {
    29→  if (n <= 1) return n;
    30→  int a = 0, b = 1;
    31→  for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
    32→    int temp = a + b;
    33→    a = b;
    34→    b = temp;
    35→  }
    36→  return b;
    37→}
    38→
    39→int main(void) {
    40→  // Create JIT context
    41→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    42→
    43→  // Generate iterative Fibonacci:
    44→  // int fib(int n) {
    45→  //     if (n <= 1) return n;
    46→  //     int a = 0, b = 1;
    47→  //     for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) {
    48→  //         int temp = a + b;
    49→  //         a = b;
    50→  //         b = temp;
    51→  //     }
    52→  //     return b;
    53→  // }
    54→  //
    55→  // ARM64 calling convention: w0 = n (32-bit int)
    56→  // We'll use: w0 = n, w1 = a, w2 = b, w3 = temp, w4 = i
    57→
    58→#ifdef __aarch64__
    59→  cj_operand w0 = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "w0"};   // n (and return value)
    60→  cj_operand w1 = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "w1"};   // a
    61→  cj_operand w2 = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "w2"};   // b
    62→  cj_operand w3 = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "w3"};   // temp
    63→  cj_operand w4 = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "w4"};   // i
    64→  cj_operand wzr = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "wzr"}; // zero register
    65→
    66→  // Create labels
    67→  cj_label return_n = cj_create_label(cj);
    68→  cj_label loop_start = cj_create_label(cj);
    69→  cj_label loop_end = cj_create_label(cj);
    70→
    71→  // if (n <= 1) return n
    72→  cj_operand one_imm = {.type = CJ_CONSTANT, .constant = 1};
    73→  cj_cmp(cj, w0, one_imm);
    74→  cj_ble(cj, return_n); // branch if n <= 1
    75→
    76→  // Initialize: a = 0, b = 1, i = 2
    77→  cj_mov(cj, w1, wzr); // a = 0
    78→  cj_mov(cj, w2, wzr); // b = 0
    79→  cj_operand one_const = {.type = CJ_CONSTANT, .constant = 1};
    80→  cj_add(cj, w2, one_const); // b = 0 + 1 = 1
    81→  cj_operand two_const = {.type = CJ_CONSTANT, .constant = 2};
    82→  cj_mov(cj, w4, wzr);       // i = 0
    83→  cj_add(cj, w4, two_const); // i = 0 + 2 = 2
    84→
    85→  // Loop: while (
... (truncated)

1→#include "ctx.h"
     2→#include "op.h"
     3→
     4→// this one works on both backends
     5→int main() {
     6→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
     7→
     8→  cj_nop(cj);
     9→  cj_ret(cj);
    10→
    11→  cj_fn f = create_cj_fn(cj);
    12→
    13→  f();
    14→
    15→  destroy_cj_fn(cj, f);

Read — cj/examples/hl_fibonacci.c
{
  "file_path": "cj/examples/hl_fibonacci.c"
}
result
1→#include <stdio.h>
     2→
     3→#include "builder.h"
     4→
     5→typedef int (*fib_fn)(int);
     6→
     7→static int fib_c(int n) {
     8→  if (n <= 1) return n;
     9→  int a = 0;
    10→  int b = 1;
    11→  for (int i = 2; i <= n; ++i) {
    12→    int tmp = a + b;
    13→    a = b;
    14→    b = tmp;
    15→  }
    16→  return b;
    17→}
    18→
    19→int main(void) {
    20→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    21→
    22→  cj_builder_frame frame;
    23→  cj_builder_fn_prologue(cj, 0, &frame);
    24→
    25→  cj_operand n = cj_builder_arg_int(cj, 0);
    26→  cj_operand one = cj_make_constant(1);
    27→  cj_operand two = cj_make_constant(2);
    28→
    29→  cj_builder_if_block early = cj_builder_if(cj, n, one, CJ_COND_LE);
    30→  cj_builder_return_value(cj, &frame, n);
    31→  cj_builder_endif(cj, &early);
    32→
    33→  cj_operand acc_a = cj_builder_scratch_reg(0);
    34→  cj_operand acc_b = cj_builder_scratch_reg(1);
    35→  cj_operand tmp = cj_builder_scratch_reg(2);
    36→  cj_operand i = cj_builder_scratch_reg(3);
    37→
    38→  cj_builder_assign(cj, acc_a, cj_builder_zero_operand());
    39→  cj_builder_assign(cj, acc_b, one);
    40→
    41→  cj_builder_for_loop loop = cj_builder_for_begin(cj, i, one, n, one, CJ_COND_GE);
    42→
    43→  cj_builder_assign(cj, tmp, acc_a);
    44→  cj_builder_add_assign(cj, tmp, acc_b);
    45→  cj_builder_assign(cj, acc_a, acc_b);
    46→  cj_builder_assign(cj, acc_b, tmp);
    47→
    48→  cj_builder_for_end(cj, &loop);
    49→  cj_builder_return_value(cj, &frame, acc_b);
    50→
    51→  fib_fn fib_jit = (fib_fn)create_cj_fn(cj);
    52→  if (!fib_jit) {
    53→    puts("failed to create jit function");
    54→    destroy_cj_ctx(cj);
    55→    return 1;
    56→  }
    57→
    58→  int all_pass = 1;
    59→  for (int idx = 0; idx <= 15; ++idx) {
    60→    int result = fib_jit(idx);
    61→    int expected = fib_c(idx);
    62→    int pass = (result == expected);
    63→    all_pass &= pass;
    64→    printf("fib(%d) = %d (expected %d)%s\n", idx, result, expected, pass ? "" : "  <-- mismatch");
    65→  }
    66→
    67→  destroy_cj_fn(cj, (cj_fn)fib_jit);
    68→  destroy_cj_ctx(cj);
    69→
    70→  return all_pass ? 0 : 1;
    71→}
    72→

}
result
1→#include "ctx.h"
     2→#include "op.h"
     3→#include "register.h"
     4→#include <stdio.h>
     5→
     6→typedef void (*simd_add_fn)(float *, const float *, const float *, int);
     7→
     8→int main(void) {
     9→#ifdef __x86_64__
    10→  float a[8] = {1.0f, 2.0f, 3.0f, 4.0f, 5.0f, 6.0f, 7.0f, 8.0f};
    11→  float b[8] = {8.0f, 7.0f, 6.0f, 5.0f, 4.0f, 3.0f, 2.0f, 1.0f};
    12→  float out[8] = {0};
    13→  const int length = 8;
    14→
    15→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    16→
    17→  cj_operand rdi = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rdi"};
    18→  cj_operand rsi = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rsi"};
    19→  cj_operand rdx = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rdx"};
    20→  cj_operand rcx = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rcx"};
    21→
    22→  cj_operand xmm0 = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "xmm0"};
    23→  cj_operand xmm1 = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "xmm1"};
    24→
    25→  cj_operand four = {.type = CJ_CONSTANT, .constant = 4};
    26→  cj_operand sixteen = {.type = CJ_CONSTANT, .constant = 16};
    27→
    28→  cj_operand dst_mem = {.type = CJ_MEMORY,
    29→                        .mem = {.base = "rdi", .index = NULL, .scale = 1, .disp = 0}};
    30→  cj_operand src_a_mem = {.type = CJ_MEMORY,
    31→                          .mem = {.base = "rsi", .index = NULL, .scale = 1, .disp = 0}};
    32→  cj_operand src_b_mem = {.type = CJ_MEMORY,
    33→                          .mem = {.base = "rdx", .index = NULL, .scale = 1, .disp = 0}};
    34→
    35→  cj_label loop = cj_create_label(cj);
    36→  cj_label done = cj_create_label(cj);
    37→
    38→  cj_cmp(cj, rcx, four);
    39→  cj_jl(cj, done);
    40→
    41→  cj_mark_label(cj, loop);
    42→
    43→  cj_movups(cj, xmm0, src_a_mem);
    44→  cj_movups(cj, xmm1, src_b_mem);
    45→  cj_addps(cj, xmm0, xmm1);
    46→  cj_movups(cj, dst_mem, xmm0);
    47→
    48→  cj_add(cj, rdi, sixteen);
    49→  cj_add(cj, rsi, sixteen);
    50→  cj_add(cj, rdx, sixteen);
    51→  cj_sub(cj, rcx, four);
    52→
    53→  cj_cmp(cj, rcx, four);
    54→  cj_jge(cj, loop);
    55→
    56→  cj_mark_label(cj, done);
    57→  cj_ret(cj);
    58→
    59→  simd_add_fn fn = (simd_add_fn)create_cj_fn(cj);
    60→  fn(out, a, b, length);
    61→
    62→  destroy_cj_fn(cj, (cj_fn)fn);
    63→  destroy_cj_ctx(cj);
    64→
    65→  for (int i = 0; i < length; ++i) {
    66→    printf("out[%d] = %.1f\n", i, out[i]);
    67→  }
    68→#elif defined(__aarch64__)
    69→  // ARM64 NEON version
    70→  float a[8] = {1.0f, 2.0f, 3.0f, 4.0f, 5.0f, 6.0f, 7.0f, 8.0f};
    71→  float b[8] = {8.0f, 7.0f, 6.0f, 5.0f, 4.0f, 3.0f, 2.0f, 1.0f};
    72→  float out[8] = {0};
    73→  const int length = 8;
    74→
    75→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    76→
    77→  // ARM64 calling convention: x0=dst, x1=src_a, x2=src_b, x3=length
    78→  cj_operand x0 = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "x0"};
    79→  cj_operand x1 = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "x1"};
    80→  cj_operand x2 = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "x2"};
   
... (truncated)

result
1→// minimal s-expression jit example for cj
     2→
     3→#include <ctype.h>
     4→#include <stdint.h>
     5→#include <stdio.h>
     6→#include <stdlib.h>
     7→#include <string.h>
     8→#if defined(__APPLE__)
     9→#include <ptrauth.h>
    10→#endif
    11→#include "builder.h"
    12→#define MAX_FUN 16
    13→#define MAX_NODES 256
    14→
    15→typedef enum { NODE_NUM, NODE_PARAM, NODE_ADD, NODE_SUB, NODE_CALL } node_kind;
    16→typedef struct node node;
    17→struct node {
    18→  node_kind kind;
    19→  int value;
    20→  int target;
    21→  char name[32];
    22→  node *left;
    23→  node *right;
    24→  node *arg;
    25→};
    26→
    27→typedef struct {
    28→  node nodes[MAX_NODES];
    29→  int count;
    30→} node_arena;
    31→static node *arena_new(node_arena *arena) {
    32→  if (arena->count >= MAX_NODES) {
    33→    fprintf(stderr, "node arena overflow\n");
    34→    exit(1);
    35→  }
    36→  node *n = &arena->nodes[arena->count++];
    37→  memset(n, 0, sizeof(*n));
    38→  n->target = -1;
    39→  return n;
    40→}
    41→
    42→typedef enum { TOK_LPAREN, TOK_RPAREN, TOK_IDENT, TOK_NUMBER, TOK_END } token_kind;
    43→typedef struct {
    44→  token_kind kind;
    45→  char text[32];
    46→  int value;
    47→} token;
    48→typedef struct {
    49→  const char *cur;
    50→  token tok;
    51→} lexer;
    52→
    53→static void next_token(lexer *lx) {
    54→  while (*lx->cur && isspace((unsigned char)*lx->cur)) lx->cur++;
    55→  char c = *lx->cur;
    56→  if (!c) {
    57→    lx->tok.kind = TOK_END;
    58→    return;
    59→  }
    60→  if (c == '(') {
    61→    lx->tok.kind = TOK_LPAREN;
    62→    lx->cur++;
    63→    return;
    64→  }
    65→  if (c == ')') {
    66→    lx->tok.kind = TOK_RPAREN;
    67→    lx->cur++;
    68→    return;
    69→  }
    70→  if (isdigit((unsigned char)c) || (c == '-' && isdigit((unsigned char)lx->cur[1]))) {
    71→    char *end = NULL;
    72→    long val = strtol(lx->cur, &end, 10);
    73→    lx->tok.kind = TOK_NUMBER;
    74→    lx->tok.value = (int)val;
    75→    size_t len = (size_t)(end - lx->cur);
    76→    if (len >= sizeof(lx->tok.text)) len = sizeof(lx->tok.text) - 1;
    77→    memcpy(lx->tok.text, lx->cur, len);
    78→    lx->tok.text[len] = '\0';
    79→    lx->cur = end;
    80→    return;
    81→  }
    82→  if (isalpha((unsigned char)c)) {
    83→    const char *start = lx->cur;
    84→    while (*lx->cur && (isalnum((unsigned char)*lx->cur) || *lx->cur == '_')) lx->cur++;
    85→    size_t len = (size_t)(lx->cur - start);
    86→    if (len >= sizeof(lx->tok.text)) len = sizeof(lx->tok.text) - 1;
    87→    memcpy(lx->tok.text, start, len);
    88→    lx->tok.text[len] = '\0';
    89→    lx->tok.kind = TOK_IDENT;
    90→    return;
    91→  }
    92→  fprintf(stderr, "unexpected character '%c'\n", c);
    93→  exit(1);
    94→}
    95→
    96→static void init_lexer(lexer *lx, const char *src) {
    97→  lx->cur = src;
    98→  next_token(l
... (truncated)
Read — cj/docs/builder.md
{
  "file_path": "cj/docs/builder.md"
}
result
1→# builder api
     2→
     3→the builder layer is a thin set of helpers on top of the raw emitted
     4→instructions. it keeps control in your hands while removing the boilerplate
     5→around abi registers, stack setup, and label plumbing, and it looks less scary.
     6→
     7→## setup
     8→
     9→- `cj_builder_fn_prologue(cj, stack_bytes, &frame)`: create a frame and reserve
    10→  optional stack space (aligned for both arches).
    11→- `cj_builder_fn_prologue_with_link_save(cj, stack_bytes, &frame)`: like above,
    12→  but on ARM64 it also saves/restores `x30` for non-leaf functions.
    13→- `cj_builder_fn_epilogue(cj, &frame)` / `cj_builder_return(cj, &frame)`:
    14→  restore the frame and emit `ret`.
    15→
    16→## operands
    17→
    18→- `cj_builder_arg_int(cj, index)`: returns the platform-specific argument
    19→  register.
    20→- `cj_builder_scratch_reg(index)`: picks a caller-saved temporary.
    21→- `cj_builder_scratch_init`, `cj_builder_scratch_acquire`,
    22→  `cj_builder_scratch_release`: managed stack of scratch registers for balanced
    23→  temporaries.
    24→- `cj_builder_zero_operand()` + `cj_builder_clear(cj, dst)`: easy zeroing.
    25→- `cj_builder_assign`, `cj_builder_add_assign`, `cj_builder_sub_assign`:
    26→  assignment sugar.
    27→- `cj_builder_call(ctx, scratch, label, args, count)`: loads integer argument
    28→  registers (up to the ABI limit), emits the proper call/bl, and optionally
    29→  preserves the return value via the scratch stack.
    30→- `cj_builder_call_unary(ctx, scratch, label, arg)`: loads the first argument
    31→  register, emits the right call/bl, and—when a scratch stack is supplied—moves
    32→  the return value into a fresh scratch slot.
    33→- `cj_resolve_label(ctx, module, label)`: convert a recorded label to a
    34→  callable pointer after finalization.
    35→
    36→## control flow
    37→
    38→- `cj_builder_if`, `cj_builder_else`, `cj_builder_endif`: structured
    39→  conditionals using `cj_condition`.
    40→- `cj_builder_loop_begin`, `loop_condition`, `loop_continue`, `loop_break`,
    41→  `loop_end`: generic loops.
    42→- `cj_builder_for_begin` / `cj_builder_for_end` (+ optional `for_continue`,
    43→  `for_break`): counting loops, pass counter, start, limit, step, and the exit
    44→  condition.
    45→
    46→## tiny example
    47→
    48→```c
    49→cj_ctx* cj = create_cj_ctx();
    50→cj_builder_frame frame;
    51→cj_builder_fn_prologue(cj, 0, &frame);
    52→
    53→cj_operand sum = cj_builder_scratch_reg(0);
    54→cj_builder_assign(cj, sum, cj_builder_zero_operand());
    55→
    56→cj_operand i = cj_builder_scratch_reg(1);
    57→cj_builder_for_loop loop = cj_builder_for_begin(
    58→    cj,
    59→    i,
    60→    cj_make_constant(1),
    61→    cj_builder_arg_int(cj, 0),
    62→    cj_make_constant(1),
    63→    CJ_COND_GE);
    64→
    65→cj_builder_add_assign(cj, sum, i);
    66→cj_builder_for_end(cj, &loop);
    67→
 
... (truncated)

Read — cj/tests/test_harness_builder.c
{
  "file_path": "cj/tests/test_harness_builder.c"
}
result
1→#include <assert.h>
     2→#include <stdint.h>
     3→#include <stdio.h>
     4→
     5→#include "builder.h"
     6→
     7→typedef int (*fn1_t)(int);
     8→typedef int (*fn2_t)(int, int);
     9→
    10→static void test_assign_and_add(void) {
    11→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    12→  cj_builder_frame frame;
    13→  cj_builder_fn_prologue(cj, 0, &frame);
    14→
    15→  cj_operand arg0 = cj_builder_arg_int(cj, 0);
    16→  cj_operand temp = cj_builder_scratch_reg(0);
    17→  cj_operand five = cj_make_constant(5);
    18→
    19→  cj_builder_assign(cj, temp, five);
    20→  cj_builder_add_assign(cj, temp, arg0);
    21→  cj_builder_return_value(cj, &frame, temp);
    22→
    23→  fn1_t fn = (fn1_t)create_cj_fn(cj);
    24→  assert(fn);
    25→  assert(fn(7) == 12);
    26→  assert(fn(10) == 15);
    27→
    28→  destroy_cj_fn(cj, (cj_fn)fn);
    29→  destroy_cj_ctx(cj);
    30→}
    31→
    32→static void test_scratch_helpers(void) {
    33→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    34→  cj_builder_frame frame;
    35→  cj_builder_fn_prologue(cj, 0, &frame);
    36→
    37→  cj_builder_scratch scratch;
    38→  cj_builder_scratch_init(&scratch);
    39→
    40→  cj_operand acc = cj_builder_scratch_acquire(&scratch);
    41→  cj_operand tmp = cj_builder_scratch_acquire(&scratch);
    42→
    43→  cj_builder_assign(cj, acc, cj_builder_arg_int(cj, 0));
    44→  cj_builder_assign(cj, tmp, cj_make_constant(7));
    45→  cj_builder_add_assign(cj, acc, tmp);
    46→
    47→  cj_builder_scratch_release(&scratch); // release tmp
    48→
    49→  cj_operand adjust = cj_builder_scratch_acquire(&scratch);
    50→  cj_builder_assign(cj, adjust, cj_make_constant(3));
    51→  cj_builder_sub_assign(cj, acc, adjust);
    52→
    53→  cj_builder_scratch_release(&scratch); // release adjust
    54→
    55→  cj_builder_return_value(cj, &frame, acc);
    56→  cj_builder_scratch_release(&scratch); // release acc
    57→
    58→  fn1_t fn = (fn1_t)create_cj_fn(cj);
    59→  assert(fn);
    60→  assert(fn(0) == 4);
    61→  assert(fn(5) == 9);
    62→
    63→  destroy_cj_fn(cj, (cj_fn)fn);
    64→  destroy_cj_ctx(cj);
    65→}
    66→
    67→static void test_call_helper(void) {
    68→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    69→  cj_label entry = cj_create_label(cj);
    70→  cj_label callee = cj_create_label(cj);
    71→
    72→  // Main function
    73→  cj_mark_label(cj, entry);
    74→  cj_builder_frame main_frame;
    75→  cj_builder_fn_prologue_with_link_save(cj, 0, &main_frame);
    76→  cj_builder_scratch scratch;
    77→  cj_builder_scratch_init(&scratch);
    78→
    79→  cj_operand arg = cj_builder_scratch_acquire(&scratch);
    80→  cj_builder_assign(cj, arg, cj_builder_arg_int(cj, 0));
    81→  cj_builder_add_assign(cj, arg, cj_make_constant(2));
    82→
    83→  cj_operand call_result = cj_builder_call_unary(cj, &scratch, callee, arg);
    84→  cj_builder_return_value(cj, &main_frame, call_result);
    85→  cj_builder_scratch_release(&scratch);
    86→
   
... (truncated)
Read — cj/examples/README.md
{
  "file_path": "cj/examples/README.md"
}
result
1→#examples
     2→
     3→Small programs that showcase `cj`:
     4→
     5→    - `simple.c`: minimal
     6→                  program(nop &ret)
     7→                      .- `add.c`: adds a constant to the first argument and returns it;
     8→demonstrates register operands and constants.- `fibonacci.c`: full control -
     9→    flow example(labels, branches, loops) emitted
    10→  with the low-level API.
    11→- `hl_fibonacci.c` – fibonacci again, but built entirely with the builder
    12→  helpers.
    13→- `simd.c` – x86 and arm simd vector addition loops, exercising memory operands
    14→  and floating-point registers.
    15→- `minilang.c` – a minimal Lisp (has addition, subtraction, functions).
    16→
    17→## building
    18→
    19→```bash
    20→#generic build(adjust CC for clang / gcc as needed)
    21→cc -std=c11 -O2 -Isrc examples/simple.c src/ctx.c -o simple_example
    22→./simple_example
    23→
    24→cc -std=c11 -O2 -Isrc examples/add.c src/ctx.c -o add_example
    25→./add_example  # returns exit code of the computed value (inspect via `echo $?` afterwards)
    26→
    27→cc -std=c11 -O2 -Isrc examples/fibonacci.c src/ctx.c -o fibonacci_example
    28→./fibonacci_example
    29→
    30→cc -std=c11 -O2 -Isrc examples/hl_fibonacci.c src/ctx.c -o hl_fibonacci_example
    31→./hl_fibonacci_example
    32→
    33→cc -std=c11 -O2 -Isrc examples/simd.c src/ctx.c -o simd_example
    34→./simd_example
    35→
    36→cc -std=c11 -O2 -Isrc examples/minilang.c src/ctx.c -o minilang
    37→./minilang "(def main (x) (sub (call inc x) 3)) (def inc (x) (add x 1))" 10
    38→```
    39→
    40→alternatively build the library and then add `-lcj -Lbin/` instead of the c file.
    41→

Let me look at a few more pieces — the test harnesses, a sample of the generated x86 instruction (like cj_add), and the GitHub CI.

Read — cj/tests/test_harness_arm64.c
{
  "file_path": "cj/tests/test_harness_arm64.c",
  "limit": 80

result
1→#ifndef _POSIX_C_SOURCE
     2→#define _POSIX_C_SOURCE 200809L
     3→#endif
     4→
     5→#if !defined(__aarch64__) && !defined(_M_ARM64)
     6→#error "This harness must be built on an ARM64 target."
     7→#endif
     8→
     9→#include <assert.h>
    10→#include <limits.h>
    11→#include <setjmp.h>
    12→#include <signal.h>
    13→#include <stdint.h>
    14→#include <stdio.h>
    15→#include <string.h>
    16→
    17→#include "ctx.h"
    18→#include "op.h"
    19→#include "register.h"
    20→
    21→static inline uint32_t mov_literal_chunk(uint16_t value, uint32_t shift) {
    22→  return ((shift / 16u) << 16) | (uint32_t)value;
    23→}
    24→
    25→static void test_mov_returns_second_argument(void) {
    26→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    27→
    28→  cj_operand x0 = cj_make_register("x0");
    29→  cj_operand x1 = cj_make_register("x1");
    30→
    31→  cj_mov(cj, x0, x1);
    32→  cj_ret(cj);
    33→
    34→  typedef long (*fn_t)(long, long);
    35→  fn_t fn = (fn_t)create_cj_fn(cj);
    36→
    37→  long res = fn(0, 99);
    38→
    39→  destroy_cj_fn(cj, (cj_fn)fn);
    40→  destroy_cj_ctx(cj);
    41→
    42→  assert(res == 99);
    43→}
    44→
    45→static void test_add_immediate(void) {
    46→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    47→
    48→  cj_operand x0 = cj_make_register("x0");
    49→  cj_operand imm = cj_make_constant(5);
    50→
    51→  cj_add(cj, x0, imm);
    52→  cj_ret(cj);
    53→
    54→  typedef long (*fn_t)(long);
    55→  fn_t fn = (fn_t)create_cj_fn(cj);
    56→
    57→  long res = fn(37);
    58→
    59→  destroy_cj_fn(cj, (cj_fn)fn);
    60→  destroy_cj_ctx(cj);
    61→
    62→  assert(res == 42);
    63→}
    64→
    65→static void test_add_register(void) {
    66→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    67→
    68→  cj_operand x0 = cj_make_register("x0");
    69→  cj_operand x1 = cj_make_register("x1");
    70→
    71→  cj_add(cj, x0, x1);
    72→  cj_ret(cj);
    73→
    74→  typedef long (*fn_t)(long, long);
    75→  fn_t fn = (fn_t)create_cj_fn(cj);
    76→
    77→  long res = fn(20, 22);
    78→
    79→  destroy_cj_fn(cj, (cj_fn)fn);
    80→  destroy_cj_ctx(cj);
Read — cj/tests/test_harness_x86.c
{
  "file_path": "cj/tests/test_harness_x86.c",
  "limit": 80
}
result
1→#include <assert.h>
     2→#include <stdio.h>
     3→#include <string.h>
     4→
     5→#if !defined(__x86_64__) && !defined(_M_X64)
     6→#error "This harness must be built on an x86-64 target."
     7→#endif
     8→
     9→#ifndef __x86_64__
    10→#define __x86_64__ 1
    11→#endif
    12→
    13→#include "ctx.h"
    14→#include "op.h"
    15→#include "register.h"
    16→
    17→static void test_add_constant(void) {
    18→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    19→
    20→  cj_operand rax = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rax"};
    21→  cj_operand rdi = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rdi"};
    22→  cj_operand constant = {.type = CJ_CONSTANT, .constant = 32};
    23→
    24→  cj_mov(cj, rax, rdi);
    25→  cj_add(cj, rax, constant);
    26→  cj_ret(cj);
    27→
    28→  typedef int (*fn_t)(int);
    29→  fn_t fn = (fn_t)create_cj_fn(cj);
    30→  int res = fn(10);
    31→  destroy_cj_fn(cj, (cj_fn)fn);
    32→  destroy_cj_ctx(cj);
    33→
    34→  assert(res == 42);
    35→}
    36→
    37→static void test_branch_loop(void) {
    38→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    39→
    40→  cj_operand rax = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rax"};
    41→  cj_operand rdi = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rdi"};
    42→  cj_operand rcx = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rcx"};
    43→  cj_operand zero = {.type = CJ_CONSTANT, .constant = 0};
    44→  cj_operand one = {.type = CJ_CONSTANT, .constant = 1};
    45→
    46→  cj_label loop = cj_create_label(cj);
    47→  cj_label done = cj_create_label(cj);
    48→
    49→  cj_mov(cj, rax, zero);
    50→  cj_mov(cj, rcx, one);
    51→
    52→  cj_mark_label(cj, loop);
    53→  cj_cmp(cj, rcx, rdi);
    54→  cj_jg(cj, done);
    55→  cj_add(cj, rax, rcx);
    56→  cj_add(cj, rcx, one);
    57→  cj_jmp(cj, loop);
    58→
    59→  cj_mark_label(cj, done);
    60→  cj_ret(cj);
    61→
    62→  typedef int (*fn_t)(int);
    63→  fn_t fn = (fn_t)create_cj_fn(cj);
    64→  int res = fn(5);
    65→  destroy_cj_fn(cj, (cj_fn)fn);
    66→  destroy_cj_ctx(cj);
    67→
    68→  assert(res == 15);
    69→}
    70→
    71→static void test_simd_add(void) {
    72→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    73→
    74→  cj_operand rdi = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rdi"};
    75→  cj_operand rsi = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rsi"};
    76→  cj_operand rdx = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rdx"};
    77→  cj_operand rcx = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "rcx"};
    78→  cj_operand xmm0 = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "xmm0"};
    79→  cj_operand xmm1 = {.type = CJ_REGISTER, .reg = "xmm1"};
    80→  cj_operand sixteen = {.type = CJ_CONSTANT, .constant = 16};

2628-    return;
2629-  }

2631-    int8_t dst_reg = parse_reg(dst.reg);

Write — <claude-internal>
{
  "file_path": "<claude-internal>",
  "content": "# cj - Minimal JIT Framework for C\n\n## Architecture Overview\n- **Purpose**: Minimal JIT framework in C with x86-64 and ARM64 backends\n- **Core**: `src/ctx.c` + `src/ctx.h` (runtime: mmap/mprotect, label/fixup system, byte emission)\n- **Operands**: `src/op.h` (register, constant, memory, register-list operand types; shifts, extends, conditions; `CJ_REG` macro)\n- **Registers**: `src/register.h` dispatches to `src/arch/{x86_64,arm64}/registers.h`\n- **Backends**: `src/arch/{x86_64,arm64}/backend.h` — auto-generated, 100k+ and 17k+ lines respectively\n- **Builder**: `src/builder.h` — higher-level helpers (prologue/epilogue, loops, if/else, scratch regs, calling convention)\n- **Only `.c` file**: `src/ctx.c` — everything else is header-only\n\n## Codegen Pipeline\n- x86: `codegen/x86_encoder.js` reads `asmdb` npm package → generates `backend.h`\n- ARM64: `codegen/arm64_encoder.js` reads `codegen/arm64_instructions_full.jsonl` → generates `backend.h`\n- Run via `make codegen` or `npm run generate`\n\n## Key Design Patterns\n- Registers identified by **string names** (e.g., `\"rax\"`, `\"w0\"`), parsed at emit time via `parse_reg()`\n- Operands are value types (`cj_operand` struct) with tagged union\n- Labels use forward-reference fixup system (ARM branch fixups + x86 relative fixups)\n- `create_cj_fn()` copies code buffer into mmap'd RX memory; prepends code size for cleanup\n- `cj_resolve_label()` maps labels to callable pointers within a finalized module\n- Builder layer is cross-platform via `#if defined(__x86_64__)` / `#elif defined(__aarch64__)`\n\n## Build\n- `make dev` (debug), `make all` (release) → `bin/libcj.so`\n- Examples: `cc -std=c11 -O2 -Isrc examples/X.c src/ctx.c -o X`\n- CI: GitHub Actions, tests on ubuntu x86 (gcc/clang), ubuntu arm64 (gcc/clang), macOS arm64 (clang)\n\n## Tests\n- `tests/test_harness_x86.c` / `tests/test_harness_arm64.c`: arch-specific instruction tests\n- `tests/test_harness_bu
... (truncated)
result
File created successfully at: <claude-internal>
claude

Here's what I've learned about cj:

What it is

A minimal JIT compilation framework in C with dual x86-64 and ARM64 backends. It lets you emit native machine instructions from C code at runtime, mmap them as executable, and call the result as a function pointer.

Architecture

Three layers, bottom to top:

1. Runtime (src/ctx.c — the only .c file besides tests/examples): Manages a growable byte buffer, labels with forward-reference fixups (ARM branch offsets and x86 relative displacements), and the mmap/mprotect dance to make emitted code executable. create_cj_fn() copies the buffer into RX memory with a prepended size header for later munmap cleanup.

2. Backends (src/arch/{x86_64,arm64}/backend.h): ~100k lines for x86 and ~18k lines for ARM64, auto-generated by Node.js scripts. The x86 codegen reads the asmdb npm package; the ARM64 codegen reads a JSONL instruction database. These are massive static inline functions like cj_add(), cj_mov(), cj_jmp() etc., each a big if-chain matching operand types/sizes and emitting the correct encoding (REX prefixes, ModR/M, SIB bytes for x86; fixed-width 32-bit encodings for ARM64).

3. Builder (src/builder.h): A thin cross-platform layer providing function prologue/epilogue (stack frame setup), ABI-correct argument/return registers, scratch register management, structured control flow (for-loops, while-loops, if/else), and a calling convention helper. All static inline, no separate .c file.

Key design choices

- Registers are strings: Operands carry register names as const char* (e.g., "rax", "w0"). The generated backends parse these at emit time via strcmp chains. Simple but not zero-cost.

- Operands are value types: cj_operand is a tagged-union struct passed by value, constructed with helpers like cj_make_register(), cj_make_constant(), cj_make_memory().

- Header-only everything except ctx.c: The entire encoder is #included — the backend selection happens via #if defined(__x86_64__) / #elif defined(__aarch64__).

- Label fixup system: Supports both forward and backward references. Forward refs record a fixup entry; when cj_mark_label() is called, it patches all pending fixups for that label.

- Multi-function modules: cj_resolve_label() converts a label to a callable pointer within a finalized module, enabling JIT'd code to call other JIT'd functions (used by minilang.c).

Examples showcase progression

- simple.c → nop + ret (minimal proof of life)

- add.c → constant addition (operand types)

- fibonacci.c → labels, branches, loops (low-level control flow)

- hl_fibonacci.c → same algorithm using builder helpers (cross-platform)

- simd.c → SSE movups/addps on x86, NEON ldr/add on ARM64

- minilang.c → a complete s-expression mini-language with lexer, parser, AST, and multi-function JIT compilation using the builder layer

human

I want to build a Lisp VM with JIT compilation for it. Syntax should be a bit like Clojure. We can start with a somewhat minimal language based on a bytecode VM in C without the JIT compiler (but with the knowledge that we want to add the JIT compiler later). The language should be written in a subdirectory and called Lisa.

result
Excellent! Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the cj repository. Let me provide a detailed analysis report.

## Comprehensive Exploration Report: cj Repository

I have thoroughly explored the cj JIT compilation framework. Here's a detailed breakdown of the five areas you requested:

---

### 1. BUILD CONVENTIONS

**Makefile Structure** (`cj/Makefile`):
- **Build targets**: Compiles to `libcj.so` (shared library) in `bin/` directory
- **Compiler flags**:
  - Base: `-std=c11 -O2 -Wno-gnu`
  - Dev mode: `-Werror -Wall -g -fPIC -DNDEBUG -Wfloat-equal -Wundef -Wwrite-strings -Wuninitialized -pedantic -O0`
  - Linker: `-shared` for shared library output
- **Source structure**: `src/*.c`, `src/arch/*.c`, `src/arch/*/*.c` (recursive architecture-specific files)
- **Key targets**:
  - `all`: Release build
  - `dev`: Development build with strict warnings and debug symbols
  - `codegen`: Regenerates x86 and ARM64 instruction encoders (Node.js-based)
  - `install/uninstall`: System-wide library installation
- **Example builds**: Examples compile as: `cc -std=c11 -O2 -Isrc examples/*.c src/ctx.c -o binary`
  - Include path: `-Isrc` for accessing public headers
  - Compile just context implementation: `src/ctx.c` contains all JIT runtime

---

### 2. MINILANG EXAMPLE ARCHITECTURE

**File**: `cj/examples/minilang.c` (329 lines)

**Language Specification** - Minimal Lisp variant supporting:
- Numbers (integers, including negative)
- Parameters (single parameter per function)
- Operators: `add`, `sub`, `call`
- S-expression syntax: `(op left right)` or `(call func arg)`
- Function definitions: `(def name (param) body)`

**Four-Phase Architecture**:

1. **Lexer** (`next_token`, `init_lexer`):
   - Tokenizes input string into: `TOK_LPAREN`, `TOK_RPAREN`, `TOK_IDENT`, `TOK_NUMBER`, `TOK_END`
   - Handles negative numbers via lookahead: `isdigit(cur[1])`
   - Stores token text in `char text[32]` and numeric value in `int value`
   - Error handling: `fprintf(stderr, "...")` + `exit(1)`

2. **Parser** (`parse_function`, `parse_expr`):
   - Recursive descent parser with `lexer` state management
   - AST node types: `NODE_NUM`, `NODE_PARAM`, `NODE_ADD`, `NODE_SUB`, `NODE_CALL`
   - Node structure includes: `kind`, `value` (for numbers), `name` (for functions), `left`/`right`/`arg` pointers
   - Validates parameter names in expressions
   - Node arena allocation: fixed `MAX_NODES = 256` with bounds checking

3. **Semantic Analysis** (`resolve_calls`):
   - Second pass iterates all nodes looking for `NODE_CALL` entries
   - Resolves function names to array indices stored in `node->target`
   - Reports unknown function names via stderr + exit

4. **Code Generation** (`emit_function`, `emit_expr`):
   - Uses builder helpers from `builder.h` for prologue/epilogue
   - Registers return values in scratch register pool (`cj_builder_scratch`)
   - Recursive expression emission with register allocation:
     - `NODE_NUM`: Loads constant into scratch register
     - `NODE
... (truncated)
⚙ Task

Now let me design the VM architecture.

Task
{
  "subagent_type": "Plan",
  "description": "Design Lisa bytecode VM",
  "prompt": "Design a bytecode VM implementation for a Clojure-inspired Lisp called \"Lisa\" that will live in a `lisa/` subdirectory of a C JIT framework repository. This is the first phase — bytecode interpreter only, but the design must accommodate future JIT compilation via the cj framework.\n\n## Requirements\n\n**Data types**: integers (int64_t), floats (double), strings, nil, booleans, symbols\n**Features**: def, fn, let, if, do, basic arithmetic/comparison, println, lambda closures (captured upvalues), list operations (cons, car, cdr, list)\n**Syntax**: Clojure-like s-expressions\n**Interface**: file/string eval from CLI (no REPL yet)\n**Build**: Own Makefile in lisa/ subdirectory, C11, POSIX\n\n**Future JIT considerations**: The bytecode design should be straightforward to lower to native code via cj. This means:\n- Prefer register-based or operand-stack operations that map cleanly to machine registers\n- Keep bytecode ops simple (no complex multi-step ops that would be hard to JIT)\n- The VM's value representation should be compatible with C calling conventions\n\n## Coding conventions (from the parent cj project)\n- C11, `#pragma once`, 2-space indent, 100-char line limit, LLVM-style clang-format\n- Naming: `lisa_` prefix for public API, snake_case, `LISA_` prefix for enum constants\n- Error handling: fprintf to stderr + return codes (not exit), NULL returns for failures\n- Memory: malloc/realloc with doubling growth, explicit cleanup functions\n- Headers are kept small and focused; implementation in .c files\n\n## Design the following\n\n1. **Value representation** (`value.h`): NaN-boxing or tagged union for lisa_value. Must efficiently represent all 6 types. Consider what works best for future JIT (where values will live in registers).\n\n2. **Object system** (`object.h/c`): Heap-allocated objects (strings, closures, lists, functions). GC considerations — what allocator/collector s
... (truncated)
result
Now I have a thorough understanding of the cj framework. Let me now design the complete Lisa VM implementation plan.

---

# Lisa Bytecode VM -- Complete Implementation Plan

## 1. Architecture Overview

Lisa is a Clojure-inspired Lisp that lives inside the `cj` JIT framework repository. Phase 1 is a bytecode interpreter; the bytecode design is chosen to be straightforward to lower to native code via cj in a future phase.

The pipeline is: **Source -> Lexer -> Parser (AST) -> Compiler (bytecode) -> VM (interpreter)**

**Key design decision: stack-based VM.** A stack-based VM is simpler to implement for a dynamically typed language with closures. Each bytecode operation has clear, predictable stack effects that a JIT can later translate to register operations using the cj builder's scratch register pool. Lua 5 is register-based, but Lua 5's registers are "virtual slots in the stack frame" -- our operand stack slots serve the same purpose and a future JIT can map them to physical registers. The critical thing is that each opcode does exactly one thing.

**Key design decision: NaN-boxing for values.** NaN-boxing packs all value types into a single `uint64_t`, which means values fit in machine registers and can be passed/returned using standard C calling conventions. This is exactly what we want for JIT: a `lisa_value` is just a `uint64_t` in a register.

---

## 2. File Layout

```
lisa/
  Makefile
  main.c            -- CLI entry point
  value.h           -- NaN-boxed value representation
  value.c           -- value utility functions (printing, type checks)
  object.h          -- heap-allocated object types (string, closure, list, function proto)
  object.c          -- object allocation, simple GC
  lexer.h           -- lexer interface
  lexer.c           -- tokenizer
  parser.h          -- parser interface (produces AST)
  parser.c          -- recursive descent parser
  ast.h             -- AST node definitions
  ast.c             -- AST construction/destruction helpers
  chunk.h           -- bytecode chunk (instruction stream + constant pool)
  chunk.c           -- chunk construction
  compiler.h        -- AST -> bytecode compiler interface
  compiler.c        -- compiler implementation
  vm.h              -- VM interface
  vm.c              -- bytecode interpreter
  debug.h           -- bytecode disassembler (optional but useful)
  debug.c           -- disassembler implementation
```

---

## 3. Value Representation (`value.h` / `value.c`)

### NaN-Boxing Scheme

IEEE 754 doubles use the bit pattern `0x7FF8000000000000` and above for quiet NaNs. We exploit the payload bits (51 bits) to encode non-double values. This gives us a single 64-bit word that is either a valid double or a tagged pointer/immediate.

```c
// value.h
#pragma once

#include <stdint.h>
#include <stdbool.h>

typedef uint64_t lisa_value;

// Bit layout:
//   If the top 13 bits are NOT 0x7FF8 or higher (NaN), it's a double.
//   If the top 13 bits == 0x7FFC (QNAN + sign bit cle
... (truncated)

Phase 1 is a bytecode interpreter in `lisa/` with its own Makefile. The bytecode design must be JIT-friendly so we can later lower ops to native code via cj. The language has Clojure-like syntax.\n\n**Types**: int64, double, string, nil, bool, symbol\n**Features**: def, fn, let, if, do, arithmetic/comparison, println, closures with upvalues, list ops (cons, car, cdr, list)\n**Interface**: CLI with file eval (`lisa foo.lisa`) and string eval (`lisa -e \"(+ 1 2)\"`)\n\n## Architecture\n\n```\nSource -> Lexer (tokens) -> Parser (AST) -> Compiler (bytecode) -> VM (interpreter)\n```\n\n**Stack-based VM** with NaN-boxed values (`uint64_t`) and Lua-style upvalue capture for closures. Each bytecode op does exactly one thing, making future JIT translation to cj instructions straightforward.\n\n## File Layout\n\n```\nlisa/\n  Makefile\n  main.c              -- CLI entry point\n  value.h / value.c   -- NaN-boxed value representation\n  object.h / object.c -- heap objects (string, function, closure, upvalue, list) + mark-and-sweep GC\n  lexer.h / lexer.c   -- tokenizer\n  ast.h / ast.c       -- AST node types and construction\n  parser.h / parser.c -- recursive descent parser\n  chunk.h / chunk.c   -- bytecode chunk (instruction stream + constant pool)\n  compiler.h / .c     -- AST -> bytecode with scope resolution and upvalue capture\n  vm.h / vm.c         -- bytecode dispatch loop, call frames, upvalue runtime\n  debug.h / debug.c   -- bytecode disassembler\n```\n\n## Key Design Decisions\n\n### NaN-boxing (`value.h`)\n\nAll values are `uint64_t`. Doubles are stored as-is. Non-double values use the quiet NaN payload space:\n\n| Tag (bits 50:48) | Type | Payload |\n|---|---|---|\n| 000 | nil | none |\n| 001 | bool | bit 0 |\n| 010 | int | 48-bit sign-extended integer |\n| 011 | object | 48-bit pointer |\

session 2. building lisa: bytecode vm

human

Implement the following plan:

Lisa: Bytecode VM for a Clojure-inspired Lisp

Context

Build a Lisp called Lisa inside the cj JIT framework repo. Phase 1 is a bytecode interpreter in lisa/ with its own Makefile. The bytecode design must be JIT-friendly so we can later lower ops to native code via cj. The language has Clojure-like syntax.

Types: int64, double, string, nil, bool, symbol

Features: def, fn, let, if, do, arithmetic/comparison, println, closures with upvalues, list ops (cons, car, cdr, list)

Interface: CLI with file eval (lisa foo.lisa) and string eval (lisa -e "(+ 1 2)")

Architecture

Source -> Lexer (tokens) -> Parser (AST) -> Compiler (bytecode) -> VM (interpreter)

Stack-based VM with NaN-boxed values (uint64_t) and Lua-style upvalue capture for closures. Each bytecode op does exactly one thing, making future JIT translation to cj instructions straightforward.

File Layout

lisa/
  Makefile
  main.c              -- CLI entry point
  value.h / value.c   -- NaN-boxed value representation
  object.h / object.c -- heap objects (string, function, closure, upvalue, list) + mark-and-sweep GC
  lexer.h / lexer.c   -- tokenizer
  ast.h / ast.c       -- AST node types and construction
  parser.h / parser.c -- recursive descent parser
  chunk.h / chunk.c   -- bytecode chunk (instruction stream + constant pool)
  compiler.h / .c     -- AST -> bytecode with scope resolution and upvalue capture
  vm.h / vm.c         -- bytecode dispatch loop, call frames, upvalue runtime
  debug.h / debug.c   -- bytecode disassembler

Key Design Decisions

NaN-boxing (value.h)

All values are uint64_t. Doubles are stored as-is. Non-double values use the quiet NaN payload space:

| Tag (bits 50:48) | Type | Payload | |---|---|---| | 000 | nil | none | | 001 | bool | bit 0 | | 010 | int | 48-bit sign-extended integer | | 011 | object | 48-bit pointer |

Untagged (not a NaN) = double. This means values fit in machine registers for future JIT.

Object system (object.h)

Common header (lisa_obj) with type tag, GC mark bit, and intrusive next pointer. Types:

- lisa_obj_string — interned, with FNV-1a hash, flexible array member for chars

- lisa_obj_function — compiled bytecode prototype (arity, upvalue_count, chunk, name)

- lisa_obj_closure — function + array of captured upvalue pointers

- lisa_obj_upvalue — Lua-style: location pointer (into stack while open, into closed field after closing)

- lisa_obj_list — cons cell (car + cdr)

- lisa_obj_native — C function wrapper for built-ins as first-class values

GC: simple mark-and-sweep, triggered when bytes_allocated > next_gc (threshold doubles after each collection).

Bytecode opcodes (chunk.h)

Single-byte opcode + 0-2 byte operands. Key ops:

- Stack: CONSTANT [idx], NIL, TRUE, FALSE, POP

- Variables: GET_LOCAL [slot], SET_LOCAL [slot], GET_UPVALUE [idx], SET_UPVALUE [idx], GET_GLOBAL [idx], DEF_GLOBAL [idx]

- Arithmetic: ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, MOD, NEGATE (pop operands, push result)

- Comparison: EQUAL, NOT_EQUAL, LESS, LESS_EQUAL, GREATER, GREATER_EQUAL

- Logic: NOT

- Control flow: JUMP [lo][hi], JUMP_IF_FALSE [lo][hi], LOOP [lo][hi]

- Functions: CLOSURE [idx] [is_local, index]..., CALL [argc], RETURN

- Upvalues: CLOSE_UPVALUE

- Lists: CONS, CAR, CDR, LIST [n]

- I/O: PRINTLN [argc]

Compiler (compiler.c)

Walks AST, emits bytecode. Locals tracked in a fixed array with scope depth. Upvalue resolution walks enclosing compilers (Lua algorithm). Built-in operators (+, -, cons, etc.) compile to dedicated opcodes when in call position; when used as values they resolve as globals pointing to native function wrappers.

VM (vm.c)

Fixed-size value stack + call frame stack. switch-dispatch loop. Call frames store closure pointer, instruction pointer, and stack base. CALL pushes a new frame; RETURN pops it and restores the caller. Upvalue capture/closing follows Lua's open-upvalue linked list design.

Implementation Order

Each step produces something testable before moving on:

1. value.h + value.c — NaN-boxing primitives, printing, equality

2. chunk.h + chunk.c — bytecode container + constant pool

3. debug.h + debug.c — disassembler (verify bytecode visually)

4. object.h + object.c — heap objects, allocation, string interning, free-all cleanup (full GC deferred)

5. lexer.h + lexer.c — tokenizer

6. ast.h + ast.c — AST node types

7. parser.h + parser.c — recursive descent parser

8. compiler.h + compiler.c — incremental: literals/arithmetic first, then locals/let, then if/do, then fn/closures

9. vm.h + vm.c — incremental: arithmetic dispatch first, then globals, locals, control flow, calls, closures, lists

10. main.c + Makefile — wire together, add GC

Makefile

CC ?= cc
CFLAGS = -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wno-gnu -O2
DEVFLAGS = -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wno-gnu -Werror -g -O0
SOURCES = main.c value.c object.c lexer.c parser.c ast.c chunk.c compiler.c vm.c debug.c
TARGET = bin/lisa

.PHONY: all dev clean

all:
	mkdir -p bin
	$(CC) $(SOURCES) -o $(TARGET) $(CFLAGS) -lm

dev:
	mkdir -p bin
	$(CC) $(SOURCES) -o $(TARGET) $(DEVFLAGS) -lm

clean:
	rm -rf bin

Example Lisa Program

(def factorial
  (fn [n]
    (if (<= n 1)
      1
      (* n (factorial (- n 1))))))

(def make-adder (fn [x] (fn [y] (+ x y))))
(def add5 (make-adder 5))

(println (factorial 10))    ;; 3628800
(println (add5 3))          ;; 8
(println (car (list 1 2 3))) ;; 1

(let [x 10 y 20]
  (println (+ x y)))        ;; 30

Verification

After implementation, test with:

1. cd lisa && make dev — compiles cleanly with strict warnings

2. bin/lisa -e "(println (+ 1 2))" — prints 3

3. bin/lisa -e "(println ((fn [x] (+ x 1)) 41))" — prints 42 (closure call)

4. bin/lisa -e "(def f (fn [x] (fn [y] (+ x y)))) (println ((f 5) 3))" — prints 8 (upvalue capture)

5. bin/lisa -e "(println (car (list 1 2 3)))" — prints 1 (list ops)

6. Recursive factorial from example above — tests recursion + conditionals

Write — lisa/value.h
{
  "file_path": "lisa/value.h",
  "content": "#ifndef LISA_VALUE_H\n#define LISA_VALUE_H\n\n#include <stdbool.h>\n#include <stdint.h>\n#include <stdio.h>\n\n/*\n * NaN-boxed value representation.\n *\n * IEEE 754 double: if bits [62:52] are all 1 and bit 51 is 1, it's a quiet NaN.\n * We use the remaining payload bits for non-double values.\n *\n * Layout:\n *   Double: any bit pattern that is NOT a quiet NaN with our tag prefix\n *   Tagged: [sign=1][exp=0x7FF][quiet=1][tag 50:48][payload 47:0]\n *\n * Tags (bits 50:48):\n *   000 = nil\n *   001 = bool (payload bit 0)\n *   010 = int  (48-bit sign-extended integer)\n *   011 = object pointer (48-bit)\n */\n\ntypedef uint64_t lisa_value;\n\n/* The quiet NaN mask: sign(1) + exponent(0x7FF) + quiet(1) = bits 63,62:52,51 */\n#define QNAN    ((uint64_t)0x7FFC000000000000)\n#define SIGN_BIT ((uint64_t)0x8000000000000000)\n\n/* Tag values shifted into bits 50:48 */\n#define TAG_NIL    ((uint64_t)0x0000000000000000)  /* 000 */\n#define TAG_BOOL   ((uint64_t)0x0001000000000000)  /* 001 */\n#define TAG_INT    ((uint64_t)0x0002000000000000)  /* 010 */\n#define TAG_OBJ    ((uint64_t)0x0003000000000000)  /* 011 */\n\n#define TAG_MASK   ((uint64_t)0x0003000000000000)\n#define PAYLOAD_MASK ((uint64_t)0x0000FFFFFFFFFFFF) /* 48 bits */\n\n/* Construct values */\n#define LISA_NIL        (QNAN | TAG_NIL)\n#define LISA_TRUE       (QNAN | TAG_BOOL | 1)\n#define LISA_FALSE      (QNAN | TAG_BOOL | 0)\n#define LISA_BOOL(b)    ((b) ? LISA_TRUE : LISA_FALSE)\n#define LISA_INT(i)     (QNAN | TAG_INT | ((uint64_t)(i) & PAYLOAD_MASK))\n#define LISA_OBJ(ptr)   (QNAN | TAG_OBJ | ((uint64_t)(uintptr_t)(ptr) & PAYLOAD_MASK))\n\nstatic inline lisa_value lisa_double(double d) {\n    union { double d; uint64_t u; } conv;\n    conv.d = d;\n    return conv.u;\n}\n\n/* Type checks */\n#define IS_NIL(v)    ((v) == LISA_NIL)\n#define IS_BOOL(v)   (((v) & (QNAN | TAG_MASK)) == (QNAN | TAG_BOOL))\n#define IS_INT(v)    (((v) & (QNAN | TAG_MASK)) == (QNAN |
... (truncated)

File created successfully at: lisa/value.h
Write — lisa/value.c
{
  "file_path": "lisa/value.c",
  "content": "#include \"value.h\"\n#include \"object.h\"\n#include <stdio.h>\n#include <string.h>\n\nvoid lisa_fprint_value(FILE *f, lisa_value value) {\n    if (IS_NIL(value)) {\n        fprintf(f, \"nil\");\n    } else if (IS_BOOL(value)) {\n        fprintf(f, AS_BOOL(value) ? \"true\" : \"false\");\n    } else if (IS_INT(value)) {\n        fprintf(f, \"%lld\", (long long)AS_INT(value));\n    } else if (IS_DOUBLE(value)) {\n        double d = AS_DOUBLE(value);\n        /* Print integers without decimal point */\n        if (d == (int64_t)d && d >= -1e15 && d <= 1e15) {\n            fprintf(f, \"%.1f\", d);\n        } else {\n            fprintf(f, \"%g\", d);\n        }\n    } else if (IS_OBJ(value)) {\n        lisa_print_object(f, value);\n    } else {\n        fprintf(f, \"<unknown>\");\n    }\n}\n\nvoid lisa_print_value(lisa_value value) {\n    lisa_fprint_value(stdout, value);\n}\n\nbool lisa_values_equal(lisa_value a, lisa_value b) {\n    if (IS_DOUBLE(a) && IS_DOUBLE(b)) {\n        return AS_DOUBLE(a) == AS_DOUBLE(b);\n    }\n    if (IS_INT(a) && IS_DOUBLE(b)) {\n        return (double)AS_INT(a) == AS_DOUBLE(b);\n    }\n    if (IS_DOUBLE(a) && IS_INT(b)) {\n        return AS_DOUBLE(a) == (double)AS_INT(b);\n    }\n    /* For NaN-boxed values, bit equality works for nil, bool, int, and\n     * interned strings (same pointer = same string). */\n    return a == b;\n}\n\nbool lisa_is_falsey(lisa_value value) {\n    if (IS_NIL(value)) return true;\n    if (IS_BOOL(value)) return !AS_BOOL(value);\n    return false;\n}\n"
}

  "file_path": "lisa/chunk.h",
  "content": "#ifndef LISA_CHUNK_H\n#define LISA_CHUNK_H\n\n#include \"value.h\"\n#include <stdint.h>\n\ntypedef enum {\n    OP_CONSTANT,      /* [idx]          push constants[idx] */\n    OP_NIL,           /*                push nil */\n    OP_TRUE,          /*                push true */\n    OP_FALSE,         /*                push false */\n    OP_POP,           /*                pop top */\n\n    OP_GET_LOCAL,     /* [slot]         push stack[base+slot] */\n    OP_SET_LOCAL,     /* [slot]         stack[base+slot] = peek */\n    OP_GET_UPVALUE,   /* [idx]          push *upvalues[idx]->location */\n    OP_SET_UPVALUE,   /* [idx]          *upvalues[idx]->location = peek */\n    OP_GET_GLOBAL,    /* [idx]          push globals[constants[idx]] */\n    OP_DEF_GLOBAL,    /* [idx]          globals[constants[idx]] = pop */\n\n    OP_ADD,\n    OP_SUB,\n    OP_MUL,\n    OP_DIV,\n    OP_MOD,\n    OP_NEGATE,\n\n    OP_EQUAL,\n    OP_NOT_EQUAL,\n    OP_LESS,\n    OP_LESS_EQUAL,\n    OP_GREATER,\n    OP_GREATER_EQUAL,\n\n    OP_NOT,\n\n    OP_JUMP,          /* [lo][hi]       ip += offset */\n    OP_JUMP_IF_FALSE, /* [lo][hi]       if falsey(pop) ip += offset */\n    OP_LOOP,          /* [lo][hi]       ip -= offset */\n\n    OP_CLOSURE,       /* [idx] then pairs of [is_local, index] */\n    OP_CALL,          /* [argc]         call top function with argc args */\n    OP_RETURN,        /*                return top of stack */\n\n    OP_CLOSE_UPVALUE, /*                close upvalue at stack top */\n\n    OP_CONS,          /*                push cons(pop2, pop1) */\n    OP_CAR,           /*                push car(pop) */\n    OP_CDR,           /*                push cdr(pop) */\n    OP_LIST,          /* [n]            pop n items, build list */\n\n    OP_PRINTLN,       /* [argc]         print argc values with spaces, newline */\n} lisa_op;\n\n/* Dynamic array of constants */\ntypedef struct {\n    int count;\n    int capacity;\n    lisa_value *va
