{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/vm.c",
  "old_string": "            /* JIT dispatch for tail calls */\n            if (closure->function->jit_code) {\n                lisa_jit_fn jit_fn = (lisa_jit_fn)closure->function->jit_code;\n                lisa_value result = jit_fn(vm, frame->closure, frame->slots);\n                close_upvalues(vm, frame->slots);\n                vm->frame_count--;\n                if (vm->frame_count == base_frame) {\n                    if (base_frame == 0) pop(vm);\n                    else {\n                        vm->stack_top = frame->slots;\n                        push(vm, result);\n                    }\n                    return INTERPRET_OK;\n                }\n                vm->stack_top = frame->slots;\n                push(vm, result);\n                frame = &vm->frames[vm->frame_count - 1];\n            }",
  "new_string": "            /* JIT dispatch for tail calls */\n            if (closure->function->jit_code) {\n                lisa_jit_fn jit_fn = (lisa_jit_fn)closure->function->jit_code;\n                lisa_value result = jit_fn(vm, frame->closure, frame->slots);\n                if (IS_TAIL_PENDING(result))\n                    result = jit_trampoline(vm, result);\n                close_upvalues(vm, frame->slots);\n                vm->frame_count--;\n                if (vm->frame_count == base_frame) {\n                    if (base_frame == 0) pop(vm);\n                    else {\n                        vm->stack_top = frame->slots;\n                        push(vm, result);\n                    }\n                    return INTERPRET_OK;\n                }\n                vm->stack_top = frame->slots;\n                push(vm, result);\n                frame = &vm->frames[vm->frame_count - 1];\n            }"
}

⚙ Grep, Read, Edit

Now I need to fix the forward declaration issue — jit_trampoline is used in lisa_run but defined later. Let me add a forward declaration. Let me find where the jit_fn typedef is:

  "pattern": "typedef.*lisa_jit_fn",
  "path": "lisa/vm.c",
  "output_mode": "content",
  "-n": true
}
result

1174→            }

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/jit.c",
  "old_string": "            /* Non-self tail call: use helper */\n            cj_mark_label(ctx, not_self);\n            emit_call_vm_int(ctx, (void *)lisa_jit_tail_call_helper, argc);\n            cj_mov(ctx, reg(REG_RET), reg(REG_RET));\n            emit_epilogue(ctx);",
  "new_string": "            /* Non-self tail call: return sentinel for trampoline */\n            cj_mark_label(ctx, not_self);\n            emit_load_imm64(ctx, REG_RET, LISA_TAIL_PENDING(argc));\n            emit_epilogue(ctx);"
}

⚙ Bash

Good. Now let me build:

Bash — Build lisa
{
  "command": "make -C lisa dev 2>&1",
  "description": "Build lisa"
}
result
mkdir -p bin
cc main.c value.c object.c lexer.c parser.c ast.c chunk.c compiler.c vm.c debug.c jit.c ../src/ctx.c -o bin/lisa -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wno-gnu -Werror -g -O0 -I../src -lm
⚙ Bash

Clean build. Now let me run all verification tests:

Bash — Test mutual recursion at 100K depth
{
  "command": "lisa/bin/lisa -e '(def e (fn [n] (if (= n 0) true (o (- n 1))))) (def o (fn [n] (if (= n 0) false (e (- n 1))))) (println (e 100000))' 2>&1",
  "description": "Test mutual recursion at 100K depth"
}
result
Exit code 139
⚙ Bash

Unknown opcode 232.
⚙ Grep, Bash

It crashes even at depth 10 — "Unknown opcode" means memory corruption. Let me check what the JIT does for OP_CALL to understand the full flow:

Grep — case OP_CALL:
{
  "pattern": "case OP_CALL:",
  "path": "lisa/jit.c",
  "output_mode": "content",
  "-n": true,
  "-A": 25
}
result
753:        case OP_CALL: case OP_TAIL_CALL:
754-        case OP_LIST: case OP_PRINTLN:
755-            i += 2;
756-            break;
757-        default:
758-            i += 1;
759-            break;
760-        }
761-    }
762-
763-    for (i = 0; i < len; i++) {
764-        if (map->is_target[i])
765-            map->labels[i] = cj_create_label(ctx);
766-    }
767-}
768-
769-static void free_label_map(label_map *map) {
770-    free(map->labels);
771-    free(map->is_target);
772-}
773-
774-/* ===== Main JIT Compilation ===== */
775-
776-bool lisa_jit_compile(lisa_vm *vm, lisa_obj_function *fn) {
777-    (void)vm;
778-
--
1144:        case OP_CALL: {
1145-            int argc = chunk->code[i + 1];
1146-            cache_flush(ctx, &cache);
1147-            emit_call_vm_int(ctx, (void *)lisa_jit_call_helper, argc);
1148-            /* Result already pushed to memory stack by helper */
1149-            i += 2;
1150-            break;
1151-        }
1152-
1153-        case OP_TAIL_CALL: {
1154-            int argc = chunk->code[i + 1];
1155-            cache_flush(ctx, &cache);
1156-            emit_sync_stack_top(ctx);
1157-
1158-            /* Runtime self-call check: compare callee with current closure.
1159-               Callee on stack is NaN-boxed (QNAN|TAG_OBJ|ptr), but REG_CLOSURE
1160-               is a raw pointer. NaN-box REG_CLOSURE into TMP2 for comparison. */
1161-            cj_label not_self = cj_create_label(ctx);
1162-            int32_t callee_off = (int32_t)(-8 * (argc + 1));
1163-            emit_load64(ctx, REG_TMP1, REG_STKTOP, callee_off);
1164-            emit_load_imm64(ctx, REG_TMP2, QNAN | TAG_OBJ);
1165-            emit_or(ctx, REG_TMP2, REG_CLOSURE);
1166-            cj_cmp(ctx, reg(REG_TMP1), reg(REG_TMP2));
1167-            EMIT_JNE(ctx, not_self);
1168-
1169-            /* Self-call: move args to slots, reset stack, jump to body */
Bash — Test mutual recursion without JIT
{
  "command": "lisa/bin/lisa --no-jit -e '(def e (fn [n] (if (= n 0) true (o (- n 1))))) (def o (fn [n] (if (= n 0) false (e (- n 1))))) (println (e 10))' 2>&1",
  "description": "Test mutual recursion without JIT"
}
result
true
⚙ Read, Edit ×2, Bash

Works without JIT. Let me add temporary debug output to the trampoline to see what's happening:

Read — lisa/vm.c
{
  "file_path": "lisa/vm.c",
  "offset": 718,
  "limit": 48
}
result
718→}
   719→
   720→/* Handle pending JIT tail calls iteratively (trampoline).
   721→   Called when a JIT function returns LISA_TAIL_PENDING(argc). */
   722→static lisa_value jit_trampoline(lisa_vm *vm, lisa_value result) {
   723→    while (IS_TAIL_PENDING(result)) {
   724→        int argc = TAIL_PENDING_ARGC(result);
   725→        lisa_value callee = vm->stack_top[-1 - argc];
   726→
   727→        if (IS_OBJ(callee) && OBJ_TYPE(callee) == OBJ_NATIVE) {
   728→            call_value(vm, callee, argc);
   729→            return vm->stack_top[-1];
   730→        }
   731→
   732→        if (!IS_OBJ(callee) || OBJ_TYPE(callee) != OBJ_CLOSURE) {
   733→            runtime_error(vm, "Can only call functions and closures.");
   734→            return LISA_NIL;
   735→        }
   736→
   737→        lisa_obj_closure *closure = AS_CLOSURE(callee);
   738→        if (argc != closure->function->arity) {
   739→            runtime_error(vm, "Expected %d arguments but got %d.",
   740→                          closure->function->arity, argc);
   741→            return LISA_NIL;
   742→        }
   743→
   744→        /* Reuse the current top frame */
   745→        lisa_call_frame *frame = &vm->frames[vm->frame_count - 1];
   746→        close_upvalues(vm, frame->slots);
   747→
   748→        lisa_value *src = vm->stack_top - argc - 1;
   749→        memmove(frame->slots, src, (size_t)(argc + 1) * sizeof(lisa_value));
   750→        vm->stack_top = frame->slots + argc + 1;
   751→
   752→        frame->closure = closure;
   753→        frame->ip = closure->function->chunk.code;
   754→
   755→        if (closure->function->jit_code) {
   756→            lisa_jit_fn jit_fn = (lisa_jit_fn)closure->function->jit_code;
   757→            result = jit_fn(vm, frame->closure, frame->slots);
   758→            /* If result is TAIL_PENDING, loop continues */
   759→        } else {
   760→            int target_depth = vm->frame_count - 1;
   761→            lisa_run(vm, target_depth);
   762→            return vm->stack_top[-1];
   763→        }
   764→    }
   765→    return result;
Edit — lisa/vm.c
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/vm.c",
  "old_string": "static lisa_value jit_trampoline(lisa_vm *vm, lisa_value result) {\n    while (IS_TAIL_PENDING(result)) {\n        int argc = TAIL_PENDING_ARGC(result);\n        lisa_value callee = vm->stack_top[-1 - argc];",
  "new_string": "static lisa_value jit_trampoline(lisa_vm *vm, lisa_value result) {\n    int iter = 0;\n    while (IS_TAIL_PENDING(result)) {\n        int argc = TAIL_PENDING_ARGC(result);\n        lisa_value callee = vm->stack_top[-1 - argc];\n        fprintf(stderr, \"[trampoline] iter=%d argc=%d callee=0x%llx fc=%d\\n\",\n                iter++, argc, (unsigned long long)callee, vm->frame_count);"
}

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/vm.c",
  "old_string": "        if (closure->function->jit_code) {\n            lisa_jit_fn jit_fn = (lisa_jit_fn)closure->function->jit_code;\n            result = jit_fn(vm, frame->closure, frame->slots);\n            /* If result is TAIL_PENDING, loop continues */\n        } else {\n            int target_depth = vm->frame_count - 1;\n            lisa_run(vm, target_depth);\n            return vm->stack_top[-1];\n        }\n    }\n    return result;",

772→    return result;
   773→}
   774→
   775→lisa_value lisa_jit_call_helper(lisa_vm *vm, int argc) {
   776→    lisa_value callee = vm->stack_top[-1 - argc];
   777→    if (!call_value(vm, callee, argc)) {
   778→        return LISA_NIL; /* error already reported */
   779→    }
   780→    /* Check if callee was a native (call_value already handled it) */
   781→    if (IS_OBJ(callee) && OBJ_TYPE(callee) == OBJ_NATIVE) {
   782→        return vm->stack_top[-1]; /* result already on stack */
   783→    }
   784→    /* Closure call — dispatch to JIT or interpreter */
   785→    lisa_call_frame *frame = &vm->frames[vm->frame_count - 1];
   786→    lisa_value result;
   787→    if (frame->closure->function->jit_code) {
   788→        lisa_jit_fn jit_fn = (lisa_jit_fn)frame->closure->function->jit_code;

   790→        if (IS_TAIL_PENDING(result))
   791→            result = jit_trampoline(vm, result);
   792→    } else {
   793→        int target_depth = vm->frame_count - 1;
   794→        lisa_run(vm, target_depth);
   795→        result = vm->stack_top[-1];
   796→    }

The trampoline enters but never reaches the JIT call or returns. Let me add more fine-grained debug:

[trampoline] iter=0 argc=1 callee=0x7fff6000008d0a50 fc=2 IS_OBJ=1

[trampoline] about to close upvalues and memmove
Unknown opcode 192.
Unknown opcode 232.
⚙ Edit

The problem is clear: jit=0x0 — the target closure o has never been JIT-compiled because it's being reached through the trampoline, not through call_closure. The trampoline falls to the interpreter, which recursively enters the trampoline again. Let me fix this by triggering JIT compilation in the trampoline:

Edit — lisa/vm.c
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/vm.c",
  "old_string": "        if (closure->function->jit_code) {\n            lisa_jit_fn jit_fn = (lisa_jit_fn)closure->function->jit_code;\n            fprintf(stderr, \"[trampoline] calling jit for arity=%d\\n\", closure->function->arity);\n            result = jit_fn(vm, frame->closure, frame->slots);\n            fprintf(stderr, \"[trampoline] jit returned 0x%llx tail=%d\\n\",\n                    (unsigned long long)result, IS_TAIL_PENDING(result));\n            /* If result is TAIL_PENDING, loop continues */\n        } else {\n            int target_depth = vm->frame_count - 1;\n            lisa_run(vm, target_depth);\n            return vm->stack_top[-1];\n        }",
  "new_string": "        /* JIT-compile the target if needed */\n        if (!closure->function->jit_code && vm->jit_enabled) {\n            lisa_jit_compile(vm, closure->function);\n        }\n\n        if (closure->function->jit_code) {\n            lisa_jit_fn jit_fn = (lisa_jit_fn)closure->function->jit_code;\n            result = jit_fn(vm, frame->closure, frame->slots);\n            /* If result is TAIL_PENDING, loop continues */\n        } else {\n            /* JIT compilation failed; use interpreter (no trampoline risk\n               since this function can't produce TAIL_PENDING) */\n            int target_depth = vm->frame_count - 1;\n            lisa_run(vm, target_depth);\n            return vm->stack_top[-1];\n        }"
}
result
The file lisa/vm.c has been updated successfully.
⚙ Grep, Read

}

    12→                                  lisa_value *slots);
⚙ Edit ×2

  "new_string": "    return result;"
}

claude

Clean — no remaining references to the removed function. Let me do a final summary of what was fixed:

Summary

Bug fixed: NaN-boxing mismatch in self-call comparison (jit.c)

The self-call optimization in OP_TAIL_CALL compared the NaN-boxed callee value from the stack (QNAN | TAG_OBJ | ptr) with the raw closure pointer in REG_CLOSURE. These could never be equal, so the self-call path was never taken. Fixed by NaN-boxing REG_CLOSURE into a temp register before comparing.

Trampoline for non-self tail calls (vm.c, vm.h)

Non-self tail calls (e.g., mutual recursion e→o→e→o...) previously caused C stack overflow because JIT functions recursively called lisa_jit_tail_call_helper which recursively called JIT functions.

Solution:

- JIT returns a sentinel value LISA_TAIL_PENDING(argc) (with 0xDEAD in the top 16 bits — an invalid NaN-boxing tag) instead of calling the helper

- A new jit_trampoline() function in vm.c iteratively handles the tail-call chain

- All JIT call sites check for the sentinel

- The trampoline eagerly JIT-compiles target functions that haven't been compiled yet

- Removed lisa_jit_tail_call_helper (no longer needed)

Results

- All verification tests pass (factorial, fibonacci, tail recursion 1M, mutual recursion 100K, closures, list ops)

- ~2x speedup: fib(35) runs in 0.53s (JIT) vs 1.05s (interpreter) with -O2

{
  "subagent_type": "Explore",

(prologue/epilogue, scratch registers, calling convention, control flow, etc.)\n2. What register operations are available? (mov, add, sub, cmp, shifts, bitwise ops, loads/stores with offsets, immediate loading)\n3. What's missing that a JIT compiler would need? Specifically look for:\n   - Logical shift right (LSR/SHR) by immediate\n   - Logical shift left (LSL/SHL) by immediate\n   - Arithmetic shift right (ASR/SAR) by immediate\n   - Bitwise OR of two registers\n   - Bitwise AND with immediate (for masking)\n   - Loading 64-bit immediates into registers\n   - Memory loads/stores with register+offset addressing and large offsets\n   - Conditional set (setcc/cset) - producing a 0/1 from a comparison\n   - Compare + branch patterns\n   - STP/LDP (store/load pair) or equivalent\n\nAlso look at src/op.h to see what operand types exist (reg, imm, mem with offset, etc.) and whether memory operands support negative offsets or large offsets.\n\nBe very thorough - read the full builder.h and relevant parts of op.h.",
  "description": "Explore cj builder capabilities"
}
result
Excellent! I have all the information I need. Let me create the final comprehensive report:

---

## CJ Builder Layer Comprehensive Analysis

I've thoroughly analyzed the cj builder layer (`src/builder.h`) and the operand system (`src/op.h`), along with the architecture-specific backends. Here's the complete breakdown:

### 1. **Cross-Platform Abstractions Provided**

The builder layer provides excellent cross-platform abstraction:

**Function Prologue/Epilogue:**
- `cj_builder_fn_prologue()` - Sets up function frame (16-byte aligned stack)
- `cj_builder_fn_prologue_with_link_save()` - Saves link register on ARM
- `cj_builder_fn_epilogue()` - Cleans up and restores registers
- `cj_builder_return()` - Epilogue + RET instruction

**Calling Convention Abstraction:**
- `cj_builder_arg_int(index)` - Returns argument register by platform
  - **X86_64:** edi, esi, edx, ecx, r8d, r9d (6 args)
  - **ARM64:** w0-w7 (8 args)
- `cj_builder_return_reg()` - Returns eax (X86) or w0 (ARM)
- `cj_builder_call()` - Automatically sets arguments and invokes function

**Stack Frame Management:**
- Handles x86 RBP-based frames vs ARM SP-based frames
- Auto-alignment to 16 bytes
- Tracks link register save location on ARM

**Scratch Register Allocation:**
- `cj_builder_scratch_*` API with depth tracking (LIFO stack)
- 6 scratch registers available on both architectures:
  - X86: r8d, r9d, r10d, r11d, ecx, edx
  - ARM: w2, w3, w4, w5, w6, w7

**Control Flow Abstractions:**
- **Loops:** `cj_builder_loop_begin/condition/continue/break/end`
- **If-Else:** `cj_builder_if/else/endif` with condition inversion
- **For-Loops:** `cj_builder_for_begin/continue/break/end` with counter/limit/step
- **Condition Abstraction:** `branch_on_condition()` handles 16 condition codes across architectures

**Memory Addressing:**
- Base register + displacement (int32_t, negative offsets supported)
- Base + index register + scale (1,2,4,8)
- Pre/post-indexed modes
- Shift and extend modifiers on index registers

---

### 2. **Register Operations Available**

**Move & Assignment:**
- ✓ `cj_mov()` - Basic register/memory move
- ✓ `cj_builder_assign()` - Smart assignment with ARM64 multi-part immediate support (MOVZ+MOVK chain)
- ✓ `cj_builder_clear()` - Zero out register (XOR on X86, move wzr on ARM)

**Arithmetic:**
- ✓ `cj_add()` - Addition with immediate and shift modifiers
- ✓ `cj_sub()` - Subtraction with shift modifiers
- ✓ `cj_cmp()` - Comparison (sets flags)
- ✓ `cj_imul()`, `cj_mul()` - Multiplication

**Bitwise Operations:**
- ✓ `cj_and()` - AND (register-register with shifts)
- ✓ `cj_or()` - OR (register-register)
- ✓ `cj_xor()` - XOR
- ✓ `cj_not()` - Bitwise NOT
- ✓ `cj_orr()` (ARM) - OR with shift modifiers
- ✓ `cj_bic()` (ARM) - Bit Clear

**Shifts (X86_64):**
- ✓ `cj_shl()` - Shift left (immediate or CL)
- ✓ `cj_shr()` - Logical shift right
- ✓ `cj_sar()` - Arithmetic shift right
- ✓ `cj_rol()`, `cj_ror()` - Rotates

**Shifts (ARM64):**
- ✓ `cj_lsl()`, `cj_lsr()`, `cj_asr()`, `cj_r
... (truncated)
claude

That's a really good question. The analysis reveals several concrete gaps:

What's missing from cj that forced platform-specific code

1. ARM64 shift-by-immediate (the biggest one)

cj_lsr(), cj_lsl(), and cj_asr() on ARM64 only accept register operands — they emit LSRV/LSLV/ASRV (variable shift). There's no way to emit LSR x0, x0, #48 through the API. The JIT needs this *constantly* for NaN-box type checks (shr val, 48; cmp 0x7FFE), so I had to emit raw UBFM/SBFM encodings via cj_add_u32().

On x86, cj_shr()/cj_shl()/cj_sar() with immediates work fine — this is an ARM64-only gap.

2. CSET is declared but has an empty body

cj_cset() on ARM64 exists in the header but emits nothing. The JIT needs it to produce a 0/1 boolean from a comparison for inline <, <=, = etc. I had to emit raw CSINC instructions.

3. AND with immediate doesn't work for general registers

On x86, cj_and() only works with AL/AX/EAX accumulators. On ARM64, the AND-immediate path produces a wrong encoding. Payload masking (val & 0x0000FFFFFFFFFFFF) requires loading the mask into a register first.

4. Backend bugs that broke the builder's own abstractions

- cj_stp/cj_ldp ignore pre/post-indexed addressing modes — always emit signed-offset encoding. This broke cj_builder_fn_prologue() on ARM64 (it uses pre-indexed STP for the frame setup).

- cj_mov(x29, sp) generates x29 = 0 — the ORR encoding treats register 31 as XZR, not SP. This is a fundamental issue for any frame pointer setup.

- cj_ldr/cj_str silently drop instructions when the offset exceeds the ARM64 unsigned-immediate range (~32KB). The VM struct has stack_top at offset 38920, so every access to it disappeared silently.

Why not use the builder layer

Given the above, I couldn't use the builder for several reasons:

1. Prologue/epilogue was broken — STP pre-indexed mode doesn't work, MOV from SP doesn't work. Had to hand-emit the frame setup.

2. Need specific register assignments — The JIT permanently pins 5 callee-saved registers (vm ptr, slots ptr, closure ptr, stack_top, constants ptr) plus 4 cache registers. The builder's scratch register API doesn't support this — it gives you an opaque pool of 32-bit scratch registers, not persistent 64-bit register assignments.

3. Builder uses 32-bit registers — cj_builder_scratch_* returns w0-w7 / r8d-r11d. The JIT works with 64-bit lisa_value throughout and needs x0-x23 / rax-r15.

4. No cross-platform shift/cset/and-imm — The inline fast paths (the whole point of the JIT) are built on shift-by-immediate, conditional-set, and bitwise masking. Without portable versions, every fast path had to be #if defined(__x86_64__) ... #elif defined(__aarch64__).

What would make jit.c portable

If cj added:

- cj_builder_shr_imm()/cj_builder_shl_imm()/cj_builder_sar_imm() — cross-platform shift by constant

- cj_builder_cset() — cross-platform conditional-set (setcc on x86, csinc on ARM64)

- cj_builder_and_imm() — cross-platform AND with immediate

- 64-bit scratch register variants in the builder

- Fix the STP/LDP, MOV-from-SP, and silent-drop bugs

...then the vast majority of jit.c could use the builder and drop the #ifdef blocks. The platform macros (EMIT_JEQ, EMIT_SHR, etc.) and raw instruction hacks would become unnecessary.

result
Perfect! Now I have the complete information. Let me compile my findings:

## Summary of x86-64 Backend Support

Based on my search of `cj/src/arch/x86_64/backend.h`:

### 1. **cj_shr** (Line 968)
- **Immediate operands**: YES, but only with immediate value of `1`
- Only supports 16-bit register (16-bit constant 1), or memory operands with immediate 1
- Does NOT support arbitrary immediate values

### 2. **cj_shl** (Line 557)
- **Immediate operands**: YES, but only with immediate value of `1`
- Only supports 16-bit register or memory operands with immediate 1
- Does NOT support arbitrary immediate values

### 3. **cj_sar** (Line 1379)
- **Immediate operands**: YES, but only with immediate value of `1`
- Only supports 16-bit register or memory operands with immediate 1
- Does NOT support arbitrary immediate values

### 4. **Conditional Set Instructions** (All YES - they exist)
- **cj_setz** (Line 12490) - SET if Zero / SET if Equal
- **cj_setl** (Line 12984) - SET if Less (signed)
- **cj_setle** (Line 13136) - SET if Less or Equal (signed)
- **cj_setg** (Line 13212) - SET if Greater (signed)
- **cj_setge** (Line 13060) - SET if Greater or Equal (signed)
- **cj_sete** (Line 12452) - SET if Equal (alias for setz)
- **cj_setne** (Line 12528) - SET if Not Equal
- **cj_setb** (Line 12224) - SET if Below (unsigned)

All set* instructions accept:
- 8-bit register operands (with REX handling for r8-r15)
- Memory operands

Signature: `void cj_set*(cj_ctx* ctx, cj_operand dst)` where dst can be an 8-bit register or memory location.

### 5. **cj_and** (Line 5462)
- **Immediate operands**: YES, but ONLY for special accumulator registers:
  - AL (with 8-bit immediate)
  - AX (with 16-bit immediate)
  - EAX (with 32-bit immediate)
- General registers + register: YES (16-bit and 32-bit register pairs support `cj_and(r/r)`)
- Does NOT support general register + arbitrary immediate operands

### 6. **cj_neg** (Line 70554) - YES, EXISTS
- Accepts 16-bit register operands
- Accepts memory operands
- No operand to pass (unary operation on single operand)

### 7. **cj_or** (Line 6032)
- **Two register operands**: YES (16-bit and 32-bit register pairs support `cj_or(r, r)`)
- **Immediate operands**: YES, but ONLY for special accumulator registers:
  - AL (with 8-bit immediate)
  - AX (with 16-bit immediate)
  - EAX (with 32-bit immediate)

### 8. **cj_mov MOVABS Support** (Line 7270)
- **64-bit immediate to 64-bit register**: YES
- Supports `cj_mov(rax, 0xFFFFFFFFFFFFFFFF)` and similar
- Uses REX.W prefix (0x48) + 0xb8 + 8-byte immediate
- Line 7279-7280: `cj_add_u8(ctx, 0xb8 + (dst_reg & 7)); cj_add_u64(ctx, (uint64_t)src.constant);`

### Key File Location
All implementations are in: `cj/src/arch/x86_64/backend.h`

Perfect! I now have all the information needed. Let me compile a comprehensive report on the ARM64 backend instruction support.

## ARM64 Backend Instruction Support Report

Based on my analysis of `cj/src/arch/arm64/backend.h`, here's the detailed breakdown:

### 1. **cj_lsr** (Logical Shift Right)
- **Operand types supported:**
  - Register-Register: `dst=REGISTER, src=REGISTER` (register-based shift amount)
  - Register-Immediate: `dst=REGISTER, src=CONSTANT` (immediate shift amount)
- **Implementation:** Full - emits 32-bit instructions for both variants
- **Immediate support:** YES - accepts constant operands for immediate shifts (0-31 for 32-bit, 0-63 for 64-bit)
- **Lines:** 10621-10655

### 2. **cj_lsl** (Logical Shift Left)
- **Operand types supported:**
  - Register-Register: `dst=REGISTER, src=REGISTER` only
  - **NO immediate variant** - only register-register is implemented
- **Implementation:** Partial/Stub - only register-register path has body; no immediate path
- **Lines:** 10554-10573

### 3. **cj_asr** (Arithmetic Shift Right)
- **Operand types supported:**
  - Register-Register: `dst=REGISTER, src=REGISTER` (register-based shift amount)
  - Register-Immediate: `dst=REGISTER, src=CONSTANT` (immediate shift amount)
- **Implementation:** Full - emits 32-bit instructions for both variants
- **Immediate support:** YES - accepts constant operands for immediate shifts (0-31 for 32-bit, 0-63 for 64-bit)
- **Lines:** 860-894

### 4. **cj_cset** (Conditional Set)
- **Status:** STUB/EMPTY - function body is empty (no implementation)
- **Signature:** `cj_cset(cj_ctx* ctx, cj_operand dst, cj_operand src)`
- **Lines:** 2864-2865

### 5. **cj_csel** (Conditional Select)
- **Status:** IMPLEMENTED
- **Operand types:** Register-Register only: `dst=REGISTER, src=REGISTER`
- **Implementation:** Full - emits instruction `0x1A800000`
- **Note:** This is a 3-operand instruction but the API shows 2 parameters; condition code may be encoded elsewhere
- **Lines:** 2843-2862

### 6. **cj_and** (Bitwise AND)
- **Operand types supported:**
  - Vector Register-Vector Register (SIMD path) with matching Q and size
  - Register-Register with shift support: `dst=REGISTER, src=REGISTER` with shift.kind (LSL/LSR/ASR/ROR) and shift.amount (0-31 for 32-bit, 0-63 for 64-bit)
  - Register-Immediate: `dst=REGISTER, src=CONSTANT`
- **Implementation:** Full - three distinct code paths for vector, register with shifts, and immediate constants
- **Lines:** 717-793

### 7. **cj_orr** (Bitwise OR)
- **Operand types supported:**
  - Vector Register-Vector Register (SIMD path) with matching Q and size
  - Register-Register with shift support: `dst=REGISTER, src=REGISTER` with shift.kind (LSL/LSR/ASR/ROR) and shift.amount
  - Register-Immediate: `dst=REGISTER, src=CONSTANT`
- **Implementation:** Full - three distinct code paths (vector, register with shifts, immediate)
- **Lines:** 11376-11452

### 8. **cj_neg** (Negate)
- **Status:** IMPLEMENTED
- **Operand types supported:*
... (truncated)

Perfect! Now I have the complete file. Let me analyze it thoroughly and provide a comprehensive summary.

## Comprehensive Analysis of lisa/jit.c Platform-Specific Workarounds

Based on my complete read of `lisa/jit.c`, here's the detailed breakdown:

---

### 1. `#if defined(__x86_64__)` / `#elif defined(__aarch64__)` Blocks

**Block 1: Register Definitions (Lines 26-96)**
- **x86_64 variant** (lines 26-60): Defines 5 persistent registers (VM, SLOTS, CLOSURE, STKTOP, CONSTS), 4 cache registers, and 5 temp registers
- **ARM64 variant** (lines 61-95): Parallels x86 layout but with different register names (x19-x23 for persistent, x10-x13 for cache, x0-x4 for temps)
- **Operation abstracted**: Calling convention and ABI register allocation

**Block 2: emit_load_imm64() (Lines 113-131)**
- **x86 path**: Single `cj_mov()` with immediate (handles 64-bit directly)
- **ARM64 path**: Uses `movz` + multiple `movk` instructions to load immediate in chunks, with special-case optimization for zero
- **Operation abstracted**: Loading 64-bit constants (different imm encoding capabilities)

**Block 3: emit_load64() (Lines 133-158)**
- **x86 path**: Single `cj_mov()` from memory
- **ARM64 path**: Complex offset handling due to LDR's max unsigned offset (32760 for 8-byte aligned). Handles three cases:
  - Valid offset: Direct LDR
  - Small negative offset: SUB then LDR
  - Large offset: Load offset into register, add to base, then LDR
- **Operation abstracted**: Loading 64-bit values from memory with arbitrary offsets

**Block 4: emit_store64() (Lines 160-182)**
- **x86 path**: Single `cj_mov()` to memory
- **ARM64 path**: Three-case handling (same offset constraints as emit_load64, using STR instead of LDR)
- **Operation abstracted**: Storing 64-bit values to memory with arbitrary offsets

**Block 5: emit_call_abs() (Lines 184-191)**
- **x86 path**: Load immediate into r10, then `cj_call(r10)`
- **ARM64 path**: Load immediate into x9, then `cj_blr(x9)`
- **Operation abstracted**: Indirect absolute function calls

**Block 6: emit_arith_compute() (Lines 523-535)**
- **x86 path**: `cj_imul()` for multiplication
- **ARM64 path**: `cj_mul()` for multiplication
- **Operation abstracted**: Multiply instruction name differs between architectures

**Block 7: emit_int_type_check() (Lines 446-456)**
- **x86 path**: Compare REG_TMP1 directly with imm(TAG_INT_HI), then `cj_jnz()`
- **ARM64 path**: Move immediate into REG_TMP2, compare register-to-register, then `cj_bne()`
- **Operation abstracted**: NaN-box tag checking (ARM64 can't compare imm directly)

**Block 8: emit_non_double_check() (Lines 461-471)**
- **x86 path**: Compare REG_TMP1 with imm, jump if below (unsigned)
- **ARM64 path**: Move imm to REG_TMP2, compare reg-to-reg, jump if carry clear (unsigned below)
- **Operation abstracted**: Unsigned comparison for double detection (different flag semantics)

**Block 9: emit_bool_from_flags() (Lines 479-507)**
- **x86 path**: Load LISA_FALSE first, then use `setl/setle/
... (truncated)

10621→static inline void cj_lsr(cj_ctx* ctx, cj_operand dst, cj_operand src) {
 10622→  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && src.type == CJ_REGISTER && src.extend.kind == CJ_EXTEND_KIND_NONE && !src.extend.has_amount) {
 10623→    int rd = arm64_parse_reg(dst.reg);
 10624→    if (rd < 0) return;
 10625→    int rn = rd;
 10626→    int rm = arm64_parse_reg(src.reg);
 10627→    if (rm < 0) return;
 10628→    uint32_t instr = 0x1AC02400;
 10629→    int sf = arm64_is_64bit(dst.reg) ? 1 : 0;
 10630→    instr |= (sf << 31);
 10631→    instr &= ~((1u << 5) - 1u);
 10632→    instr |= (rd & ((1u << 5) - 1u));
 10633→    instr &= ~(((1u << 5) - 1u) << 5);
 10634→    instr |= ((rn & ((1u << 5) - 1u)) << 5);
 10635→    instr &= ~(((1u << 5) - 1u) << 16);
 10636→    instr |= ((rm & ((1u << 5) - 1u)) << 16);

 10638→    return;
 10639→  }

968→void cj_shr(cj_ctx* ctx, cj_operand dst, cj_operand src) {
   969→  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && detect_reg_size(dst.reg) == 16 && src.type == CJ_CONSTANT && src.constant == 1) {
   970→    int8_t dst_reg = parse_reg(dst.reg);
   971→    if (dst_reg < 0) return;
   972→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0x66);
   973→    uint8_t rex_w = 0;
   974→    uint8_t rex_r = 0;
   975→    uint8_t rex_x = 0;
   976→    uint8_t rex_b = (dst_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
   977→    uint8_t need_rex = rex_w || rex_r || rex_x || rex_b;
   978→    if (need_rex) emit_rex(ctx, rex_w, rex_r, rex_x, rex_b);
   979→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0xd1);
   980→    emit_modrm(ctx, 3, 5, dst_reg & 7);
   981→    return;
   982→  }
   983→  if (dst.type == CJ_MEMORY && src.type == CJ_CONSTANT && src.constant == 1) {
   984→    int8_t dst_base_reg = dst.mem.base ? parse_reg(dst.mem.base) : -1;
   985→    int8_t dst_index_reg = dst.mem.index ? parse_reg(dst.mem.index) : -1;
   986→    uint8_t dst_rex_b = (dst_base_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
   987→    uint8_t dst_rex_x = (dst_index_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
   988→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0x66);
   989→    uint8_t rex_w = 0;
   990→    uint8_t rex_r = 0;
   991→    uint8_t rex_x = dst_rex_x;
   992→    uint8_t rex_b = dst_rex_b;
   993→    uint8_t need_rex = rex_w || rex_r || rex_x || rex_b;
   994→    if (need_rex) emit_rex(ctx, rex_w, rex_r, rex_x, rex_b);
   995→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0xd1);
   996→    int mod = emit_memory_modrm(ctx, 5, dst.mem.base, dst.mem.index, dst.mem.scale, dst.mem.disp);
   997→    if (mod == 1) cj_add_u8(ctx, (int8_t)dst.mem.disp);
   998→    else if (mod == 2) cj_add_u32(ctx, (uint32_t)dst.mem.disp);
   999→    return;
  1000→  }

⚙ Grep

Confirmed. Both backends have severe issues:

- x86 cj_shr/cj_shl/cj_sar: Only work for 16-bit registers with immediate=1

- ARM64 cj_lsr/cj_asr immediate path: Never reads src.constant — shift amount is never encoded

- ARM64 cj_lsl: No immediate path at all

- ARM64 cj_cset: Empty body

- ARM64 cj_csel: Doesn't encode condition code

Let me check the condition enum the builder already uses:

  "pattern": "typedef enum.*cj_condition|CJ_COND_",
  "path": "cj/src/op.h",
  "output_mode": "content",
  "-n": true,
  "-A": 20
}

235-} cj_condition;
236-
237-#define CJ_REG(NAME)                                                                               \
238-  static const cj_operand cj_##NAME = {                                                            \
239-      .type = CJ_REGISTER,                                                                         \
240-      .mask = NULL,                                                                                \
241-      .zero_mask = 0,                                                                              \
242-      .sae = 0,                                                                                    \
243-      .rounding = CJ_ROUND_DEFAULT,                                                                \
244-      .reg = #NAME,                                                                                \
245-      .shift = {.kind = CJ_SHIFT_KIND_NONE, .amount = 0, .has_amount = 0},                         \
246-      .extend = {.kind = CJ_EXTEND_KIND_NONE, .amount = 0, .has_amount = 0},                       \
247-  }
248-
249-#if defined(__x86_64__) || defined(_M_X64)
250-#include "arch/x86_64/backend.h"
251-#elif defined(__aarch64__) || defined(_M_ARM64)
252-#include "arch/arm64/backend.h"
253-#else
254-#error "Unsupported architecture"
⚙ Read ×2

Good — cj_condition is already defined with all the conditions, and the builder's invert_condition and branch_on_condition already use it. Now let me also check the x86 cj_setl and friends to see their exact signatures and how they work with 64-bit results:

Read — cj/src/arch/x86_64/backend.h
{
  "file_path": "cj/src/arch/x86_64/backend.h",
  "offset": 12984,
  "limit": 25
}
result
12984→void cj_setl(cj_ctx* ctx, cj_operand dst) {
 12985→  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && detect_reg_size(dst.reg) == 8) {
 12986→    int8_t dst_reg = parse_reg(dst.reg);
 12987→    if (dst_reg < 0) return;
 12988→    uint8_t rex_w = 0;
 12989→    uint8_t rex_r = 0;
 12990→    uint8_t rex_x = 0;
 12991→    uint8_t rex_b = (dst_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
 12992→    uint8_t need_rex = rex_w || rex_r || rex_x || rex_b;
 12993→    if (!need_rex && (dst_reg >= 4)) need_rex = 1;
 12994→    if (need_rex) emit_rex(ctx, rex_w, rex_r, rex_x, rex_b);
 12995→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0x0f);
 12996→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0x9c);
 12997→    emit_modrm(ctx, 3, 0, dst_reg & 7);
 12998→    return;
 12999→  }
 13000→  if (dst.type == CJ_MEMORY) {
 13001→    int8_t dst_base_reg = dst.mem.base ? parse_reg(dst.mem.base) : -1;
 13002→    int8_t dst_index_reg = dst.mem.index ? parse_reg(dst.mem.index) : -1;
 13003→    uint8_t dst_rex_b = (dst_base_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
 13004→    uint8_t dst_rex_x = (dst_index_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
 13005→    uint8_t rex_w = 0;
 13006→    uint8_t rex_r = 0;
 13007→    uint8_t rex_x = dst_rex_x;
 13008→    uint8_t rex_b = dst_rex_b;

⚙ Grep

Wait — line 828 shows cj_shl on x86 DOES have a 64-bit register + arbitrary constant path! It uses opcode 0xc1 (SHL r64, imm8) with REX.W. So x86 cj_shl works for 64-bit shifts. Let me verify the same for cj_shr and cj_sar:

Grep — detect_reg_size\(dst.reg\) == 64 && src.type == CJ_CONSTANT\b
{
  "pattern": "detect_reg_size\\(dst.reg\\) == 64 && src.type == CJ_CONSTANT\\b",
  "path": "cj/src/arch/x86_64/backend.h",
  "output_mode": "content",
  "-n": true,
  "-A": 10,
  "head_limit": 60
}
result

⚙ Grep, Read

Good news — x86 SHL/SHR/SAR all support 64-bit registers with arbitrary immediates (0xC1 encoding with REX.W). Let me now check x86 cj_or and cj_neg for 64-bit support:

Grep — ^void cj_or\(
{
  "pattern": "^void cj_or\\(",
  "path": "cj/src/arch/x86_64/backend.h",
  "output_mode": "content",
  "-n": true
}
result
6032:void cj_or(cj_ctx* ctx, cj_operand dst, cj_operand src) {
Read — cj/src/arch/x86_64/backend.h
{
  "file_path": "cj/src/arch/x86_64/backend.h",
  "offset": 6032,
  "limit": 120
}
result
6032→void cj_or(cj_ctx* ctx, cj_operand dst, cj_operand src) {
  6033→  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && dst.reg && strcmp(dst.reg, "al") == 0 && src.type == CJ_CONSTANT) {
  6034→    int8_t dst_reg = parse_reg(dst.reg);
  6035→    if (dst_reg < 0) return;
  6036→    int64_t src_imm = (int64_t)src.constant;
  6037→    if (src_imm < -128 || src_imm > 127) return;
  6038→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0x0c);
  6039→    cj_add_u8(ctx, (uint8_t)src_imm);
  6040→    return;
  6041→  }
  6042→  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && dst.reg && strcmp(dst.reg, "ax") == 0 && src.type == CJ_CONSTANT && src.constant <= 0xFFFF) {
  6043→    int8_t dst_reg = parse_reg(dst.reg);
  6044→    if (dst_reg < 0) return;
  6045→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0x66);
  6046→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0x0d);
  6047→    cj_add_u16(ctx, (uint16_t)src.constant);
  6048→    return;
  6049→  }
  6050→  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && dst.reg && strcmp(dst.reg, "eax") == 0 && src.type == CJ_CONSTANT && src.constant <= 0xFFFFFFFFu) {
  6051→    int8_t dst_reg = parse_reg(dst.reg);
  6052→    if (dst_reg < 0) return;
  6053→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0x0d);
  6054→    cj_add_u32(ctx, (uint32_t)src.constant);
  6055→    return;
  6056→  }
  6057→  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && detect_reg_size(dst.reg) == 16 && src.type == CJ_REGISTER && detect_reg_size(src.reg) == 16) {
  6058→    int8_t dst_reg = parse_reg(dst.reg);
  6059→    if (dst_reg < 0) return;
  6060→    int8_t src_reg = parse_reg(src.reg);
  6061→    if (src_reg < 0) return;
  6062→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0x66);
  6063→    uint8_t rex_w = 0;
  6064→    uint8_t rex_r = (dst_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
  6065→    uint8_t rex_x = 0;
  6066→    uint8_t rex_b = (src_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
  6067→    uint8_t need_rex = rex_w || rex_r || rex_x || rex_b;
  6068→    if (need_rex) emit_rex(ctx, rex_w, rex_r, rex_x, rex_b);
  6069→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0x0b);
  6070→    emit_modrm(ctx, 3, dst_reg & 7, src_reg & 7);
  6071→    return;
  6072→  }
  6073→  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && detect_reg_size(dst.reg) == 16 && src.type == CJ_MEMORY) {
  6074→    int8_t dst_reg = parse_reg(dst.reg);
  6075→    if (dst_reg < 0) return;
  6076→    int8_t src_base_reg = src.mem.base ? parse_reg(src.mem.base) : -1;
  6077→    int8_t src_index_reg = src.mem.index ? parse_reg(src.mem.index) : -1;
  6078→    uint8_t src_rex_b = (src_base_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
  6079→    uint8_t src_rex_x = (src_index_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
  6080→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0x66);
  6081→    uint8_t rex_w = 0;
  6082→    uint8_t rex_r = (dst_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
  6083→    uint8_t rex_x = src_rex_x;
  6084→    uint8_t rex_b = src_rex_b;
  6085→    uint8_t need_rex = rex_w || rex_r || rex_x || rex_b;
  6086→    if (need_rex) emit_rex(ctx, rex_w, rex_r, rex_x, rex_b);
  6087→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0x0b);
  6088→    int mod = emit_memory_modrm(ctx, dst_reg & 7, src.mem.base, src.mem.index, src.mem.scale, src.mem.disp);
  6089→    if (mod == 1) cj_add_u8(ctx, (int8_t)src.mem.disp);
  6090→    else if (mod == 2) cj_add_u32(ctx, (uint32_t)src.mem.disp);
  6091

⚙ Read ×2

x86 cj_or supports 64-bit reg-reg (line 6127). Let me check cj_neg more thoroughly:

Read — cj/src/arch/x86_64/backend.h
{
  "file_path": "cj/src/arch/x86_64/backend.h",
  "offset": 70554,
  "limit": 50
}
result
70554→void cj_neg(cj_ctx* ctx, cj_operand value) {
 70555→  if (value.type == CJ_REGISTER && detect_reg_size(value.reg) == 16) {
 70556→    int8_t value_reg = parse_reg(value.reg);
 70557→    if (value_reg < 0) return;
 70558→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0x66);
 70559→    uint8_t rex_w = 0;
 70560→    uint8_t rex_r = 0;
 70561→    uint8_t rex_x = 0;
 70562→    uint8_t rex_b = (value_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
 70563→    uint8_t need_rex = rex_w || rex_r || rex_x || rex_b;
 70564→    if (need_rex) emit_rex(ctx, rex_w, rex_r, rex_x, rex_b);
 70565→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0xf7);
 70566→    emit_modrm(ctx, 3, 3, value_reg & 7);
 70567→    return;
 70568→  }
 70569→  if (value.type == CJ_MEMORY) {
 70570→    int8_t value_base_reg = value.mem.base ? parse_reg(value.mem.base) : -1;
 70571→    int8_t value_index_reg = value.mem.index ? parse_reg(value.mem.index) : -1;
 70572→    uint8_t value_rex_b = (value_base_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
 70573→    uint8_t value_rex_x = (value_index_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
 70574→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0x66);
 70575→    uint8_t rex_w = 0;
 70576→    uint8_t rex_r = 0;
 70577→    uint8_t rex_x = value_rex_x;
 70578→    uint8_t rex_b = value_rex_b;
 70579→    uint8_t need_rex = rex_w || rex_r || rex_x || rex_b;
 70580→    if (need_rex) emit_rex(ctx, rex_w, rex_r, rex_x, rex_b);
 70581→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0xf7);
 70582→    int mod = emit_memory_modrm(ctx, 3, value.mem.base, value.mem.index, value.mem.scale, value.mem.disp);
 70583→    if (mod == 1) cj_add_u8(ctx, (int8_t)value.mem.disp);
 70584→    else if (mod == 2) cj_add_u32(ctx, (uint32_t)value.mem.disp);
 70585→    return;
 70586→  }
 70587→  if (value.type == CJ_REGISTER && detect_reg_size(value.reg) == 32) {
 70588→    int8_t value_reg = parse_reg(value.reg);
 70589→    if (value_reg < 0) return;
 70590→    uint8_t rex_w = 0;
 70591→    uint8_t rex_r = 0;
 70592→    uint8_t rex_x = 0;
 70593→    uint8_t rex_b = (value_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;
 70594→    uint8_t need_rex = rex_w || rex_r || rex_x || rex_b;
 70595→    if (need_rex) emit_rex(ctx, rex_w, rex_r, rex_x, rex_b);
 70596→    cj_add_u8(ctx, 0xf7);
 70597→    emit_modrm(ctx, 3, 3, value_reg & 7);
 70598→    return;
 70599→  }
 70600→  if (value.type == CJ_MEMORY) {
 70601→    int8_t value_base_reg = value.mem.base ? parse_reg(value.mem.base) : -1;
 70602→    int8_t value_index_reg = value.mem.index ? parse_reg(value.mem.index) : -1;
 70603→    uint8_t value_rex_b = (value_base_reg >= 8) ? 1 : 0;

⚙ Grep

x86 cj_neg supports 64-bit (line 70617). So the x86 backend actually has good 64-bit support for all the operations we need. Now let me check how the ARM64 cj_neg works:

Grep — ^static inline void cj_neg\(
{
  "pattern": "^static inline void cj_neg\\(",
  "path": "cj/src/arch/arm64/backend.h",
  "output_mode": "content",
  "-n": true,
  "-A": 25
}
result
11138:static inline void cj_neg(cj_ctx* ctx, cj_operand dst, cj_operand src) {
11139-  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && src.type == CJ_REGISTER) {
11140-    arm64_vec_reg_info vd = arm64_parse_vec_reg(dst.reg);
11141-    if (vd.reg >= 0) {
11142-      arm64_vec_reg_info vn = arm64_parse_vec_reg(src.reg);
11143-      if (vn.reg >= 0 && vd.q == vn.q && vd.size == vn.size) {
11144-        uint32_t instr = 0x2E20B800;
11145-        instr |= (vd.q << 30);
11146-        instr |= ((vd.size & 0x3) << 22);
11147-        instr |= (vd.reg & 0x1f);
11148-        instr |= ((vn.reg & 0x1f) << 5);
11149-        cj_add_u32(ctx, instr);
11150-        return;
11151-      }
11152-    }
11153-  }
11154-  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && src.type == CJ_REGISTER && src.extend.kind == CJ_EXTEND_KIND_NONE && !src.extend.has_amount) {
11155-    int rd = arm64_parse_reg(dst.reg);
11156-    if (rd < 0) return;
11157-    int rn = arm64_parse_reg(src.reg);
11158-    if (rn < 0) return;
11159-    uint32_t instr = 0x4B0003E0;
11160-    instr = arm64_is_64bit(dst.reg) ? 0xCB0003E0 : 0x4B0003E0;
11161-    instr &= ~((1u << 5) - 1u);
11162-    instr |= (rd & ((1u << 5) - 1u));
11163-    instr &= ~(((1u << 5) - 1u) << 16);
⚙ Read ×7, Grep ×6, Bash

Now I have a clear picture of the backend landscape. Let me check ARM64 cj_and with immediates:

Read — cj/src/arch/arm64/backend.h
{
  "file_path": "cj/src/arch/arm64/backend.h",
  "offset": 717,
  "limit": 80
}
result
717→static inline void cj_and(cj_ctx* ctx, cj_operand dst, cj_operand src) {
   718→  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && src.type == CJ_REGISTER) {
   719→    arm64_vec_reg_info vd = arm64_parse_vec_reg(dst.reg);
   720→    if (vd.reg >= 0) {
   721→      arm64_vec_reg_info vm = arm64_parse_vec_reg(src.reg);
   722→      if (vm.reg >= 0 && vd.q == vm.q && vd.size == vm.size) {
   723→        uint32_t instr = 0x0E201C00;
   724→        instr |= (vd.q << 30);
   725→        instr |= ((vd.size & 0x3) << 22);
   726→        instr |= (vd.reg & 0x1f);
   727→        instr |= ((vd.reg & 0x1f) << 5);
   728→        instr |= ((vm.reg & 0x1f) << 16);
   729→        cj_add_u32(ctx, instr);
   730→        return;
   731→      }
   732→    }
   733→  }
   734→  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && src.type == CJ_REGISTER && src.extend.kind == CJ_EXTEND_KIND_NONE && !src.extend.has_amount) {
   735→    int rd = arm64_parse_reg(dst.reg);
   736→    if (rd < 0) return;
   737→    int rn = rd;
   738→    int rm = arm64_parse_reg(src.reg);
   739→    if (rm < 0) return;
   740→    uint32_t instr = 0x0A000000;
   741→    int sf = arm64_is_64bit(dst.reg) ? 1 : 0;
   742→    instr |= (sf << 31);
   743→    instr &= ~((1u << 5) - 1u);
   744→    instr |= (rd & ((1u << 5) - 1u));
   745→    instr &= ~(((1u << 5) - 1u) << 5);
   746→    instr |= ((rn & ((1u << 5) - 1u)) << 5);
   747→    instr &= ~(((1u << 5) - 1u) << 16);
   748→    instr |= ((rm & ((1u << 5) - 1u)) << 16);
   749→    uint32_t shift_mode = 0;
   750→    switch (src.shift.kind) {
   751→      case CJ_SHIFT_KIND_NONE:
   752→      case CJ_SHIFT_KIND_LSL:
   753→        shift_mode = 0;
   754→        break;
   755→      case CJ_SHIFT_KIND_LSR:
   756→        shift_mode = 1;
   757→        break;
   758→      case CJ_SHIFT_KIND_ASR:
   759→        shift_mode = 2;
   760→        break;
   761→      case CJ_SHIFT_KIND_ROR:
   762→        shift_mode = 3;
   763→        break;
   764→      default:
   765→        return;
   766→    }
   767→    instr &= ~(((1u << 2) - 1u) << 22);
   768→    instr |= ((shift_mode & ((1u << 2) - 1u)) << 22);
   769→    uint32_t shift_amount = (src.shift.kind == CJ_SHIFT_KIND_NONE && !src.shift.has_amount) ? 0u : src.shift.amount;
   770→    if (arm64_is_64bit(dst.reg)) {
   771→      if (shift_amount > 63u) return;
   772→    } else {
   773→      if (shift_amount > 31u) return;
   774→    }
   775→    instr &= ~(((1u << 6) - 1u) << 10);
   776→    instr |= ((shift_amount & ((1u << 6) - 1u)) << 10);
   777→    cj_add_u32(ctx, instr);
   778→    return;
   779→  }
   780→  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && src.type == CJ_CONSTANT) {
   781→    int rd = arm64_parse_reg(dst.reg);
   782→    if (rd < 0) return;
   783→    int rn = rd;
   784→
   785→    uint32_t instr = 0x12000000;
   786→    instr &= ~((1u << 5) - 1u);
   787→    instr |= (rd & ((1u << 5) - 1u));
   788→    instr &= ~(((1u << 5) - 1u) << 5);
   789→    instr |= ((rn & ((1u << 5) - 1u)) << 5);
   790→    cj_add_u32(ctx, instr);

... (truncated)

Now let me check the builder test file to understand testing patterns:

Read — cj/tests/test_harness_builder.c

result
1→#include <assert.h>
     2→#include <stdint.h>
     3→#include <stdio.h>
     4→
     5→#include "builder.h"
     6→
     7→typedef int (*fn1_t)(int);
     8→typedef int (*fn2_t)(int, int);
     9→
    10→static void test_assign_and_add(void) {
    11→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    12→  cj_builder_frame frame;
    13→  cj_builder_fn_prologue(cj, 0, &frame);
    14→
    15→  cj_operand arg0 = cj_builder_arg_int(cj, 0);
    16→  cj_operand temp = cj_builder_scratch_reg(0);
    17→  cj_operand five = cj_make_constant(5);
    18→
    19→  cj_builder_assign(cj, temp, five);
    20→  cj_builder_add_assign(cj, temp, arg0);
    21→  cj_builder_return_value(cj, &frame, temp);
    22→
    23→  fn1_t fn = (fn1_t)create_cj_fn(cj);
    24→  assert(fn);
    25→  assert(fn(7) == 12);
    26→  assert(fn(10) == 15);
    27→
    28→  destroy_cj_fn(cj, (cj_fn)fn);
    29→  destroy_cj_ctx(cj);
    30→}
    31→
    32→static void test_scratch_helpers(void) {
    33→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    34→  cj_builder_frame frame;
    35→  cj_builder_fn_prologue(cj, 0, &frame);
    36→
    37→  cj_builder_scratch scratch;
    38→  cj_builder_scratch_init(&scratch);
    39→
    40→  cj_operand acc = cj_builder_scratch_acquire(&scratch);
    41→  cj_operand tmp = cj_builder_scratch_acquire(&scratch);
    42→
    43→  cj_builder_assign(cj, acc, cj_builder_arg_int(cj, 0));
    44→  cj_builder_assign(cj, tmp, cj_make_constant(7));
    45→  cj_builder_add_assign(cj, acc, tmp);
    46→
    47→  cj_builder_scratch_release(&scratch); // release tmp
    48→
    49→  cj_operand adjust = cj_builder_scratch_acquire(&scratch);
    50→  cj_builder_assign(cj, adjust, cj_make_constant(3));
    51→  cj_builder_sub_assign(cj, acc, adjust);
    52→
    53→  cj_builder_scratch_release(&scratch); // release adjust
    54→
    55→  cj_builder_return_value(cj, &frame, acc);
    56→  cj_builder_scratch_release(&scratch); // release acc
    57→
    58→  fn1_t fn = (fn1_t)create_cj_fn(cj);
    59→  assert(fn);
    60→  assert(fn(0) == 4);
    61→  assert(fn(5) == 9);
    62→
    63→  destroy_cj_fn(cj, (cj_fn)fn);
    64→  destroy_cj_ctx(cj);
    65→}
    66→
    67→static void test_call_helper(void) {
    68→  cj_ctx *cj = create_cj_ctx();
    69→  cj_label entry = cj_create_label(cj);
    70→  cj_label callee = cj_create_label(cj);
    71→
    72→  // Main function
    73→  cj_mark_label(cj, entry);
    74→  cj_builder_frame main_frame;
    75→  cj_builder_fn_prologue_with_link_save(cj, 0, &main_frame);
    76→  cj_builder_scratch scratch;
    77→  cj_builder_scratch_init(&scratch);
    78→
    79→  cj_operand arg = cj_builder_scratch_acquire(&scratch);
    80→  cj_builder_assign(cj, arg, cj_builder_arg_int(cj, 0));
⚙ Write

Now I have all the information needed. Let me write the plan.

Write — <claude-internal>
{
  "file_path": "<claude-internal>",
  "content": "# Add Cross-Platform Builder Operations to cj\n\n## Context\n\nThe Lisa JIT (`lisa/jit.c`) needs several operations that the cj builder layer doesn't provide cross-platform abstractions for. The JIT currently works around this with `#ifdef` blocks, raw `cj_add_u32()` instruction encoding, and `EMIT_*` macros. These should be proper builder functions.\n\nThe generated backends have bugs (ARM64 shift-by-immediate never encodes the shift amount, `cj_cset` is empty, etc.) but fixing the codegen is deferred. Instead, the builder will use raw instruction encoding internally as needed, providing a correct cross-platform API that the JIT can use.\n\n## File Modified\n\n**`src/builder.h`** — add new functions (the only file with production changes).\n\n**`tests/test_harness_builder.c`** — add tests for each new operation.\n\n## New Builder Functions\n\n### 1. `cj_builder_shl(ctx, dst, shift)` — Shift Left by Immediate\n\n```c\nstatic inline void cj_builder_shl(cj_ctx *ctx, cj_operand dst, unsigned shift);\n```\n\n- **x86**: `cj_shl(ctx, dst, imm(shift))` — backend handles r64+imm8 correctly\n- **ARM64**: Raw UBFM encoding. LSL by `shift` is `UBFM Xd, Xn, #(-shift mod 64), #(63-shift)`:\n  ```c\n  uint32_t immr = (-shift) & 63;\n  uint32_t imms = 63 - shift;\n  uint32_t instr = 0xD3400000 | (immr << 16) | (imms << 10) | (rn << 5) | rd;\n  ```\n  For 32-bit (w regs): base `0x53000000`, mod 32, imms limit 31.\n\n### 2. `cj_builder_shr(ctx, dst, shift)` — Logical Shift Right by Immediate\n\n```c\nstatic inline void cj_builder_shr(cj_ctx *ctx, cj_operand dst, unsigned shift);\n```\n\n- **x86**: `cj_shr(ctx, dst, imm(shift))` — works\n- **ARM64**: Raw UBFM. LSR by `shift` is `UBFM Xd, Xn, #shift, #63`:\n  ```c\n  uint32_t instr = 0xD340FC00 | (shift << 16) | (rn << 5) | rd;\n  ```\n  For 32-bit: base `0x53007C00`.\n\n### 3. `cj_builder_sar(ctx, dst, shift)` — Arithmetic Shift Right by Immediate\n\n```c\nstatic inline void cj_buil
... (truncated)
result

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Analysis:

Let me trace through the conversation chronologically:

1. Session start: This is a continuation from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary tells us about a JIT rewrite for lisa/jit.c with register cache and inline integer fast paths. Several bugs were found and fixed in previous sessions. The current bug at the start is: self-call comparison in OP_TAIL_CALL compares NaN-boxed lisa_value with raw lisa_obj_closure * pointer - they can never be equal.

2. Fix NaN-boxing mismatch: I fixed the self-call comparison by NaN-boxing REG_CLOSURE before comparing:

- Load QNAN | TAG_OBJ into REG_TMP2

- OR REG_TMP2 with REG_CLOSURE

- Compare REG_TMP1 (stack callee) with REG_TMP2 (NaN-boxed closure)

- This fixed tail recursion for self-calls (tested to 1M depth)

3. Mutual recursion crash: Testing (def e (fn [n] (if (= n 0) true (o (- n 1))))) (def o ...) crashed because non-self tail calls recursively called lisa_jit_tail_call_helper → JIT → helper → JIT, overflowing C stack.

4. Trampoline implementation:

- Defined sentinel: LISA_TAIL_PENDING_BASE 0xDEAD000000000000 with argc encoded in low bits

- IS_TAIL_PENDING(v) checks top 16 bits == 0xDEAD

- TAIL_PENDING_ARGC(v) extracts argc from low 8 bits

- JIT's non-self tail call now returns the sentinel instead of calling helper

- Added jit_trampoline() in vm.c that loops iteratively on sentinel

- All JIT call sites check for sentinel

- Removed lisa_jit_tail_call_helper function

5. First trampoline bug: Target closure o was never JIT-compiled because it was reached through the trampoline (not through call_closure). The trampoline fell to interpreter, which recursively entered trampoline. Fixed by adding eager JIT compilation in the trampoline:

```c

if (!closure->function->jit_code && vm->jit_enabled) {

lisa_jit_compile(vm, closure->function);

}

```

6. All tests pass: factorial, fibonacci, tail recursion 1M, mutual recursion 100K, closures, list ops all work. Benchmark: ~2x speedup (0.53s JIT vs 1.05s interpreter for fib(35) with -O2).

7. User asks about builder portability: "was there a big feature missing from cj that would have made the code more portable across arm and amd? i see you made a lot of the choices backend-dependent instead of using the builder. why is that?"

8. I explained the gaps: Missing ARM64 shift-by-immediate, empty CSET, broken AND-immediate, backend bugs (STP/LDP, MOV from SP, silent instruction drops), 32-bit-only builder registers.

9. User says to fix the builder: "since we have control over cj, can we fix these in the builder? the individual instructions are created from code generation, so we'd have to touch that. i'd defer those until we fixed the builder."

10. Plan mode entered: I explored the codebase extensively to understand what the builder provides and what's missing:

- Read full src/builder.h (632 lines)

- Explored ARM64 and x86 backends for specific instruction support

- Key findings from backend investigation:

- x86 SHL/SHR/SAR: DO work with 64-bit registers and arbitrary immediates

- x86 OR: works with r64+r64

- x86 NEG: works with r64

- x86 SETcc: works with 8-bit registers

- x86 MOVZX: works with r64←r8

- x86 AND: works with r64+r64 and r64+imm32

- ARM64 LSR/ASR immediate path: exists but NEVER ENCODES the shift amount

- ARM64 LSL: no immediate path at all

- ARM64 CSET: empty body

- ARM64 CSEL: doesn't encode condition code

- ARM64 AND immediate: never encodes the bitmask

11. Writing plan: I attempted to write the plan file but got an error because I hadn't read it first. This is where the conversation ends.

Key files modified in this session:

- lisa/jit.c: Fixed NaN-boxing comparison, changed non-self tail call to return sentinel

- lisa/vm.c: Added jit_trampoline(), eager JIT compilation in trampoline, removed lisa_jit_tail_call_helper, added trampoline checks at all JIT call sites

- lisa/vm.h: Added LISA_TAIL_PENDING macros, removed lisa_jit_tail_call_helper declaration

The plan being written covers adding 6 new builder functions to src/builder.h:

1. cj_builder_shl - shift left by immediate

2. cj_builder_shr - logical shift right by immediate

3. cj_builder_sar - arithmetic shift right by immediate

4. cj_builder_cset - conditional set (0/1 from flags)

5. cj_builder_or - bitwise OR

6. cj_builder_neg - negate in place

Summary:

1. Primary Request and Intent:

The session has two phases:

- Phase 1 (completed): Continue implementing the JIT rewrite for lisa/jit.c. Specifically: fix the NaN-boxing mismatch in self-call comparison, implement a trampoline for non-self tail calls (mutual recursion), and verify all tests pass with benchmarks.

- Phase 2 (in progress): The user asked about why the JIT code has so many platform-dependent #ifdef blocks instead of using the cj builder layer. After I explained the missing builder features and backend bugs, the user requested: "since we have control over cj, can we fix these in the builder? the individual instructions are created from code generation, so we'd have to touch that. i'd defer those until we fixed the builder." — meaning: add cross-platform abstractions to src/builder.h, using raw instruction encoding internally to work around backend bugs, and defer fixing the generated backend code.

2. Key Technical Concepts:

- NaN-boxing: Values are uint64_t with tag bits in top 16 bits. QNAN=0x7FFC000000000000, TAG_INT=0x0002000000000000, TAG_OBJ=0x0003000000000000. LISA_OBJ(ptr) = QNAN | TAG_OBJ | (ptr & PAYLOAD_MASK). PAYLOAD_MASK=0x0000FFFFFFFFFFFF.

- Trampoline pattern: JIT functions return LISA_TAIL_PENDING(argc) sentinel (top 16 bits = 0xDEAD, low 8 bits = argc) for non-self tail calls. Callers loop iteratively via jit_trampoline().

- cj JIT framework: Header-only backends for x86-64 and ARM64. Registers by string names. Labels with forward-reference fixup. Builder layer (src/builder.h) provides cross-platform helpers.

- ARM64 backend bugs found: cj_lsr/cj_asr immediate paths exist but never encode the shift amount from src.constant. cj_lsl has no immediate path. cj_cset is empty. cj_csel doesn't encode condition code. cj_and immediate path never encodes bitmask. cj_stp/cj_ldp ignore pre/post-indexed modes. cj_mov(x29, sp) generates wrong instruction.

- x86 backend status: cj_shl/cj_shr/cj_sar all support 64-bit registers with arbitrary immediates (opcode 0xC1 with REX.W). cj_or supports r64+r64. cj_neg supports r64. cj_setcc works with 8-bit registers. cj_movzx(r64, r8) works. cj_and supports r64+r64 and r64+imm32.

- Register cache: Compile-time tracker with 4 cache register slots (x10-x13 on ARM64, r8-r11 on x86-64)

3. Files and Code Sections:

- lisa/jit.c — Main JIT compiler

- Fixed NaN-boxing comparison in OP_TAIL_CALL self-call check:

```c

/* Runtime self-call check: compare callee with current closure.

Callee on stack is NaN-boxed (QNAN|TAG_OBJ|ptr), but REG_CLOSURE

is a raw pointer. NaN-box REG_CLOSURE into TMP2 for comparison. */

cj_label not_self = cj_create_label(ctx);

int32_t callee_off = (int32_t)(-8 * (argc + 1));

emit_load64(ctx, REG_TMP1, REG_STKTOP, callee_off);

emit_load_imm64(ctx, REG_TMP2, QNAN | TAG_OBJ);

emit_or(ctx, REG_TMP2, REG_CLOSURE);

cj_cmp(ctx, reg(REG_TMP1), reg(REG_TMP2));

EMIT_JNE(ctx, not_self);

```

- Changed non-self tail call from calling helper to returning sentinel:

```c

/* Non-self tail call: return sentinel for trampoline */

cj_mark_label(ctx, not_self);

emit_load_imm64(ctx, REG_RET, LISA_TAIL_PENDING(argc));

emit_epilogue(ctx);

```

- All references to lisa_jit_tail_call_helper removed

- lisa/vm.h — VM header

- Added trampoline sentinel macros:

```c

/* JIT trampoline sentinel: top 16 bits = 0xDEAD (invalid as any lisa_value tag),

low 8 bits = argc for the pending tail call. */

#define LISA_TAIL_PENDING_BASE ((uint64_t)0xDEAD000000000000)

#define LISA_TAIL_PENDING(argc) (LISA_TAIL_PENDING_BASE | (uint64_t)(argc))

#define IS_TAIL_PENDING(v) (((v) >> 48) == 0xDEAD)

#define TAIL_PENDING_ARGC(v) ((int)((v) & 0xFF))

```

- Removed lisa_jit_tail_call_helper declaration

- lisa/vm.c — VM runtime

- Added forward declaration: static lisa_value jit_trampoline(lisa_vm *vm, lisa_value result);

- Added jit_trampoline() function with eager JIT compilation:

```c

static lisa_value jit_trampoline(lisa_vm *vm, lisa_value result) {

while (IS_TAIL_PENDING(result)) {

int argc = TAIL_PENDING_ARGC(result);

lisa_value callee = vm->stack_top[-1 - argc];

// ... validate callee ...

lisa_obj_closure *closure = AS_CLOSURE(callee);

// ... check arity ...

lisa_call_frame *frame = &vm->frames[vm->frame_count - 1];

close_upvalues(vm, frame->slots);

lisa_value *src = vm->stack_top - argc - 1;

memmove(frame->slots, src, (size_t)(argc + 1) * sizeof(lisa_value));

vm->stack_top = frame->slots + argc + 1;

frame->closure = closure;

frame->ip = closure->function->chunk.code;

/* JIT-compile the target if needed */

if (!closure->function->jit_code && vm->jit_enabled) {

lisa_jit_compile(vm, closure->function);

}

if (closure->function->jit_code) {

lisa_jit_fn jit_fn = (lisa_jit_fn)closure->function->jit_code;

result = jit_fn(vm, frame->closure, frame->slots);

} else {

int target_depth = vm->frame_count - 1;

lisa_run(vm, target_depth);

return vm->stack_top[-1];

}

}

return result;

}

```

- Added IS_TAIL_PENDING checks at all 3 JIT call sites: interpreter's OP_CALL, interpreter's OP_TAIL_CALL, and lisa_jit_call_helper

- Removed entire lisa_jit_tail_call_helper function (~45 lines)

- src/builder.h — Builder layer (read-only during planning)

- 632 lines, provides: prologue/epilogue, scratch registers (32-bit names only), if/else/loop control flow, calling convention, cj_builder_assign (handles ARM64 MOVZ+MOVK), branch_on_condition (maps cj_condition to platform branches), invert_condition

- Uses cj_condition enum from src/op.h

- src/arch/arm64/backend.h — ARM64 backend (read-only, 17k+ lines)

- cj_lsr immediate path (line 10640): has CJ_CONSTANT case but never reads src.constant — shift amount not encoded

- cj_asr immediate path (line 879): same bug — shift amount not encoded

- cj_lsl (line 10554): only register-register, no immediate path

- cj_cset (line 2864): empty function body

- cj_csel (line 2843): doesn't encode condition code bits

- cj_and immediate (line 780): never encodes bitmask

- cj_neg (line 11138): two-operand cj_neg(ctx, dst, src) using SUB Xd, XZR, Xn

- src/arch/x86_64/backend.h — x86 backend (read-only, 100k+ lines)

- cj_shl/cj_shr/cj_sar: all support r64+imm8 (line ~828/1239/1650)

- cj_or: supports r64+r64 (line 6127)

- cj_neg: supports r64 (line 70617), one-operand

- cj_setl/cj_setz/etc: work with r8 registers

- cj_movzx: supports r64←r8 (line 8180)

- cj_and: supports r64+r64 (line 5924) and r64+imm32 (line 5852)

- src/op.h — Operand types and condition enum

- cj_condition enum: CJ_COND_O through CJ_COND_G, with aliases CJ_COND_E=CJ_COND_Z, CJ_COND_NE=CJ_COND_NZ, etc.

- tests/test_harness_builder.c — Builder tests (read-only)

- Pattern: create ctx, prologue, use scratch/arg registers, do operations, epilogue, create_cj_fn, assert results, destroy

4. Errors and fixes:

- NaN-boxing mismatch in self-call comparison: Stack callee was NaN-boxed (QNAN|TAG_OBJ|ptr) but REG_CLOSURE was raw pointer — comparison always failed. Fixed by ORing QNAN|TAG_OBJ into REG_CLOSURE before comparing.

- C stack overflow on mutual recursion: Non-self tail calls recursively called lisa_jit_tail_call_helper → JIT → helper → JIT. Fixed with trampoline pattern using sentinel return value.

- Forward declaration error: jit_trampoline used in lisa_run before definition. Fixed by adding static lisa_value jit_trampoline(lisa_vm *vm, lisa_value result); forward declaration.

- Target closure not JIT-compiled in trampoline: Closures reached through trampoline (not call_closure) were never JIT-compiled, causing trampoline to fall to interpreter which recursively entered trampoline. Debug output showed jit=0x0. Fixed by adding eager JIT compilation: if (!closure->function->jit_code && vm->jit_enabled) lisa_jit_compile(vm, closure->function);

- Plan file write error: Attempted to write plan file without reading it first. Got File has not been read yet error.

5. Problem Solving:

- Self-call tail recursion: Fixed (works to 1M iterations)

- Mutual recursion tail calls: Fixed via trampoline (works to 100K iterations)

- All verification tests pass: fac(20), fib(30), tail loop 1M, mutual recursion 100K, closures, list ops

- Benchmark: ~2x speedup: fib(35) in 0.53s (JIT) vs 1.05s (interpreter) with -O2

- Builder exploration: Complete analysis of what's missing and what works in both backends

6. All user messages:

- "since we have control over cj, can we fix these in the builder? the individual instructions are created from code generation, so we'd have to touch that. i'd defer those until we fixed the builder."

- Get user approval on the plan

- Implement the 6 new builder functions in src/builder.h

- Add tests in tests/test_harness_builder.c

- (Future, after builder is done) Simplify lisa/jit.c to use the new builder functions instead of #ifdef blocks

result
1→# Rewrite JIT: Register Cache + Inline Fast Paths
     2→
     3→## Context
     4→
     5→The current `lisa/jit.c` implements a "stack machine in native code" — every operation pushes/pops values through memory and calls C helpers for all arithmetic and comparisons. Benchmarks show no speedup over the interpreter. The user wants a "real JIT, no shortcuts."
     6→
     7→This plan rewrites `jit.c` to use a **compile-time register cache** and **inline integer fast paths**. Only `jit.c` changes — all other files (vm.c helpers, object.h, jit.h, Makefile) remain as-is.
     8→
     9→## Core Concept: Register Cache
    10→
    11→A compile-time tracker that knows the top N values of the bytecode stack are currently in CPU registers instead of memory. This eliminates load/store traffic for common instruction sequences like `GET_LOCAL; GET_LOCAL; ADD`.
    12→
    13→### Design
    14→
    15→```
    16→reg_cache_t cache;  // compile-time state
    17→cache.depth = 0;    // how many stack values are in registers (0..MAX_CACHE)
    18→cache.regs[0..3];   // fixed register names for cache slots
    19→```
    20→
    21→- **MAX_CACHE = 4** — 4 caller-saved registers per platform
    22→- `cache.regs[0]` = bottom of cache (deepest stack value), `cache.regs[depth-1]` = top of stack
    23→- **Push to cache**: if `depth < MAX_CACHE`, place value in `cache.regs[depth++]`; otherwise, spill `cache.regs[0]` to memory, shift entries down, put new value in `cache.regs[MAX_CACHE-1]`
    24→- **Pop from cache**: if `depth > 0`, value is in `cache.regs[--depth]`; otherwise, decrement REG_STKTOP and load from memory
    25→- **Flush**: write all cached values to memory stack (store at `[REG_STKTOP + i*8]`), advance REG_STKTOP by `depth * 8`, set `depth = 0`
    26→- **Required flush points**: before any helper call, before any branch/jump, at every branch target label
    27→
    28→### Register Assignments
    29→
    30→| Purpose | x86-64 | ARM64 |
    31→|---------|--------|-------|
    32→| **Callee-saved (persistent)** | | |
    33→| vm pointer | `rbx` | `x19` |
    34→| slots pointer | `r12` | `x20` |
    35→| closure pointer | `r13` | `x21` |
    36→| stack_top (committed) | `r14` | `x22` |
    37→| constants pointer | `r15` | `x23` |
    38→| **Cache (4 slots)** | | |
    39→| cache[0] | `r8` | `x10` |
    40→| cache[1] | `r9` | `x11` |
    41→| cache[2] | `r10` | `x12` |
    42→| cache[3] | `r11` | `x13` |
    43→| **Scratch (computation)** | | |
    44→| scratch / return | `rax` | `x0` |
    45→| scratch / arg regs | `rcx,rdx,rsi,rdi` | `x1-x9,x14,x15` |
    46→
    47→Cache registers are caller-saved, so they're naturally clobbered by C calls. Since we always flush before calling, this is consistent.
    48→
    49→## Inline Fast Paths
    50→
    51→### Integer type check
    52→
    53→```
    54→// IS_INT(v): top 16 bits == 0x7FFE
    55→mov scratch, value
    56→shr scratch, 48          // x86: shr; ARM64: lsr
    57→cmp scratch, 0x7FFE      // fits
... (truncated)

    51→static inline void cj_builder_loop_end(cj_ctx *ctx, cj_builder_block block);
    52→
    53→static inline cj_builder_if_block cj_builder_if(cj_ctx *ctx, cj_operand lhs, cj_operand rhs,
    54→                                                cj_condition cond);
    55→static inline void cj_builder_else(cj_ctx *ctx, cj_builder_if_block *block);
    56→static inline void cj_builder_endif(cj_ctx *ctx, cj_builder_if_block *block);
    57→
    58→static inline cj_builder_for_loop cj_builder_for_begin(cj_ctx *ctx, cj_operand counter,
    59→                                                       cj_operand start, cj_operand limit,
    60→                                                       cj_operand step, cj_condition exit_cond);
    61→static inline void cj_builder_for_continue(cj_ctx *ctx, cj_builder_for_loop *loop);
    62→static inline void cj_builder_for_break(cj_ctx *ctx, cj_builder_for_loop *loop);
    63→static inline void cj_builder_for_end(cj_ctx *ctx, cj_builder_for_loop *loop);
    64→

In `src/arch/arm64/backend.h`: Find the implementations of `cj_lsr`, `cj_asr`, `cj_lsl`, `cj_cset`, `cj_csel`, `cj_and`, `cj_neg`, `cj_orr` (or `cj_or`). Pay special attention to:\n   - `cj_lsr`: the CJ_CONSTANT case that doesn't encode the shift amount\n   - `cj_asr`: same issue\n   - `cj_lsl`: whether there's an immediate path at all\n   - `cj_cset`: reported as empty body\n   - `cj_csel`: doesn't encode condition code\n   - `cj_and` immediate: never encodes bitmask\n   - `cj_neg`: two-operand signature\n   - `cj_orr`: what's available\n\n2. In `src/ctx.h` or `src/ctx.c`: Find the `cj_add_u32` function (raw 32-bit word emission) that I can use for manual encoding.\n\n3. In `src/op.h`: Show the full `cj_condition` enum.\n\n4. In `src/register.h` and `src/arch/arm64/registers.h`: How does `parse_reg` work? How are register numbers obtained from string names?\n\nBe thorough - show enough context around each function to understand the full picture.",

result
I now have everything needed for a complete and accurate report. Here is the full analysis:

---

## 1. Function-by-function analysis: `src/arch/arm64/backend.h`

### `cj_lsr` (line 10621)

```c
static inline void cj_lsr(cj_ctx* ctx, cj_operand dst, cj_operand src) {
  // REG, REG path — correct, encodes LSRV (variable shift)
  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && src.type == CJ_REGISTER && ...) {
    int rd = arm64_parse_reg(dst.reg);  int rn = rd;
    int rm = arm64_parse_reg(src.reg);
    uint32_t instr = 0x1AC02400;          // LSRV base opcode
    instr |= (sf << 31);
    instr |= (rd & 0x1f);
    instr |= ((rn & 0x1f) << 5);
    instr |= ((rm & 0x1f) << 16);
    cj_add_u32(ctx, instr);
    return;
  }
  // REG, CONSTANT path — BROKEN
  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && src.type == CJ_CONSTANT) {
    int rd = arm64_parse_reg(dst.reg);  int rn = rd;
    uint32_t instr = 0x53007C00;          // UBFM base, shift=0 baked in 0x7C00
    instr |= (sf << 31);
    instr |= (rd & 0x1f);
    instr |= ((rn & 0x1f) << 5);
    // BUG: src.constant is NEVER read. The shift amount bits [21:16] (immr)
    // and bits [15:10] (imms) are left as whatever 0x53007C00 encodes (immr=0,
    // imms=0x1f/0x3f), so you always get a shift of 0.
    cj_add_u32(ctx, instr);
    return;
  }
}
```

**Bug**: The `CJ_CONSTANT` branch sets a fixed `0x53007C00` (which has `immr=0`, `imms` encoding the register width - 1) but never reads `src.constant` to fill the actual shift amount into `immr[21:16]`. Every immediate LSR emits a no-op shift-by-zero.

---

### `cj_asr` (line 860)

```c
static inline void cj_asr(cj_ctx* ctx, cj_operand dst, cj_operand src) {
  // REG, REG path — ASRV, correct
  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && src.type == CJ_REGISTER && ...) {
    uint32_t instr = 0x1AC02800;  // ASRV
    // ... encodes rd, rn=rd, rm correctly ...
    cj_add_u32(ctx, instr);
    return;
  }
  // REG, CONSTANT path — BROKEN (same pattern as cj_lsr)
  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && src.type == CJ_CONSTANT) {
    int rd = arm64_parse_reg(dst.reg);  int rn = rd;
    uint32_t instr = 0x13007C00;  // SBFM base, shift baked to 0
    instr |= (sf << 31);
    instr |= (rd & 0x1f);
    instr |= ((rn & 0x1f) << 5);
    // BUG: src.constant never read; immr always 0
    cj_add_u32(ctx, instr);
    return;
  }
}
```

**Bug**: Identical problem to `cj_lsr`. The SBFM encoding for ASR #N requires `immr = N` in bits [21:16] and `imms = 63` (or 31 for 32-bit) in bits [15:10]. Neither is set from `src.constant`.

---

### `cj_lsl` (line 10554)

```c
static inline void cj_lsl(cj_ctx* ctx, cj_operand dst, cj_operand src) {
  // REG, REG path only — encodes LSLV correctly
  if (dst.type == CJ_REGISTER && src.type == CJ_REGISTER && ...) {
    uint32_t instr = 0x1AC02000;  // LSLV
    // ... rd, rn=rd, rm ...
    cj_add_u32(ctx, instr);
    return;
  }
  // NO CJ_CONSTANT path at all — function ends here.
}
```

**Bug**: There is no immediate path. LSL #N on ARM64 is encoded as UBFM Rd, Rn, #(-N MOD size), #(si
... (truncated)
⚙ Edit

Good, I have everything I need. Now let me write the updated plan for the builder additions.

Edit — <claude-internal>
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "<claude-internal>",
  "old_string": "# Rewrite JIT: Register Cache + Inline Fast Paths\n\n## Context\n\nThe current `lisa/jit.c` implements a \"stack machine in native code\" — every operation pushes/pops values through memory and calls C helpers for all arithmetic and comparisons. Benchmarks show no speedup over the interpreter. The user wants a \"real JIT, no shortcuts.\"\n\nThis plan rewrites `jit.c` to use a **compile-time register cache** and **inline integer fast paths**. Only `jit.c` changes — all other files (vm.c helpers, object.h, jit.h, Makefile) remain as-is.\n\n## Core Concept: Register Cache\n\nA compile-time tracker that knows the top N values of the bytecode stack are currently in CPU registers instead of memory. This eliminates load/store traffic for common instruction sequences like `GET_LOCAL; GET_LOCAL; ADD`.\n\n### Design\n\n```\nreg_cache_t cache;  // compile-time state\ncache.depth = 0;    // how many stack values are in registers (0..MAX_CACHE)\ncache.regs[0..3];   // fixed register names for cache slots\n```\n\n- **MAX_CACHE = 4** — 4 caller-saved registers per platform\n- `cache.regs[0]` = bottom of cache (deepest stack value), `cache.regs[depth-1]` = top of stack\n- **Push to cache**: if `depth < MAX_CACHE`, place value in `cache.regs[depth++]`; otherwise, spill `cache.regs[0]` to memory, shift entries down, put new value in `cache.regs[MAX_CACHE-1]`\n- **Pop from cache**: if `depth > 0`, value is in `cache.regs[--depth]`; otherwise, decrement REG_STKTOP and load from memory\n- **Flush**: write all cached values to memory stack (store at `[REG_STKTOP + i*8]`), advance REG_STKTOP by `depth * 8`, set `depth = 0`\n- **Required flush points**: before any helper call, before any branch/jump, at every branch target label\n\n### Register Assignments\n\n| Purpose | x86-64 | ARM64 |\n|---------|--------|-------|\n| **Callee-saved (persistent)** | | |\n| vm pointer | `rbx` | `x19` |\n| slots pointer | `r12` 
... (truncated)

  "plan": "# Add Cross-Platform Builder Functions to `src/builder.h`\n\n## Context\n\nThe JIT rewrite (`lisa/jit.c`) is complete and working (~2x speedup), but uses extensive `#ifdef __x86_64__ / __aarch64__` blocks because the cj builder layer lacks several primitives that the JIT needs. Meanwhile, several ARM64 backend functions have encoding bugs (shift-by-immediate never encodes the shift amount, CSET is empty, etc.). The backend is auto-generated from codegen scripts, so fixing those is a separate task.\n\nThis plan adds **7 new cross-platform builder functions** to `src/builder.h`. On x86-64, they delegate to working backend functions. On ARM64, they use raw `cj_add_u32()` encoding to work around backend bugs.\n\n## Files Modified\n\n- **`src/builder.h`** — add 7 new functions + internal helpers\n- **`tests/test_harness_builder.c`** — add tests for each new function\n\n## New Builder Functions\n\n### 1. `cj_builder_shl(ctx, dst, shift_amount)`\nShift left by immediate. `dst` is modified in place.\n\n- **x86**: `cj_shl(ctx, dst, cj_make_constant(shift))` — backend works correctly\n- **ARM64**: raw-encode `UBFM Xd, Xn, #(64-N), #(63-N)` via `cj_add_u32()` (backend has no immediate path for LSL)\n\nARM64 encoding detail:\n```\nLSL Xd, Xn, #N  →  UBFM Xd, Xn, #(-N MOD 64), #(63-N)\nint immr = (64 - shift) & 63;\nint imms = 63 - shift;\nuint32_t instr = 0xD3400000 | (rd & 0x1f) | ((rn & 0x1f) << 5)\n               | ((imms & 0x3f) << 10) | ((immr & 0x3f) << 16);\n// For 32-bit (w-regs): base=0x53000000, size=32 instead of 64\n```\n\n### 2. `cj_builder_shr(ctx, dst, shift_amount)`\nLogical shift right by immediate. `dst` modified in place.\n\n- **x86**: `cj_shr(ctx, dst, cj_make_constant(shift))` — backend works\n- **ARM64**: raw-encode `UBFM Xd, Xn, #N, #63` via `cj_add_u32()` (backend bug: never reads `src.constant`)\n\nARM64 encoding:\n```\nLSR Xd, Xn, #N  →  UB
... (truncated)
