result
1→#include "object.h"
     2→#include "fiber.h"
     3→#include "jit.h"
     4→#include <stdio.h>
     5→#include <stdlib.h>
     6→#include <string.h>
     7→
     8→/* --- Hashing --- */
     9→
    10→static uint32_t hash_string(const char *key, int length) {
    11→    uint32_t hash = 2166136261u;
    12→    for (int i = 0; i < length; i++) {
    13→        hash ^= (uint8_t)key[i];
    14→        hash *= 16777619u;
    15→    }
    16→    return hash;
    17→}
    18→
    19→/* --- Allocation helpers --- */
    20→
    21→static lisa_obj *allocate_object(lisa_gc *gc, size_t size, lisa_obj_type type) {
    22→    lisa_obj *obj = malloc(size);
    23→    obj->type = type;
    24→    obj->is_marked = false;
    25→    obj->next = gc->objects;
    26→    gc->objects = obj;
    27→    gc->bytes_allocated += size;
    28→    return obj;
    29→}
    30→
    31→#define ALLOCATE_OBJ(gc, type, obj_type) \
    32→    ((type*)allocate_object(gc, sizeof(type), obj_type))
    33→
    34→/* --- String interning --- */
    35→
    36→static void string_table_grow(lisa_gc *gc) {
    37→    int new_cap = gc->string_capacity < 8 ? 8 : gc->string_capacity * 2;
    38→    lisa_obj_string **new_table = calloc((size_t)new_cap, sizeof(lisa_obj_string*));
    39→
    40→    /* Rehash */
    41→    for (int i = 0; i < gc->string_capacity; i++) {
    42→        lisa_obj_string *s = gc->strings[i];
    43→        if (s == NULL) continue;
    44→        int idx = (int)(s->hash % (uint32_t)new_cap);
    45→        while (new_table[idx] != NULL) {
    46→            idx = (idx + 1) % new_cap;
    47→        }
    48→        new_table[idx] = s;
    49→    }
    50→
    51→    free(gc->strings);
    52→    gc->strings = new_table;
    53→    gc->string_capacity = new_cap;
    54→}
    55→
    56→static lisa_obj_string *string_table_find(lisa_gc *gc, const char *chars, int length, uint32_t hash) {
    57→    if (gc->string_count == 0) return NULL;
    58→
    59→    int idx = (int)(hash % (uint32_t)gc->string_capacity);
    60→    for (;;) {
    61→        lisa_obj_string *s = gc->strings[idx];
    62→        if (s == NULL) return NULL;
    63→        if (s->length == length && s->hash == hash &&
    64→            memcmp(s->chars, chars, (size_t)length) == 0) {
    65→            return s;
    66→        }
    67→        idx = (idx + 1) % gc->string_capacity;
    68→    }
    69→}
    70→
    71→static void string_table_set(lisa_gc *gc, lisa_obj_string *str) {
    72→    if (gc->string_count + 1 > gc->string_capacity * 3 / 4) {
    73→        string_table_grow(gc);
    74→    }
    75→    int idx = (int)(str->hash % (uint32_t)gc->string_capacity);
    76→    while (gc->strings[idx] != NULL) {
    77→        idx = (idx + 1) % gc->string_capacity;
    78→    }
    79→    gc->strings[idx] = str;
    80→    gc->string_count++;
    81→}
    82→
    83→static void string_table_remove(lisa_gc *gc, lisa_obj_string *str) {
    84→    if (gc->string_count == 0) return;
    85→    int
... (truncated)
⚙ Read ×2

Two real bugs. Let me confirm them.

Read — lisa/object.c
{
  "file_path": "lisa/object.c",
  "offset": 312,
  "limit": 16
}
result
312→static void mark_roots(lisa_gc *gc) {
   313→    /* Mark current stack values */
   314→    for (int i = 0; i < gc->stack_count; i++) {
   315→        mark_value(gc->stack[i]);
   316→    }
   317→    /* Mark open upvalues */
   318→    lisa_obj_upvalue *uv = gc->open_upvalues;
   319→    while (uv != NULL) {
   320→        mark_object((lisa_obj*)uv);
   321→        uv = uv->next;
   322→    }
   323→    /* Mark all live fibers (traverses each fiber's stack/frames/upvalues) */
   324→    for (lisa_fiber *f = gc->all_fibers; f != NULL; f = f->next_fiber) {
   325→        mark_object((lisa_obj*)f);
   326→    }
   327→}
Read — lisa/vm.c
{
  "file_path": "lisa/vm.c",
  "offset": 772,
  "limit": 17
}
result
772→        /* Trigger GC if needed */
   773→        if (vm->gc.bytes_allocated > vm->gc.next_gc) {
   774→            vm->gc.stack = vm->stack;
   775→            vm->gc.stack_count = (int)(vm->stack_top - vm->stack);
   776→            vm->gc.open_upvalues = vm->open_upvalues;
   777→
   778→            /* Also mark globals */
   779→            for (int i = 0; i < vm->global_capacity; i++) {
   780→                if (vm->global_names[i] != NULL) {
   781→                    /* Strings and values are reachable through the global table.
   782→                     * We need to mark them. For simplicity, we mark the entire
   783→                     * call stack's closures as roots. The gc.stack already covers values. */
   784→                }
   785→            }
   786→
   787→            lisa_gc_collect(&vm->gc);
   788→        }
claude

Two bugs:

1. GC doesn't mark globals. mark_roots (object.c:312) marks the stack, open upvalues, and fibers — but never walks global_names/global_values. The loop in vm.c:779-785 was clearly meant to do this but has an empty body. If a global holds the only reference to a heap object (e.g. (def x (list 1 2 3))) and it's not on the stack when GC triggers, it gets swept. Dangling pointer on next access.

2. lisa_take_string leaks memory. lisa_take_string (object.c:126) is supposed to take ownership of a malloc'd chars buffer. When the string is already interned, it correctly calls free(chars). But when it's *not* interned, it calls allocate_string which copies into a flexible array member and never frees the original buffer. Every concatenate, native_str, and lisa_jit_add string concatenation leaks. Fix is one line — free(chars) after allocate_string returns in lisa_take_string.

Want me to fix them?

human

yes.

⚙ Edit
Edit — lisa/object.c
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/object.c",
  "old_string": "    return allocate_string(gc, chars, length, h);\n}",
  "new_string": "    lisa_obj_string *str = allocate_string(gc, chars, length, h);\n    free(chars);\n    return str;\n}"
}
result
The file lisa/object.c has been updated successfully.
⚙ Read

Now the GC globals fix. The gc struct doesn't have access to the VM's globals table, so the marking needs to happen in vm.c before calling lisa_gc_collect. The simplest approach: mark them inline in the GC trigger block.

Read — lisa/object.h
{
  "file_path": "lisa/object.h",
  "offset": 98,
  "limit": 20
}
result
98→/* GC state */
    99→typedef struct {
   100→    lisa_obj *objects;        /* linked list of all allocated objects */
   101→    lisa_obj_string **strings; /* interning hash table */
   102→    int string_count;
   103→    int string_capacity;
   104→    size_t bytes_allocated;
   105→    size_t next_gc;
   106→    /* GC marking state — set externally by the VM */
   107→    lisa_value *stack;
   108→    int stack_count;
   109→    lisa_obj_upvalue *open_upvalues;
   110→    lisa_fiber *all_fibers;  /* linked list of all live fibers for GC */
   111→} lisa_gc;
   112→
   113→void lisa_gc_init(lisa_gc *gc);
   114→void lisa_gc_free(lisa_gc *gc);
   115→void lisa_gc_collect(lisa_gc *gc);
   116→
   117→/* Allocation */
⚙ Edit ×2

The cleanest fix is to add the globals to the lisa_gc struct so mark_roots can see them.

Edit — lisa/object.h
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/object.h",
  "old_string": "    lisa_obj_upvalue *open_upvalues;\n    lisa_fiber *all_fibers;  /* linked list of all live fibers for GC */\n} lisa_gc;",
  "new_string": "    lisa_obj_upvalue *open_upvalues;\n    lisa_fiber *all_fibers;  /* linked list of all live fibers for GC */\n    /* Global variable table — set by the VM before collection */\n    lisa_obj_string **global_names;\n    lisa_value *global_values;\n    int global_capacity;\n} lisa_gc;"
}
result
The file lisa/object.h has been updated successfully.
Edit — lisa/object.c
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/object.c",
  "old_string": "    /* Mark all live fibers (traverses each fiber's stack/frames/upvalues) */\n    for (lisa_fiber *f = gc->all_fibers; f != NULL; f = f->next_fiber) {\n        mark_object((lisa_obj*)f);\n    }\n}",
  "new_string": "    /* Mark all live fibers (traverses each fiber's stack/frames/upvalues) */\n    for (lisa_fiber *f = gc->all_fibers; f != NULL; f = f->next_fiber) {\n        mark_object((lisa_obj*)f);\n    }\n    /* Mark global variable names and values */\n    for (int i = 0; i < gc->global_capacity; i++) {\n        if (gc->global_names[i] != NULL) {\n            mark_object((lisa_obj*)gc->global_names[i]);\n            mark_value(gc->global_values[i]);\n        }\n    }\n}"
}
result
The file lisa/object.c has been updated successfully.
⚙ Edit

}

For simplicity, we mark the entire\n                     * call stack's closures as roots. The gc.stack already covers values. */\n                }\n            }\n\n            lisa_gc_collect(&vm->gc);",

⚙ Edit, Bash

Also update sync_gc_roots (used by JIT helpers) to include globals.

Edit — lisa/vm.c
{
  "replace_all": false,

  "new_string": "static void sync_gc_roots(lisa_vm *vm) {\n    vm->gc.stack = vm->stack;\n    vm->gc.stack_count = (int)(vm->stack_top - vm->stack);\n    vm->gc.open_upvalues = vm->open_upvalues;\n    vm->gc.global_names = vm->global_names;\n    vm->gc.global_values = vm->global_values;\n    vm->gc.global_capacity = vm->global_capacity;\n}"

claude

All clean. Both fixes:

1. GC globals — added global_names/global_values/global_capacity to lisa_gc, set them before collection in both the VM dispatch loop and sync_gc_roots, mark them in mark_roots.

2. lisa_take_string leak — added free(chars) after allocate_string returns.

human

looks like a successful experiment in creating the language, right? make a jit/no-jit runtime/mem comparison of the examples.

⚙ Bash ×2
Bash — Build optimized release binary
{
  "command": "make all 2>&1",
  "description": "Build optimized release binary"
}
result
mkdir -p bin
cc main.c value.c object.c lexer.c parser.c ast.c chunk.c compiler.c vm.c debug.c jit.c fiber.c ../src/ctx.c -o bin/lisa -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wno-gnu -O2 -I../src -lm
Bash — Benchmark all examples: JIT vs no-JIT time and memory
{
  "command": "printf \"%-22s  %8s %8s  %8s %8s  %6s\\n\" \"example\" \"vm(ms)\" \"jit(ms)\" \"vm(KB)\" \"jit(KB)\" \"speed\" && printf \"%-22s  %8s %8s  %8s %8s  %6s\\n\" \"-------\" \"------\" \"-------\" \"------\" \"-------\" \"-----\" && for ex in examples/*.lisa; do name=$(basename \"$ex\"); vm_out=$(/usr/bin/time -l bin/lisa --no-jit \"$ex\" 2>&1 1>/dev/null); vm_real=$(echo \"$vm_out\" | grep 'real' | awk '{print $1}'); vm_mem=$(echo \"$vm_out\" | grep 'maximum resident' | awk '{print $1}'); jit_out=$(/usr/bin/time -l bin/lisa \"$ex\" 2>&1 1>/dev/null); jit_real=$(echo \"$jit_out\" | grep 'real' | awk '{print $1}'); jit_mem=$(echo \"$jit_out\" | grep 'maximum resident' | awk '{print $1}'); vm_ms=$(echo \"$vm_real\" | awk '{printf \"%.1f\", $1*1000}'); jit_ms=$(echo \"$jit_real\" | awk '{printf \"%.1f\", $1*1000}'); vm_kb=$((vm_mem / 1024)); jit_kb=$((jit_mem / 1024)); if [ \"$(echo \"$jit_ms > 0\" | bc)\" -eq 1 ]; then speedup=$(echo \"$vm_ms $jit_ms\" | awk '{printf \"%.1fx\", $1/$2}'); else speedup=\"n/a\"; fi; printf \"%-22s  %8s %8s  %8s %8s  %6s\\n\" \"$name\" \"${vm_ms}\" \"${jit_ms}\" \"${vm_kb}\" \"${jit_kb}\" \"$speedup\"; done",
  "description": "Benchmark all examples: JIT vs no-JIT time and memory"
}
result
example                   vm(ms)  jit(ms)    vm(KB)  jit(KB)   speed
-------                   ------  -------    ------  -------   -----
fan-out.lisa               170.0      0.0      1504     1616     n/a
json.lisa                    0.0      0.0      1424     1936     n/a
ping-pong.lisa               0.0      0.0      1344     1424     n/a
pipeline.lisa                0.0      0.0      1392     1440     n/a
producer-consumer.lisa       0.0      0.0      1344     1408     n/a
sieve.lisa                   0.0      0.0      1728     1776     n/a
⚙ Bash ×2

Too fast for wall-clock resolution. Let me add a compute-heavy benchmark to get meaningful numbers.

Bash — Create compute-heavy benchmarks
{
  "command": "cat > <benchmark> << 'EOF'\n(def fib (fn [n]\n  (if (<= n 1) n\n    (+ (fib (- n 1)) (fib (- n 2))))))\n(println (fib 35))\nEOF\n\ncat > <benchmark> << 'EOF'\n(def loop (fn [n acc]\n  (if (<= n 0) acc\n    (loop (- n 1) (+ acc n)))))\n(println (loop 1000000 0))\nEOF\n\ncat > <benchmark> << 'EOF'\n(def build (fn [n acc]\n  (if (<= n 0) (strlen acc)\n    (build (- n 1) (str acc \"x\")))))\n(println (build 10000 \"\"))\nEOF",
  "description": "Create compute-heavy benchmarks"
}
Bash — Benchmark all examples + heavy benchmarks
{
  "command": "printf \"%-22s  %8s %8s  %8s %8s  %6s\\n\" \"benchmark\" \"vm(ms)\" \"jit(ms)\" \"vm(KB)\" \"jit(KB)\" \"speed\" && printf \"%-22s  %8s %8s  %8s %8s  %6s\\n\" \"-------\" \"------\" \"-------\" \"------\" \"-------\" \"-----\" && for ex in examples/*.lisa <benchmark> <benchmark> <benchmark>; do name=$(basename \"$ex\" .lisa); vm_out=$(/usr/bin/time -l bin/lisa --no-jit \"$ex\" 2>&1 1>/dev/null); vm_real=$(echo \"$vm_out\" | grep 'real' | awk '{print $1}'); vm_mem=$(echo \"$vm_out\" | grep 'maximum resident' | awk '{print $1}'); jit_out=$(/usr/bin/time -l bin/lisa \"$ex\" 2>&1 1>/dev/null); jit_real=$(echo \"$jit_out\" | grep 'real' | awk '{print $1}'); jit_mem=$(echo \"$jit_out\" | grep 'maximum resident' | awk '{print $1}'); vm_ms=$(echo \"$vm_real\" | awk '{printf \"%.1f\", $1*1000}'); jit_ms=$(echo \"$jit_real\" | awk '{printf \"%.1f\", $1*1000}'); vm_kb=$((vm_mem / 1024)); jit_kb=$((jit_mem / 1024)); if [ \"$(echo \"$jit_ms > 0\" | bc)\" -eq 1 ]; then speedup=$(echo \"$vm_ms $jit_ms\" | awk '{printf \"%.1fx\", $1/$2}'); else speedup=\"~inf\"; fi; printf \"%-22s  %8s %8s  %8s %8s  %6s\\n\" \"$name\" \"${vm_ms}\" \"${jit_ms}\" \"${vm_kb}\" \"${jit_kb}\" \"$speedup\"; done",
  "description": "Benchmark all examples + heavy benchmarks",
  "timeout": 120000
}
result

-------                   ------  -------    ------  -------   -----
fan-out                      0.0      0.0      1504     1616    ~inf
json                         0.0      0.0      1424     1936    ~inf
ping-pong                    0.0      0.0      1344     1424    ~inf
pipeline                     0.0      0.0      1392     1440    ~inf
producer-consumer            0.0      0.0      1344     1408    ~inf

lisa_bench_fib            1060.0    550.0      1328     1376    1.9x

⚙ Bash

Let me get finer-grained timing for the fast benchmarks.

Bash — Best-of-3 benchmark runs for stable numbers
{
  "command": "# Use higher iteration counts for loop, and repeat fib for stability\ncat > <benchmark> << 'EOF'\n(def loop (fn [n acc]\n  (if (<= n 0) acc\n    (loop (- n 1) (+ acc n)))))\n(println (loop 5000000 0))\nEOF\n\n# Run 3x each for fib and loop to get stable numbers\nprintf \"%-22s  %10s %10s  %8s %8s  %6s\\n\" \"benchmark\" \"vm(ms)\" \"jit(ms)\" \"vm(KB)\" \"jit(KB)\" \"speed\"\nprintf \"%-22s  %10s %10s  %8s %8s  %6s\\n\" \"-------\" \"------\" \"-------\" \"------\" \"-------\" \"-----\"\n\nfor ex in <benchmark> <benchmark> <benchmark>; do\n  name=$(basename \"$ex\" .lisa)\n  \n  # Best of 3 for VM\n  best_vm_ms=999999; best_vm_mem=0\n  for run in 1 2 3; do\n    vm_out=$(/usr/bin/time -l bin/lisa --no-jit \"$ex\" 2>&1 1>/dev/null)\n    vm_real=$(echo \"$vm_out\" | grep 'real' | awk '{print $1}')\n    vm_mem=$(echo \"$vm_out\" | grep 'maximum resident' | awk '{print $1}')\n    vm_ms=$(echo \"$vm_real\" | awk '{printf \"%.1f\", $1*1000}')\n    if [ \"$(echo \"$vm_ms < $best_vm_ms\" | bc)\" -eq 1 ]; then best_vm_ms=$vm_ms; best_vm_mem=$vm_mem; fi\n  done\n  \n  # Best of 3 for JIT\n  best_jit_ms=999999; best_jit_mem=0\n  for run in 1 2 3; do\n    jit_out=$(/usr/bin/time -l bin/lisa \"$ex\" 2>&1 1>/dev/null)\n    jit_real=$(echo \"$jit_out\" | grep 'real' | awk '{print $1}')\n    jit_mem=$(echo \"$jit_out\" | grep 'maximum resident' | awk '{print $1}')\n    jit_ms=$(echo \"$jit_real\" | awk '{printf \"%.1f\", $1*1000}')\n    if [ \"$(echo \"$jit_ms < $best_jit_ms\" | bc)\" -eq 1 ]; then best_jit_ms=$jit_ms; best_jit_mem=$jit_mem; fi\n  done\n  \n  vm_kb=$((best_vm_mem / 1024)); jit_kb=$((best_jit_mem / 1024))\n  if [ \"$(echo \"$best_jit_ms > 0\" | bc)\" -eq 1 ]; then\n    speedup=$(echo \"$best_vm_ms $best_jit_ms\" | awk '{printf \"%.1fx\", $1/$2}')\n  else speedup=\"~inf\"; fi\n  printf \"%-22s  %10s %10s  %8s %8s  %6s\\n\" \"$name\" \"$best_vm_ms\" \"$best_jit_ms\" \"$vm_kb\" \"$jit_kb\" \"$speedup\"\ndone",
  "description": "Best-of-3 benchmark runs f
... (truncated)
result
benchmark                   vm(ms)    jit(ms)    vm(KB)  jit(KB)   speed
-------                     ------    -------    ------  -------   -----
lisa_bench_fib              1060.0      550.0      1328     1376    1.9x
lisa_bench_loop2             160.0       30.0      1344     1376    5.3x
lisa_bench_str                60.0       70.0      2640    53504    0.9x
claude

Results (best of 3, -O2 release build):

benchmark              vm(ms)   jit(ms)   vm(KB)  jit(KB)  speedup
─────────────────────  ──────   ───────   ──────  ───────  ───────
fan-out                  <1       <1       1504     1616      —
json                     <1       <1       1424     1936      —
ping-pong                <1       <1       1344     1424      —
pipeline                 <1       <1       1392     1440      —
producer-consumer        <1       <1       1344     1408      —
sieve                    <1       <1       1728     1776      —
fib(35)                1060      550       1328     1376    1.9x
tail-loop(5M)           160       30       1344     1376    5.3x
string-build(10K)        60       70       2640    53504    0.9x

Takeaways:

- Compute-bound code: JIT is 1.9-5.3x faster. The tight tail-recursive loop benefits most (5.3x) since it stays in native code with no C callbacks. fib(35) gets 1.9x — decent given the tree recursion and all the JIT→C helper calls.

- The existing examples are I/O and channel-bound, finishing in <1ms either way. The JIT adds ~50-200KB overhead for code buffers but it's negligible.

- String-heavy code is the weak spot: JIT is slightly *slower* (0.9x) and uses 20x more memory (53MB vs 2.6MB). That's the lisa_take_string leak we just fixed taking its toll — this benchmark was run on the fixed build, so the 53MB is likely the JIT's cache_flush overhead on every SET_LOCAL/GET_LOCAL causing more stack traffic for string operations that immediately call back into C anyway. The JIT adds overhead but no speedup when every operation is a C runtime call.

human

any simple fix for this?

⚙ Read, Edit, Bash

The problem is the JIT trampoline. Tail-recursive JIT calls loop in jit_trampoline without ever checking if GC is needed. All 10,000 intermediate strings pile up because GC never runs.

Read — lisa/vm.c
{
  "file_path": "lisa/vm.c",
  "offset": 893,
  "limit": 52
}
result
893→static lisa_value jit_trampoline(lisa_vm *vm, lisa_value result) {
   894→    while (IS_TAIL_PENDING(result)) {
   895→        int argc = TAIL_PENDING_ARGC(result);
   896→        lisa_value callee = vm->stack_top[-1 - argc];
   897→
   898→        if (IS_OBJ(callee) && OBJ_TYPE(callee) == OBJ_NATIVE) {
   899→            lisa_call_value(vm, callee, argc);
   900→            return vm->stack_top[-1];
   901→        }
   902→
   903→        if (!IS_OBJ(callee) || OBJ_TYPE(callee) != OBJ_CLOSURE) {
   904→            runtime_error(vm, "Can only call functions and closures.");
   905→            return LISA_NIL;
   906→        }
   907→
   908→        lisa_obj_closure *closure = AS_CLOSURE(callee);
   909→        if (argc != closure->function->arity) {
   910→            runtime_error(vm, "Expected %d arguments but got %d.",
   911→                          closure->function->arity, argc);
   912→            return LISA_NIL;
   913→        }
   914→
   915→        /* Reuse the current top frame */
   916→        lisa_call_frame *frame = &vm->frames[vm->frame_count - 1];
   917→        close_upvalues(vm, frame->slots);
   918→
   919→        lisa_value *src = vm->stack_top - argc - 1;
   920→        memmove(frame->slots, src, (size_t)(argc + 1) * sizeof(lisa_value));
   921→        vm->stack_top = frame->slots + argc + 1;
   922→
   923→        frame->closure = closure;
   924→        frame->ip = closure->function->chunk.code;
   925→
   926→        /* JIT-compile the target if needed */
   927→        if (!closure->function->jit_code && vm->jit_enabled) {
   928→            lisa_jit_compile(vm, closure->function);
   929→        }
   930→
   931→        if (closure->function->jit_code) {
   932→            lisa_jit_fn jit_fn = (lisa_jit_fn)closure->function->jit_code;
   933→            result = jit_fn(vm, frame->closure, frame->slots);
   934→            /* If result is TAIL_PENDING, loop continues */
   935→        } else {
   936→            /* JIT compilation failed; use interpreter (no trampoline risk
   937→               since this function can't produce TAIL_PENDING) */
   938→            int target_depth = vm->frame_count - 1;
   939→            lisa_run(vm, target_depth);
   940→            return vm->stack_top[-1];
   941→        }
   942→    }
   943→    return result;
   944→}
Edit — lisa/vm.c
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/vm.c",
  "old_string": "    while (IS_TAIL_PENDING(result)) {\n        int argc = TAIL_PENDING_ARGC(result);\n        lisa_value callee = vm->stack_top[-1 - argc];",
  "new_string": "    while (IS_TAIL_PENDING(result)) {\n        if (vm->gc.bytes_allocated > vm->gc.next_gc) {\n            sync_gc_roots(vm);\n            lisa_gc_collect(&vm->gc);\n        }\n        int argc = TAIL_PENDING_ARGC(result);\n        lisa_value callee = vm->stack_top[-1 - argc];"
}

⚙ Edit ×2, Bash

Still 54MB — the GC check is in the wrong spot. At the top of the loop, the old frame slots (including the previous acc string) are still on the stack below stack_top, so they're all marked as roots. GC needs to run *after* memmove overwrites the old slots.

Edit — lisa/vm.c
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/vm.c",
  "old_string": "    while (IS_TAIL_PENDING(result)) {\n        if (vm->gc.bytes_allocated > vm->gc.next_gc) {\n            sync_gc_roots(vm);\n            lisa_gc_collect(&vm->gc);\n        }\n        int argc = TAIL_PENDING_ARGC(result);\n        lisa_value callee = vm->stack_top[-1 - argc];",
  "new_string": "    while (IS_TAIL_PENDING(result)) {\n        int argc = TAIL_PENDING_ARGC(result);\n        lisa_value callee = vm->stack_top[-1 - argc];"
}
result

  "new_string": "        frame->closure = closure;\n        frame->ip = closure->function->chunk.code;\n\n        if (vm->gc.bytes_allocated > vm->gc.next_gc) {\n            sync_gc_roots(vm);\n            lisa_gc_collect(&vm->gc);\n        }\n\n        /* JIT-compile the target if needed */"
}

⚙ Read

Still 54MB. The issue is subtler — let me check where the old acc actually lives at GC time.

Read — lisa/vm.c
{
  "file_path": "lisa/vm.c",
  "offset": 893,
  "limit": 60
}
result
893→static lisa_value jit_trampoline(lisa_vm *vm, lisa_value result) {
   894→    while (IS_TAIL_PENDING(result)) {
   895→        int argc = TAIL_PENDING_ARGC(result);
   896→        lisa_value callee = vm->stack_top[-1 - argc];
   897→
   898→        if (IS_OBJ(callee) && OBJ_TYPE(callee) == OBJ_NATIVE) {
   899→            lisa_call_value(vm, callee, argc);
   900→            return vm->stack_top[-1];
   901→        }
   902→
   903→        if (!IS_OBJ(callee) || OBJ_TYPE(callee) != OBJ_CLOSURE) {
   904→            runtime_error(vm, "Can only call functions and closures.");
   905→            return LISA_NIL;
   906→        }
   907→
   908→        lisa_obj_closure *closure = AS_CLOSURE(callee);
   909→        if (argc != closure->function->arity) {
   910→            runtime_error(vm, "Expected %d arguments but got %d.",
   911→                          closure->function->arity, argc);
   912→            return LISA_NIL;
   913→        }
   914→
   915→        /* Reuse the current top frame */
   916→        lisa_call_frame *frame = &vm->frames[vm->frame_count - 1];
   917→        close_upvalues(vm, frame->slots);
   918→
   919→        lisa_value *src = vm->stack_top - argc - 1;
   920→        memmove(frame->slots, src, (size_t)(argc + 1) * sizeof(lisa_value));
   921→        vm->stack_top = frame->slots + argc + 1;
   922→
   923→        frame->closure = closure;
   924→        frame->ip = closure->function->chunk.code;
   925→
   926→        if (vm->gc.bytes_allocated > vm->gc.next_gc) {
   927→            sync_gc_roots(vm);
   928→            lisa_gc_collect(&vm->gc);
   929→        }
   930→
   931→        /* JIT-compile the target if needed */
   932→        if (!closure->function->jit_code && vm->jit_enabled) {
   933→            lisa_jit_compile(vm, closure->function);
   934→        }
   935→
   936→        if (closure->function->jit_code) {
   937→            lisa_jit_fn jit_fn = (lisa_jit_fn)closure->function->jit_code;
   938→            result = jit_fn(vm, frame->closure, frame->slots);
   939→            /* If result is TAIL_PENDING, loop continues */
   940→        } else {
   941→            /* JIT compilation failed; use interpreter (no trampoline risk
   942→               since this function can't produce TAIL_PENDING) */
   943→            int target_depth = vm->frame_count - 1;
   944→            lisa_run(vm, target_depth);
   945→            return vm->stack_top[-1];
   946→        }
   947→    }
   948→    return result;
   949→}
   950→
   951→lisa_value lisa_jit_call_helper(lisa_vm *vm, int argc) {
   952→    lisa_value callee = vm->stack_top[-1 - argc];

⚙ Bash ×2, Read, Edit
Bash — Find GC call sites
{
  "command": "cat > /tmp/lisa_gc_debug.c << 'EOF'\n#include <stdio.h>\n// Quick patch to check if GC fires\nEOF\n# Add temporary debug print to verify GC triggers\ncd cj/lisa\ngrep -n 'lisa_gc_collect' vm.c object.c",
  "description": "Find GC call sites"
}

vm.c:928:            lisa_gc_collect(&vm->gc);
object.c:422:void lisa_gc_collect(lisa_gc *gc) {
Read — lisa/object.c
{
  "file_path": "lisa/object.c",
  "offset": 413,
  "limit": 10
}
result
413→    for (lisa_obj *obj = gc->objects; obj != NULL; obj = obj->next) {
   414→        if (obj->type == OBJ_FIBER) {
   415→            lisa_fiber *f = (lisa_fiber *)obj;
   416→            f->next_fiber = gc->all_fibers;
   417→            gc->all_fibers = f;
   418→        }
   419→    }

Edit — lisa/object.c
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/object.c",
  "old_string": "void lisa_gc_collect(lisa_gc *gc) {\n    mark_roots(gc);\n    sweep(gc);\n    rebuild_fiber_list(gc);\n    gc->next_gc = gc->bytes_allocated * 2;\n}",
  "new_string": "void lisa_gc_collect(lisa_gc *gc) {\n    size_t before = gc->bytes_allocated;\n    mark_roots(gc);\n    sweep(gc);\n    rebuild_fiber_list(gc);\n    gc->next_gc = gc->bytes_allocated * 2;\n    fprintf(stderr, \"[gc] %zu -> %zu bytes\\n\", before, gc->bytes_allocated);\n}"
}
result
The file lisa/object.c has been updated successfully.
Bash — Check if GC fires at all during string benchmark
{
  "command": "make dev 2>&1 | tail -1 && bin/lisa <benchmark> 2>&1 | head -20",
  "description": "Check if GC fires at all during string benchmark"
}
result
cc main.c value.c object.c lexer.c parser.c ast.c chunk.c compiler.c vm.c debug.c jit.c fiber.c ../src/ctx.c -o bin/lisa -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wno-gnu -Werror -g -O0 -I../src -lm
[gc] 50256467 -> 1493 bytes
10000
⚙ Read

GC fires once — at 50MB, way too late. It collects almost everything (down to 1493 bytes), proving the objects are indeed unreachable. The problem is next_gc starts at 1MB but bytes_allocated rockets past it inside the JIT function before the trampoline gets a chance to check.

The JIT function runs build's body — including the (str acc "x") call which allocates strings — all before returning TAIL_PENDING. The GC check only runs between trampoline iterations, but all the allocation happens *during* the JIT call.

The fix: check GC in lisa_jit_call_helper, which is where the JIT calls back into C for function calls like str.

Read — lisa/vm.c
{
  "file_path": "lisa/vm.c",
  "offset": 951,
  "limit": 30
}
result
951→lisa_value lisa_jit_call_helper(lisa_vm *vm, int argc) {
   952→    lisa_value callee = vm->stack_top[-1 - argc];
   953→    if (!lisa_call_value(vm, callee, argc)) {
   954→        return LISA_NIL; /* error already reported */
   955→    }
   956→    /* Check if callee was a native (call_value already handled it) */
   957→    if (IS_OBJ(callee) && OBJ_TYPE(callee) == OBJ_NATIVE) {
   958→        return vm->stack_top[-1]; /* result already on stack */
   959→    }
   960→    /* Closure call — dispatch to JIT or interpreter */
   961→    lisa_call_frame *frame = &vm->frames[vm->frame_count - 1];
   962→    lisa_value result;
   963→    if (frame->closure->function->jit_code) {
   964→        lisa_jit_fn jit_fn = (lisa_jit_fn)frame->closure->function->jit_code;
   965→        result = jit_fn(vm, frame->closure, frame->slots);
   966→        if (IS_TAIL_PENDING(result))
   967→            result = jit_trampoline(vm, result);
   968→    } else {
   969→        int target_depth = vm->frame_count - 1;
   970→        lisa_run(vm, target_depth);
   971→        result = vm->stack_top[-1];
   972→    }
   973→    /* Pop the callee's frame */
   974→    close_upvalues(vm, frame->slots);
   975→    vm->frame_count--;
   976→    vm->stack_top = frame->slots;
   977→    push(vm, result);
   978→    return result;
   979→}
   980→
⚙ Edit, Bash

The native call path returns at line 958 — that's where str returns. Add GC check there, after the native has pushed its result and the stack is consistent.

Edit — lisa/vm.c
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/vm.c",
  "old_string": "    /* Check if callee was a native (call_value already handled it) */\n    if (IS_OBJ(callee) && OBJ_TYPE(callee) == OBJ_NATIVE) {\n        return vm->stack_top[-1]; /* result already on stack */\n    }",
  "new_string": "    /* Check if callee was a native (call_value already handled it) */\n    if (IS_OBJ(callee) && OBJ_TYPE(callee) == OBJ_NATIVE) {\n        if (vm->gc.bytes_allocated > vm->gc.next_gc) {\n            sync_gc_roots(vm);\n            lisa_gc_collect(&vm->gc);\n        }\n        return vm->stack_top[-1]; /* result already on stack */\n    }"
}
result
The file lisa/vm.c has been updated successfully.

  "description": "Check GC frequency after fix"
}

[gc] 1048770 -> 4386 bytes
[gc] 10184 -> 4394 bytes
[gc] 10208 -> 4402 bytes

⚙ Edit, Bash

GC now fires frequently (~2858 times for 10K iterations) and collects almost everything each time. Let me remove the debug print and benchmark.

Edit — lisa/object.c
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/object.c",
  "old_string": "void lisa_gc_collect(lisa_gc *gc) {\n    size_t before = gc->bytes_allocated;\n    mark_roots(gc);\n    sweep(gc);\n    rebuild_fiber_list(gc);\n    gc->next_gc = gc->bytes_allocated * 2;\n    fprintf(stderr, \"[gc] %zu -> %zu bytes\\n\", before, gc->bytes_allocated);\n}",
  "new_string": "void lisa_gc_collect(lisa_gc *gc) {\n    mark_roots(gc);\n    sweep(gc);\n    rebuild_fiber_list(gc);\n    gc->next_gc = gc->bytes_allocated * 2;\n}"
}

Bash — Benchmark string test after GC fix
{
  "command": "make all 2>&1 | tail -1 && echo \"--- jit ---\" && /usr/bin/time -l bin/lisa <benchmark> 2>&1 1>/dev/null | grep -E 'real|maximum resident' && echo \"--- no-jit ---\" && /usr/bin/time -l bin/lisa --no-jit <benchmark> 2>&1 1>/dev/null | grep -E 'real|maximum resident'",
  "description": "Benchmark string test after GC fix"
}
result
cc main.c value.c object.c lexer.c parser.c ast.c chunk.c compiler.c vm.c debug.c jit.c fiber.c ../src/ctx.c -o bin/lisa -std=c11 -Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wno-gnu -O2 -I../src -lm
--- jit ---
        0.33 real         0.08 user         0.00 sys
             2899968  maximum resident set size
--- no-jit ---
        0.06 real         0.06 user         0.00 sys
             2899968  maximum resident set size
⚙ Bash ×2

Memory fixed: 2.8MB for both. JIT is slower here (0.08s vs 0.06s user time) because every str call goes through the JIT→C boundary, plus GC overhead. That's expected — the JIT can't speed up native-call-heavy code.

Let me verify everything still works and run the full comparison.

Bash — Smoke test all examples
{
  "command": "bin/lisa examples/json.lisa > /dev/null && bin/lisa examples/sieve.lisa > /dev/null && bin/lisa examples/ping-pong.lisa > /dev/null && bin/lisa examples/pipeline.lisa > /dev/null && bin/lisa examples/producer-consumer.lisa > /dev/null && bin/lisa examples/fan-out.lisa > /dev/null && echo \"all pass\"",
  "description": "Smoke test all examples"
}
result
all pass
Bash — Full benchmark comparison after fix
{
  "command": "printf \"%-22s  %10s %10s  %8s %8s  %6s\\n\" \"benchmark\" \"vm(ms)\" \"jit(ms)\" \"vm(KB)\" \"jit(KB)\" \"speed\"\nprintf \"%-22s  %10s %10s  %8s %8s  %6s\\n\" \"-------\" \"------\" \"-------\" \"------\" \"-------\" \"-----\"\nfor ex in examples/*.lisa <benchmark> <benchmark> <benchmark>; do\n  name=$(basename \"$ex\" .lisa)\n  best_vm_ms=999999; best_vm_mem=0\n  for run in 1 2 3; do\n    vm_out=$(/usr/bin/time -l bin/lisa --no-jit \"$ex\" 2>&1 1>/dev/null)\n    vm_real=$(echo \"$vm_out\" | grep 'real' | awk '{print $1}')\n    vm_mem=$(echo \"$vm_out\" | grep 'maximum resident' | awk '{print $1}')\n    vm_ms=$(echo \"$vm_real\" | awk '{printf \"%.1f\", $1*1000}')\n    if [ \"$(echo \"$vm_ms < $best_vm_ms\" | bc)\" -eq 1 ]; then best_vm_ms=$vm_ms; best_vm_mem=$vm_mem; fi\n  done\n  best_jit_ms=999999; best_jit_mem=0\n  for run in 1 2 3; do\n    jit_out=$(/usr/bin/time -l bin/lisa \"$ex\" 2>&1 1>/dev/null)\n    jit_real=$(echo \"$jit_out\" | grep 'real' | awk '{print $1}')\n    jit_mem=$(echo \"$jit_out\" | grep 'maximum resident' | awk '{print $1}')\n    jit_ms=$(echo \"$jit_real\" | awk '{printf \"%.1f\", $1*1000}')\n    if [ \"$(echo \"$jit_ms < $best_jit_ms\" | bc)\" -eq 1 ]; then best_jit_ms=$jit_ms; best_jit_mem=$jit_mem; fi\n  done\n  vm_kb=$((best_vm_mem / 1024)); jit_kb=$((best_jit_mem / 1024))\n  if [ \"$(echo \"$best_jit_ms > 0\" | bc)\" -eq 1 ]; then\n    speedup=$(echo \"$best_vm_ms $best_jit_ms\" | awk '{printf \"%.1fx\", $1/$2}')\n  else speedup=\"~inf\"; fi\n  printf \"%-22s  %10s %10s  %8s %8s  %6s\\n\" \"$name\" \"$best_vm_ms\" \"$best_jit_ms\" \"$vm_kb\" \"$jit_kb\" \"$speedup\"\ndone",
  "description": "Full benchmark comparison after fix",
  "timeout": 300000
}
result
benchmark                   vm(ms)    jit(ms)    vm(KB)  jit(KB)   speed
-------                     ------    -------    ------  -------   -----
fan-out                        0.0        0.0      1504     1616    ~inf
json                           0.0        0.0      1424     1936    ~inf
ping-pong                      0.0        0.0      1344     1424    ~inf
pipeline                       0.0        0.0      1392     1440    ~inf
producer-consumer              0.0        0.0      1344     1408    ~inf
sieve                          0.0        0.0      1728     1776    ~inf
lisa_bench_fib              1070.0      550.0      1328     1376    1.9x
lisa_bench_loop2             160.0       30.0      1344     1376    5.3x
lisa_bench_str                60.0       60.0      2640     2672    1.0x
claude

Fixed. Final results:

benchmark              vm(ms)   jit(ms)   vm(KB)  jit(KB)  speedup
─────────────────────  ──────   ───────   ──────  ───────  ───────
fan-out                  <1       <1       1504     1616      —
json                     <1       <1       1424     1936      —
ping-pong                <1       <1       1344     1424      —
pipeline                 <1       <1       1392     1440      —
producer-consumer        <1       <1       1344     1408      —
sieve                    <1       <1       1728     1776      —
fib(35)                1070      550       1328     1376    1.9x
tail-loop(5M)           160       30       1344     1376    5.3x
string-build(10K)        60       60       2640     2672    1.0x

The fix: GC check in lisa_jit_call_helper after native function calls return. The JIT's dispatch loop never re-enters the VM's instruction loop (where GC normally triggers), so allocating natives like str could run indefinitely without collection. Now memory is identical (2.6MB vs 2.7MB) and speed is 1.0x — no regression.

human

alright, is lisa done then? seems like a job well done!

claude

It's in a good spot. Solid feature set for a language experiment:

- bytecode compiler with tail calls

- stack VM + JIT (1.9-5.3x speedup on compute)

- closures with proper upvalue capture

- cooperative concurrency (fibers + channels)

- GC that actually works now (globals, JIT trampoline, call helper)

- no memory leaks

- 6 examples including a real JSON parser

- docs

If you wanted to keep going, the obvious next steps would be things like a REPL, cond (the git log mentions it), hash maps, or file I/O — but none of that is needed to call it a successful demonstration of cj. It does what it set out to do.

human

very good. proud moment: you wrote all of that code. i would like to write a blog post about our exploration together. can we format this conversations complete log from the claude logs in a way that is both explorable and informative for explorers and doesn’t give too much information about my filesystem etc.?

Bash — Check message types in log
{
  "command": "# Check what message types exist\ncat <claude-internal> | python3 -c \"\nimport json, sys\ntypes = set()\nfor line in sys.stdin:\n    obj = json.loads(line)\n    types.add(obj.get('type', 'unknown'))\nprint(sorted(types))\n\"",
  "description": "Check message types in log"
}
result
['assistant', 'file-history-snapshot', 'progress', 'queue-operation', 'system', 'user']
Bash — Examine user message structure
{
  "command": "# Check structure of user and assistant messages\ncat <claude-internal> | python3 -c \"\nimport json, sys\nfor line in sys.stdin:\n    obj = json.loads(line)\n    if obj.get('type') == 'user':\n        print('USER keys:', list(obj.keys()))\n        print('message type:', type(obj.get('message')))\n        msg = obj.get('message', {})\n        if isinstance(msg, dict):\n            print('message keys:', list(msg.keys()))\n            content = msg.get('content', '')\n            if isinstance(content, list):\n                print('content[0] keys:', list(content[0].keys()) if content else 'empty')\n            else:\n                print('content:', repr(content[:100]))\n        break\n\" 2>&1",
  "description": "Examine user message structure"
}

content[0] keys: ['type', 'text']
Bash — Examine assistant message structure
{
  "command": "# Check assistant message structure\ncat <claude-internal> | python3 -c \"\nimport json, sys\nfor line in sys.stdin:\n    obj = json.loads(line)\n    if obj.get('type') == 'assistant':\n        msg = obj.get('message', {})\n        content = msg.get('content', [])\n        # show first few content block types\n        for i, block in enumerate(content[:5]):\n            print(f'block {i}: type={block.get(\\\"type\\\")}, keys={list(block.keys())}')\n        break\n\"",
  "description": "Examine assistant message structure"
}

block 0: type=thinking, keys=['type', 'thinking', 'signature']
Bash — Check tool_use block structure
{
  "command": "# Get richer view - check a few assistant messages for tool_use blocks\ncat <claude-internal> | python3 -c \"\nimport json, sys\ncount = 0\nfor line in sys.stdin:\n    obj = json.loads(line)\n    if obj.get('type') == 'assistant':\n        msg = obj.get('message', {})\n        content = msg.get('content', [])\n        block_types = [b.get('type') for b in content]\n        print(f'assistant msg: {block_types}')\n        # Show tool_use example\n        for b in content:\n            if b.get('type') == 'tool_use':\n                print(f'  tool: {b.get(\\\"name\\\")}, input keys: {list(b.get(\\\"input\\\", {}).keys())[:3]}')\n                break\n            if b.get('type') == 'tool_result':\n                print(f'  result keys: {list(b.keys())}')\n                break\n        count += 1\n        if count >= 5: break\n\"",
  "description": "Check tool_use block structure"
}

Bash — Map out message flow
{
  "command": "# Let me look at the full flow of a few messages\ncat <claude-internal> | python3 -c \"\nimport json, sys\nfor i, line in enumerate(sys.stdin):\n    obj = json.loads(line)\n    t = obj.get('type')\n    if t in ('user', 'assistant'):\n        msg = obj.get('message', {})\n        content = msg.get('content', [])\n        if isinstance(content, list):\n            types = [b.get('type') for b in content]\n            preview = ''\n            for b in content:\n                if b.get('type') == 'text':\n                    preview = b['text'][:80]\n                elif b.get('type') == 'tool_use':\n                    preview = f'{b[\\\"name\\\"]}({list(b.get(\\\"input\\\",{}).keys())})'\n                elif b.get('type') == 'tool_result':\n                    preview = f'result for {b.get(\\\"tool_use_id\\\",\\\"?\\\")[:20]}'\n            print(f'line {i}: {t:10s} blocks={types}  preview={preview}')\n        elif isinstance(content, str):\n            print(f'line {i}: {t:10s} text={content[:80]}')\n    elif t == 'progress':\n        continue  # skip progress\n    elif t not in ('file-history-snapshot', 'queue-operation'):\n        print(f'line {i}: {t}')\n    if i > 80:\n        print('...')\n        break\n\" 2>&1 | head -40",
  "description": "Map out message flow"
}

⚙ Bash ×3, AskUserQuestion

Good, I understand the format. Let me also check which logs correspond to the Lisa work — there were multiple sessions.

Bash — Identify Lisa-related sessions
{
  "command": "# Check which sessions are Lisa-related by looking at first user message\nfor f in <claude-internal> do\n  first_msg=$(python3 -c \"\nimport json, sys\nfor line in open('$f'):\n    obj = json.loads(line)\n    if obj.get('type') == 'user':\n        msg = obj.get('message', {})\n        content = msg.get('content', '')\n        if isinstance(content, list):\n            for b in content:\n                if b.get('type') == 'text' and len(b['text']) > 5:\n                    print(b['text'][:120])\n                    sys.exit()\n        elif isinstance(content, str) and len(content) > 5:\n            print(content[:120])\n            sys.exit()\n\" 2>/dev/null)\n  ts=$(python3 -c \"\nimport json\nfor line in open('$f'):\n    obj = json.loads(line)\n    if 'timestamp' in obj:\n        print(obj['timestamp'][:19])\n        break\n\" 2>/dev/null)\n  echo \"$(basename $f)  $ts  $first_msg\"\ndone",
  "description": "Identify Lisa-related sessions"
}

But there are earlier sessions too — do you want the full Lisa story from the beginning, or just the def-as-local work?"="All Lisa sessions". You can now continue with the user's answers in mind.
Bash — Map all sessions chronologically
{
  "command": "# Get session details - timestamps, message counts, first substantive user message\nfor f in <claude-internal> do\n  info=$(python3 -c \"\nimport json, sys\nlines = open('$f').readlines()\nuser_msgs = []\nfirst_ts = None\nlast_ts = None\nfor line in lines:\n    obj = json.loads(line)\n    ts = obj.get('timestamp')\n    if ts:\n        if first_ts is None: first_ts = ts\n        last_ts = ts\n    if obj.get('type') == 'user':\n        msg = obj.get('message', {})\n        content = msg.get('content', '')\n        if isinstance(content, list):\n            for b in content:\n                if b.get('type') == 'text' and len(b['text'].strip()) > 10:\n                    user_msgs.append(b['text'][:200])\n        elif isinstance(content, str) and len(content.strip()) > 10:\n            user_msgs.append(content[:200])\n# Print first substantive user message\nfirst_real = next((m for m in user_msgs if not m.startswith('[Request')), user_msgs[0] if user_msgs else 'empty')\nprint(f'{first_ts[:16]}|{last_ts[:16]}|{len(user_msgs)} user msgs|{first_real[:150]}')\n\" 2>/dev/null)\n  echo \"$(basename $f .jsonl): $info\"\ndone | sort -t'|' -k1",
  "description": "Map all sessions chronologically"
}

Bash — Chronological session list
{
  "command": "# Get clean chronological session list with titles\npython3 << 'PYEOF'\nimport json, os, glob\n\nsessions = []\nfor f in sorted(glob.glob(\"<claude-internal>")):\n    lines = open(f).readlines()\n    first_ts = None\n    user_msgs = []\n    for line in lines:\n        obj = json.loads(line)\n        ts = obj.get('timestamp')\n        if ts and first_ts is None:\n            first_ts = ts\n        if obj.get('type') == 'user':\n            msg = obj.get('message', {})\n            content = msg.get('content', '')\n            if isinstance(content, list):\n                for b in content:\n                    if b.get('type') == 'text' and len(b['text'].strip()) > 10:\n                        user_msgs.append(b['text'].strip())\n            elif isinstance(content, str) and len(content.strip()) > 10:\n                user_msgs.append(content.strip())\n    \n    # Find first real message (not [Request interrupted])\n    first_real = ''\n    for m in user_msgs:\n        if not m.startswith('[Request'):\n            first_real = m[:200]\n            break\n    \n    sessions.append((first_ts or '', os.path.basename(f), len(user_msgs), first_real))\n\nsessions.sort()\nfor i, (ts, fname, count, msg) in enumerate(sessions):\n    # Extract title from plan if present\n    title = msg.split('\\n')[0] if msg else 'unknown'\n    for line in msg.split('\\n'):\n        l = line.strip()\n        if l.startswith('# ') and not l.startswith('## '):\n            title = l[2:]\n            break\n    print(f\"{i+1}. {ts[:16]}  ({count} msgs)  {title[:100]}\")\nPYEOF",
  "description": "Chronological session list"
}
result
1. 2026-02-28T16:45  (2 msgs)  We’re in the repository of a minimal JIT for C. Read it and learn about it deeply. Take your time.
2. 2026-02-28T16:58  (4 msgs)  Lisa: Bytecode VM for a Clojure-inspired Lisp
3. 2026-02-28T18:59  (6 msgs)  Tail Call Optimization for Lisa
4. 2026-02-28T22:09  (10 msgs)  Whole-Function JIT for Lisa using cj
5. 2026-03-01T09:04  (24 msgs)  Rewrite JIT: Register Cache + Inline Fast Paths
6. 2026-03-02T18:40  (9 msgs)  Implement Fibers and Channels for Lisa
7. 2026-03-03T08:29  (4 msgs)  Add string primitives + JSON parser/printer example
8. 2026-03-03T19:11  (13 msgs)  Make `def` inside functions create local variables
⚙ Write, Bash ×3

8 sessions telling the full story. Now let me write a converter script.

Write — lisa/docs/format_log.py
{
  "file_path": "lisa/docs/format_log.py",
  "content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3\n\"\"\"\nConvert Claude Code JSONL conversation logs into a readable HTML document.\n\nUsage: python3 format_log.py [-o output.html] logfile1.jsonl [logfile2.jsonl ...]\n\nStrips filesystem paths, hides thinking blocks, makes tool calls collapsible.\n\"\"\"\n\nimport json\nimport sys\nimport os\nimport re\nimport html\nimport argparse\nfrom pathlib import Path\n\n# paths to strip from output\nPATH_REPLACEMENTS = [\n    (re.compile(r'~/]+/Documents/Code/Github/lang/cj/lisa/'), 'lisa/'),\n    (re.compile(r'~/]+/Documents/Code/Github/lang/cj/'), 'cj/'),\n    (re.compile(r'~/]+/\\.claude/[^\\s\"\\']+'), '<claude-internal>'),\n    (re.compile(r'~/]+/'), '~/'),\n    (re.compile(r'/tmp/lisa_bench_\\w+\\.lisa'), '<benchmark>'),\n]\n\n# session titles (order matches chronological sort)\nSESSION_TITLES = [\n    \"exploring cj\",\n    \"building lisa: bytecode vm\",\n    \"tail call optimization\",\n    \"whole-function jit\",\n    \"jit rewrite: register cache + inline fast paths\",\n    \"fibers and channels\",\n    \"string primitives + json parser\",\n    \"def as local + bug fixes + docs + gc fixes\",\n]\n\n\ndef sanitize(text):\n    \"\"\"Strip filesystem paths.\"\"\"\n    for pattern, replacement in PATH_REPLACEMENTS:\n        text = pattern.sub(replacement, text)\n    return text\n\n\ndef escape(text):\n    \"\"\"HTML-escape and sanitize.\"\"\"\n    return sanitize(html.escape(text))\n\n\ndef format_code_block(text, lang=''):\n    \"\"\"Wrap text in a <pre><code> block.\"\"\"\n    return f'<pre><code class=\"{lang}\">{escape(text)}</code></pre>'\n\n\ndef render_content_block(block):\n    \"\"\"Render a single content block to HTML.\"\"\"\n    btype = block.get('type', '')\n\n    if btype == 'text':\n        text = sanitize(block.get('text', ''))\n        # basic markdown: ``` blocks, **bold**, `code`, headers\n        lines = text.split('\\n')\n        out = []\n        in_code = False\n  
... (truncated)

⚙ Bash ×3

The human message count (1420) is inflated by tool results being attributed to the user role (that's how Claude's API works — tool results come back as user messages). Let me check the actual human-typed messages look clean.

Bash — Sample user messages for readability
{
  "command": "# Sample first few user messages from session 2 to check readability\npython3 -c \"\nimport re\nhtml = open('docs/conversation.html').read()\n# find session 2 content\nstart = html.index('session 2.')\nend = html.index('session 3.')\nchunk = html[start:end]\n# extract user message text (just the p tags inside user messages)\nmsgs = re.findall(r'class=\\\"message user\\\">.*?<p>(.*?)</p>', chunk[:5000])\nfor m in msgs[:5]:\n    # strip tags\n    clean = re.sub(r'<[^>]+>', '', m)\n    print(repr(clean[:200]))\n    print()\n\" 2>/dev/null",
  "description": "Sample user messages for readability"
}

pre {
        background: #161b22;
        border: 1px solid #21262d;
        border-radius: 4px;

        margin: 0.4em 0;
        font-size: 0.9em;

    code {
        font-family: inherit;
        font-size: 0.95em;
    }
    p code, li code {
        background: #161b22;
        padding: 0.1em 0.3em;
        border-radius: 3px;
        border: 1px solid #21262d;
    }
    details { margin: 0.3em 0; }
    summary {
        cursor: pointer;
        padding: 0.2em 0.4em;
        border-radius: 3px;
        font-size: 0.85em;
    }
    summary:hover { background: #161b22; }
    .tool-call summary { color: #d2a8ff; }
    .tool-result summary { color: #8b949e; }
    .tool-name { font-weight: bold; }
    .tool-id { color: #484f58; font-size: 0.8em; }
    .result-label { font-style: italic; }
    .table-line {
        display: block;
        font-family: inherit;
        white-space: pre;

result
id="session-2"><h2><span class="session-number">session 2.</span> building lisa: bytecode vm</h2></div>
<div class="message user"><div class="message-label">human</div><p>Implement the following plan:

# Lisa: Bytecode VM for a Clojure-inspired Lisp

## Context

Build a Lisp called Lisa inside the cj JIT framework repo. Phase 1 is a bytecode interpreter in `lisa/` with its own Makefile. The bytecode design must be JIT-friendly so we can later lower ops to native code via cj. The language has Clojure-like syntax.

**Types**: int64, double, string, nil, bool, symbol
**Features**: def, fn, let, if, do, arithmetic/comparison, println, closures with upvalues, list ops (cons, car, cdr, list)
**Interface**: CLI with file eval (`lisa foo.lisa`) and string eval (`lisa -e &quot;(+ 1 2)&quot;`)

## Architecture

```
Source -&gt; Lexer (tokens) -&gt; Parser (AST) -&gt; Compiler (bytecode) -&gt; VM (interpreter)
```

**Stack-based VM** with NaN-boxed values (`uint64_t`) and Lua-style upvalue capture for closures. Each bytecode op does exactly one thing, making future JIT translation to cj instructions straightforward.

## File Layout

```
lisa/
  Makefile
  main.c              -- CLI entry point
  value.h / value.c   -- NaN-boxed value representation
  object.h / object.c -- heap objects (string, function, closure, upvalue, list) + mark-and-sweep GC
  lexer.h / lexer.c   -- tokenizer
  ast.h / ast.c       -- AST node types and construction
  parser.h / parser.c -- recursive descent parser
  chunk.h / chunk.c   -- bytecode chunk (instruction stream + constant pool)
  compiler.h / .c     -- AST -&gt; bytecode with scope resolution and upvalue capture
  vm.h / vm.c         -- bytecode dispatch loop, call frames, upvalue runtime
  debug.h / debug.c   -- bytecode disassembler
```

## Key Design Decisions

### NaN-boxing (`value.h`)

All values are `uint64_t`. Doubles are stored as-is. Non-double values use the quiet NaN payload space:

| Tag (bits 50:48) | Type | Payload |
|---|---|---|
| 000 | nil | none |
| 001 | bool | bit 0 |
| 010 | int | 48-bit sign-extended integer |
| 011 | object | 48-bit pointer |

Untagged (not a NaN) = double. This means values fit in machine registers for future JIT.

### Object system (`object.h`)

Common header (`lisa_obj`) with type tag, GC mark bit, and intrusive next pointer. Types:

- `lisa_obj_string` — interned, with FNV-1a hash, flexible array member for chars
- `lisa_obj_function` — compiled bytecode prototype (arity, upvalue_count, chunk, name)
- `lisa_obj_closure` — function + array of captured upvalue pointers

Bash — Verify session 8 coverage
{
  "command": "# Check if session 8 has the later parts (docs, GC fixes, benchmarks)\npython3 -c \"\nhtml = open('docs/conversation.html').read()\ns8 = html.index('id=\\\"session-8\\\"')\nchunk = html[s8:]\n# Look for key phrases from this session\nfor phrase in ['docs dir', 'GC doesn', 'lisa_take_string', 'benchmark', 'trampoline', 'jit_call_helper']:\n    idx = chunk.find(phrase)\n    print(f'{phrase}: {\\\"found\\\" if idx > 0 else \\\"NOT FOUND\\\"} ')\n\"",
  "description": "Verify session 8 coverage"
}

Note: session 8 in the HTML only has the *previous* conversation's log (d941dd80). This current session's log won't be in that file yet since we're still in it. You'll want to regenerate after this session ends to capture the full story.

- 2.6MB, 8 sessions, dark theme, monospace

- tool calls and results are collapsible <details> blocks — click to expand

python3 docs/format_log.py -o docs/conversation.html ~/.claude/projects/<project>/*.jsonl

The approach: group consecutive tool_use/tool_result blocks into a single collapsible "work" section, so the reader sees only the human/claude conversation by default.

   156→
   157→def process_session(filepath, session_idx):
   158→    """Process one JSONL file into a list of message HTML blocks."""
   159→    messages = []
   160→
   161→    with open(filepath) as f:
   162→        for line in f:
   163→            obj = json.loads(line)
   164→            msg_type = obj.get('type')
   165→
   166→            if msg_type == 'user':
   167→                msg = obj.get('message', {})
   168→                content = msg.get('content', '')
   169→                blocks = []
   170→
   171→                if isinstance(content, list):
   172→                    for block in content:
   173→                        if block.get('type') == 'text':
   174→                            text = block['text'].strip()
   175→                            # skip [Request interrupted] noise
   176→                            if text.startswith('[Request interrupted'):
   177→                                continue
   178→                            # skip system reminders
   179→                            text = re.sub(r'', '', text, flags=re.DOTALL).strip()
   180→                            if text:
   181→                                blocks.append(f'<p>{escape(text)}</p>')
   182→                        elif block.get('type') == 'tool_result':
   183→                            rendered = render_content_block(block)
   184→                            if rendered:
   185→                                blocks.append(rendered)
   186→                elif isinstance(content, str):
   187→                    text = re.sub(r'', '', content, flags=re.DOTALL).strip()
   188→                    if text:
   189→                        blocks.append(f'<p>{escape(text)}</p>')
   190→
   191→                if blocks:
   192→                    messages.append(('user', '\n'.join(blocks)))
   193→
   194→            elif msg_type == 'assistant':
   195→                msg = obj.get('message', {})
   196→                content = msg.get('content', [])
   197→                blocks = []
   198→
   199→                if isinstance(content, list):
   200→                    for block in content:
   201→                        rendered = render_content_block(block)
   202→                        if rendered:
   203→                            blocks.append(rendered)
   204→                elif isinstance(content, str):
   205→                    text = sanitize(content).strip()
   206→                    if text:

⚙ Read

Now I need to rethink the rendering. Instead of emitting tool_use/tool_result as individual <details> blocks mixed into assistant/user messages, I'll:

1. Separate each message into "text" content vs "tool" content

2. For assistant messages with tools: show text normally, group all tool blocks into one collapsible "work" section

3. For user messages with only tool_results (no human text): merge them into the preceding assistant's work section

Read — lisa/docs/format_log.py
{
  "file_path": "lisa/docs/format_log.py"
}
result
1→#!/usr/bin/env python3
     2→"""
     3→Convert Claude Code JSONL conversation logs into a readable HTML document.
     4→
     5→Usage: python3 format_log.py [-o output.html] logfile1.jsonl [logfile2.jsonl ...]
     6→
     7→Strips filesystem paths, hides thinking blocks, makes tool calls collapsible.
     8→"""
     9→
    10→import json
    11→import sys
    12→import os
    13→import re
    14→import html
    15→import argparse
    16→from pathlib import Path
    17→
    18→# paths to strip from output
    19→PATH_REPLACEMENTS = [
    20→    (re.compile(r'~/]+/Documents/Code/Github/lang/cj/lisa/'), 'lisa/'),
    21→    (re.compile(r'~/]+/Documents/Code/Github/lang/cj/'), 'cj/'),
    22→    (re.compile(r'~/]+/\.claude/[^\s"\']+'), '<claude-internal>'),
    23→    (re.compile(r'~/]+/'), '~/'),
    24→    (re.compile(r'/tmp/lisa_bench_\w+\.lisa'), '<benchmark>'),
    25→]
    26→
    27→# session titles (order matches chronological sort)
    28→SESSION_TITLES = [
    29→    "exploring cj",
    30→    "building lisa: bytecode vm",
    31→    "tail call optimization",
    32→    "whole-function jit",
    33→    "jit rewrite: register cache + inline fast paths",
    34→    "fibers and channels",
    35→    "string primitives + json parser",
    36→    "def as local + bug fixes + docs + gc fixes",
    37→]
    38→
    39→
    40→def sanitize(text):
    41→    """Strip filesystem paths."""
    42→    for pattern, replacement in PATH_REPLACEMENTS:
    43→        text = pattern.sub(replacement, text)
    44→    return text
    45→
    46→
    47→def escape(text):
    48→    """HTML-escape and sanitize."""
    49→    return sanitize(html.escape(text))
    50→
    51→
    52→def format_code_block(text, lang=''):
    53→    """Wrap text in a <pre><code> block."""
    54→    return f'<pre><code class="{lang}">{escape(text)}</code></pre>'
    55→
    56→
    57→def render_content_block(block):
    58→    """Render a single content block to HTML."""
    59→    btype = block.get('type', '')
    60→
    61→    if btype == 'text':
    62→        text = sanitize(block.get('text', ''))
    63→        # basic markdown: ``` blocks, **bold**, `code`, headers
    64→        lines = text.split('\n')
    65→        out = []
    66→        in_code = False
    67→        code_buf = []
    68→        code_lang = ''
    69→        for line in lines:
    70→            if line.startswith('```') and not in_code:
    71→                in_code = True
    72→                code_lang = line[3:].strip()
    73→                code_buf = []
    74→            elif line.startswith('```') and in_code:
    75→                in_code = False
    76→                out.append(format_code_block('\n'.join(code_buf), code_lang))
    77→            elif in_code:
    78→                code_buf.append(line)
    79→            else:
    80→                escaped = html.escape(line)
    81→                # inline code
    82→                escaped = re.sub(r'`([^`]+)`', r'<code>\1</c

I'll rewrite process_session to collect raw typed blocks, then add a post-processing step that groups consecutive tool blocks into collapsible work sections — separate from the conversation text.

Write — lisa/docs/format_log.py
{
  "file_path": "lisa/docs/format_log.py",
  "content": "#!/usr/bin/env python3\n\"\"\"\nConvert Claude Code JSONL conversation logs into a readable HTML document.\n\nUsage: python3 format_log.py [-o output.html] logfile1.jsonl [logfile2.jsonl ...]\n\nStrips filesystem paths, hides thinking blocks. result
0
no path leaks
<details class="work-group"><summary><span class="work-icon">&#9881;</span> Bash, Glob&nbsp;&times;2, Read&nbsp;&times;3</summary><div class="work-group-inner"><details class="tool-call"><summary><span class="tool-name">Bash</span> &mdash; List top-level directory contents</summary><pre><code>{
  &quot;command&quot;: &quot;ls -la ~/Documents/Code/Github/lang/cj&quot;,
  &quot;description&quot;: &quot;List top-level directory contents&quot;
}</code></pre></details>
claude

<details> is collapsed by default in HTML — no open attribute means closed. The structure is:

- conversation messages (665) — always visible

- work groups (558) — collapsed, showing a summary like "Read x3, Edit x2, Bash" with a gear icon; click to expand and see each tool call/result inside

You can regenerate after this session with:

python3 docs/format_log.py -o docs/conversation.html ~/.claude/projects/<project>/*.jsonl
human

much better. also collapse the intermediate thinking steps by default please.

⚙ Read ×2

The idea: if an assistant text message is immediately followed by a work group, fold it into the work group as its description. That way only the substantive conversation stays visible.

Read — lisa/docs/format_log.py
{
  "file_path": "lisa/docs/format_log.py",
  "offset": 195,
  "limit": 40
}
result
195→    # First pass: collect raw blocks with roles
   196→    raw = []  # list of (role, block_type, block_or_text)
   197→
   198→    with open(filepath) as f:
   199→        for line in f:
   200→            obj = json.loads(line)
   201→            msg_type = obj.get('type')
   202→
   203→            if msg_type == 'user':
   204→                msg = obj.get('message', {})
   205→                content = msg.get('content', '')
   206→                if isinstance(content, list):
   207→                    for block in content:
   208→                        if block.get('type') == 'text':
   209→                            text = block['text'].strip()
   210→                            if text.startswith('[Request interrupted'):
   211→                                continue
   212→                            text = re.sub(r'', '', text, flags=re.DOTALL).strip()
   213→                            if text:
   214→                                raw.append(('user', 'text', text))
   215→                        elif block.get('type') == 'tool_result':
   216→                            raw.append(('user', 'tool_result', block))
   217→                elif isinstance(content, str):
   218→                    text = re.sub(r'', '', content, flags=re.DOTALL).strip()
   219→                    if text:
   220→                        raw.append(('user', 'text', text))
   221→
   222→            elif msg_type == 'assistant':
   223→                msg = obj.get('message', {})
   224→                content = msg.get('content', [])
   225→                if isinstance(content, list):
   226→                    for block in content:
   227→                        btype = block.get('type', '')
   228→                        if btype == 'text':
   229→                            text = block.get('text', '').strip()
   230→                            if text:
   231→                                raw.append(('assistant', 'text', text))
   232→                        elif btype == 'tool_use':
   233→                            raw.append(('assistant', 'tool_use', block))
   234→                        elif btype == 'thinking':
Read — lisa/docs/format_log.py
{
  "file_path": "lisa/docs/format_log.py",
  "offset": 235,
  "limit": 50
}
result
235→                            pass  # skip
   236→                elif isinstance(content, str):
   237→                    text = content.strip()
   238→                    if text:
   239→                        raw.append(('assistant', 'text', text))
   240→
   241→    # Second pass: group into conversation messages and work blocks
   242→    messages = []
   243→    i = 0
   244→    while i < len(raw):
   245→        role, btype, data = raw[i]
   246→
   247→        if btype == 'text':
   248→            messages.append((f'text-{role}', render_markdown(data)))
   249→            i += 1
   250→        elif btype in ('tool_use', 'tool_result'):
   251→            # collect consecutive tool blocks (across assistant/user boundaries)
   252→            tool_blocks = []
   253→            while i < len(raw) and raw[i][1] in ('tool_use', 'tool_result'):
   254→                tool_blocks.append((raw[i][1], raw[i][2]))
   255→                i += 1
   256→            # render them as a single collapsible work section
   257→            inner_parts = []
   258→            for kind, block in tool_blocks:
   259→                if kind == 'tool_use':
   260→                    inner_parts.append(render_tool_use(block))
   261→                elif kind == 'tool_result':
   262→                    rendered = render_tool_result(block)
   263→                    if rendered:
   264→                        inner_parts.append(rendered)
   265→            if inner_parts:
   266→                summary = summarize_tool_group(tool_blocks)
   267→                messages.append(('work', summary, '\n'.join(inner_parts)))
   268→        else:
   269→            i += 1
   270→
   271→    return messages
   272→
   273→
   274→def generate_html(sessions):
   275→    """Generate the full HTML document."""
   276→    css = """
   277→    * { box-sizing: border-box; margin: 0; padding: 0; }
   278→    body {
   279→        font-family: 'Berkeley Mono', 'IBM Plex Mono', 'JetBrains Mono', monospace;
   280→        font-size: 14px;
   281→        line-height: 1.6;
   282→        background: #0d1117;
   283→        color: #c9d1d9;
   284→        max-width: 900px;
⚙ Edit

I'll add a post-processing step that merges assistant text into the following work group when it's just narration.

{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/docs/format_log.py",
  "old_string": "    # Second pass: group into conversation messages and work blocks\n    messages = []\n    i = 0\n    while i < len(raw):\n        role, btype, data = raw[i]\n\n        if btype == 'text':\n            messages.append((f'text-{role}', render_markdown(data)))\n            i += 1\n        elif btype in ('tool_use', 'tool_result'):\n            # collect consecutive tool blocks (across assistant/user boundaries)\n            tool_blocks = []\n            while i < len(raw) and raw[i][1] in ('tool_use', 'tool_result'):\n                tool_blocks.append((raw[i][1], raw[i][2]))\n                i += 1\n            # render them as a single collapsible work section\n            inner_parts = []\n            for kind, block in tool_blocks:\n                if kind == 'tool_use':\n                    inner_parts.append(render_tool_use(block))\n                elif kind == 'tool_result':\n                    rendered = render_tool_result(block)\n                    if rendered:\n                        inner_parts.append(rendered)\n            if inner_parts:\n                summary = summarize_tool_group(tool_blocks)\n                messages.append(('work', summary, '\\n'.join(inner_parts)))\n        else:\n            i += 1\n\n    return messages",

{
  "replace_all": false,

  "old_string": "            elif msg[0] == 'work':\n                summary_text = msg[1]\n                inner_html = msg[2]\n                parts.append(\n                    f'<details class=\"work-group\">'\n                    f'<summary><span class=\"work-icon\">&#9881;</span> {summary_text}</summary>'\n                    f'<div class=\"work-group-inner\">{inner_html}</div>'\n                    f'</details>'\n                )",
  "new_string": "            elif msg[0] == 'work':\n                summary_text = msg[1]\n                inner_html = msg[2]\n                desc_html = msg[3] if len(msg) > 3 and msg[3] else ''\n                desc_block = f'<div class=\"work-desc\">{desc_html}</div>' if desc_html else ''\n                parts.append(\n                    f'<details class=\"work-group\">'\n                    f'<summary><span class=\"work-icon\">&#9881;</span> {summary_text}</summary>'\n                    f'<div class=\"work-group-inner\">{desc_block}{inner_html}</div>'\n                    f'</details>'\n                )"
}

Bash — Regenerate with narration folded into work groups
{
  "command": "python3 docs/format_log.py -o docs/conversation.html <claude-internal>",
  "description": "Regenerate with narration folded into work groups"
}
result
wrote docs/conversation.html (8 sessions, 129 conversation messages, 562 work groups, 2561KB)
claude

665 conversation messages down to 129 — the 536 intermediate narration messages got folded into their adjacent work groups. The visible conversation is now just the substantive back-and-forth: plans, questions, explanations, results.

Regenerate after this session:

python3 docs/format_log.py -o docs/conversation.html ~/.claude/projects/<project>/*.jsonl
human

did you ensure there’s no sensitive information in there?

⚙ Edit, Bash, Grep ×2

Need to add sanitization for the username, session UUIDs, and messageIds. Let me fix the sanitizer.

Edit — lisa/docs/format_log.py
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/docs/format_log.py",
  "old_string": "# paths to strip from output\nPATH_REPLACEMENTS = [\n    (re.compile(r'~/]+/Documents/Code/Github/lang/cj/lisa/'), 'lisa/'),\n    (re.compile(r'~/]+/Documents/Code/Github/lang/cj/'), 'cj/'),\n    (re.compile(r'~/]+/\\.claude/[^\\s\"\\']+'), '<claude-internal>'),\n    (re.compile(r'~/]+/'), '~/'),\n    (re.compile(r'/tmp/lisa_bench_\\w+\\.lisa'), '<benchmark>'),\n]",
  "new_string": "# paths and identifiers to strip from output\nPATH_REPLACEMENTS = [\n    (re.compile(r'~/]+/Documents/Code/Github/lang/cj/lisa/'), 'lisa/'),\n    (re.compile(r'~/]+/Documents/Code/Github/lang/cj/'), 'cj/'),\n    (re.compile(r'~/]+/\\.claude/[^\\s\"\\']+'), '<claude-internal>'),\n    (re.compile(r'~/]+/'), '~/'),\n    (re.compile(r'~/]+/'), '~/'),\n    (re.compile(r'/tmp/lisa_bench_\\w+\\.lisa'), '<benchmark>'),\n    # strip session UUIDs (8-4-4-4-12 hex)\n    (re.compile(r'[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{12}'), '<uuid>'),\n    # strip username from ls -la output (e.g. \"<user>  staff\")\n    (re.compile(r'(?<=\\d )\\S+(?=\\s+staff\\s)'), 'user'),\n]"
}

{

  "old_string": "    (re.compile(r'~/]+/'), '~/'),\n    (re.compile(r'/tmp/lisa_bench_\\w+\\.lisa'), '<benchmark>'),\n    # strip session UUIDs (8-4-4-4-12 hex)\n    (re.compile(r'[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{12}'), '<uuid>'),\n    # strip username from ls -la output (e.g. \"<user>  staff\")\n    (re.compile(r'(?<=\\d )\\S+(?=\\s+staff\\s)'), 'user'),\n]",
  "new_string": "    (re.compile(r'~/]+/'), '~/'),\n    (re.compile(r'/tmp/lisa_bench_\\w+\\.lisa'), '<benchmark>'),\n    # temp task output paths\n    (re.compile(r'/private/tmp/claude-\\d+/[^\\s]+'), '<task-output>'),\n    # claude project slugs containing username-derived paths\n    (re.compile(r'-Users-[A-Za-z0-9_]+-Documents-[^\\s\"\\'*/]+'), '<project>'),\n    # session UUIDs (8-4-4-4-12 hex)\n    (re.compile(r'[0-9a-f]{8}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{4}-[0-9a-f]{12}'), '<uuid>'),\n    # username from ls -la output (e.g. \"<user>  staff\")\n    (re.compile(r'(?<=\\d )\\S+(?=\\s+staff\\s)'), 'user'),\n]\n\n# additional words to scrub (usernames, hostnames, etc.)\nSCRUB_WORDS = []  # populate with specific words if needed"
}

⚙ Edit, Bash, Grep ×4

Now wire SCRUB_WORDS into sanitize.

Edit — lisa/docs/format_log.py
{
  "replace_all": false,
  "file_path": "lisa/docs/format_log.py",
  "old_string": "def sanitize(text):\n    \"\"\"Strip filesystem paths.\"\"\"\n    for pattern, replacement in PATH_REPLACEMENTS:\n        text = pattern.sub(replacement, text)\n    return text",
  "new_string": "def sanitize(text):\n    \"\"\"Strip filesystem paths and sensitive words.\"\"\"\n    for pattern, replacement in PATH_REPLACEMENTS:\n        text = pattern.sub(replacement, text)\n    for word in SCRUB_WORDS:\n        text = text.replace(word, '<user>')\n    return text"
}
